**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AdamF — 2018-03-05T07:11:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/world-map-5g.20989/post-236312>

Is there any kind of centralized type of world map available to see which countries are scheduled to get 5G when?

### Lahelada — 2018-03-05T21:57:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/world-map-5g.20989/post-236361>

I have not seen one. I am keeping an eye out in the news.

### ObstreperousPolarBear — 2018-04-02T05:51:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/world-map-5g.20989/post-238501>

We should make one...perhaps some techy Krusers can whip something up? Should be simple to put together, since things like google maps are free for developers.

### SlamSlask — 2018-04-02T15:27:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/world-map-5g.20989/post-238507>

I got a little experience with web page programming and opemstreetmaps
Maybe @[drezy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19879/) and I can team up for this ?

### drezy — 2018-04-02T17:39:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/world-map-5g.20989/post-238518>

There is a page [http://www.antennasearch.com](http://www.antennasearch.com). It covers the US but many claim that it has fallen behind on fresh data.

I think that keeping on top of real data would be the biggest challenge across countries, states, counties....

I'd hate to be part of the cause of people acting out [moral hazard](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moral_hazard) especially when the data could be inadvertently false. I can show you a map where it looks all clear because someone filed their tower application incorrectly, the data didn't make it into the database for any reason, or any number of reasons the model is incorrect.

I can only believe and would only communicate what is measured in this regard. A Waze-like model could be better once consumer rollout hits.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-04-02T19:35:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/world-map-5g.20989/post-238536>

Drezy,

I have used antennasearch.com and found it useful, but data is incomplete and it takes some guesswork to figure out which installations are cell base stations and which are miscellaneous little radio antennas, etc.

5G rollout is a paradigm shift in wireless networks because it relies on mini cell tranceivers (Distributed Antenna Systems, or DAS) more than traditional cell towers we are used to seeing. As proposed, 5G mini cells would proliferate in huge numbers in population centers, on buildings, utility poles, etc. They would appear like innocuous little grey boxes on poles and buildings.

Extremely large numbers of mini cells are required by the nature of the technology, particularly because 5G frequency spectrum does not pass through objects like building walls as effectively as current wireless frequencies (e.g. 2.4 GHz used in WiFi and cell transmissions).

Can you imagine trying to map all the 5G antennas after full rollout? A medium scale map would be like a dense scatter plot.

### drezy — 2018-04-02T20:06:51+0300

As proposed, 5G mini cells would proliferate in huge numbers in population centers, on buildings, utility poles, etc. They would appear like innocuous little grey boxes on poles and buildings.
>
> Extremely large numbers of mini cells are required by the nature of the technology, particularly because 5G frequency spectrum does not pass through objects like building walls as effectively as current wireless frequencies (e.g. 2.4 GHz used in WiFi and cell transmissions).

> Can you imagine trying to map all the 5G antennas after full rollout? A medium scale map would be like a dense scatter plot.

It's worse. Inevitably the data/representation would fail to account for some information.

Even with disclaimers and a nice easy to read heatmap some fool would put their baby in harms way. You and I can claim how foolish it is not to also test based on disclaimers galore, but I think we would also be complicit at least in some small part. That would inevitably effect my quantum mojo. If that wasn't a thing, it is now.

Our generation hatched Big Data so that every naive can look smart and find correlations between Honduran fruit prices and Uzbeki cattle mortality. It's almost heresy to even suggest that more data can possibly be a bad thing. Still I do it in the same way that I ask racers for cures if they think maybe they didn't run fast enough and "awareness" raisers if they can project exactly how many % points we'll need or ribbon size increase before the disease up and packs it's bags.

Ultimately, what I am looking for is a decision making tool.
A dead zone in Denver today is where they will build a cell tower tomorrow.
If we are considering where to settle long-term, the key is 1) population growth, 2) current population levels, and 3) current radiation measurements. I care not if the radiation in Denver is 1 V/M or 0.5 V/m - or if it is only 0.05 V/m where I am sitting right now. It's just too damn high.
What I think would be helpful is mapping this out just so we get a general sense of what ball-park ranges are in different places. That would help me guide people in relocation decisions.
Never thought I'd make therapeutic recommendations to up sticks and move.
\*sigh\*

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-04-03T18:34:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/world-map-5g.20989/post-238601>

Leland,

Perhaps this topic is suitable for another EMF forum thread, but you raise a good issue-- where realistically could you move to avoid our increasingly microwaved environment? The short answer is nowhere (well, maybe Antartica)-- it is pervasive and becoming ever more so (just wait till they figure out how to replace/augment terrestrial antennas with swarms of small, cheap, special-purpose low-orbit satellites beaming RF down on every spot on earth).

In the meantime, it may be a futile gesture but like-minded Krusers might try banding together in certain localities to set policies that limit or ban wireless invasions. However, this probably would be illegal because of the 1997 telecommunications law that prohibited local jurisdictions from restricting cell tower construction/use for health or environmental reasons (that was the wireless industry's ace in the hole).

# Thread: CT 4 confimed? "Cold Origin of Life?" (2017)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-confimed-cold-origin-of-life-2017.20990/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-05T16:19:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-confimed-cold-origin-of-life-2017.20990/post-236334>

**Abstract**
Psychrophiles thrive permanently in the various cold environments on Earth. Their unsuspected ability to remain metabolically active in the most extreme low temperature conditions provides insights into a possible cold step in the origin of life. More specifically, metabolically active psychrophilic bacteria have been observed at −20 °C in the ice eutectic phase (i.e., the liquid veins between sea ice crystals). In the context of the RNA world hypothesis, this ice eutectic phase would have provided stability to the RNA molecules and confinement of the molecules in order to react and replicate. This aspect has been convincingly tested by laboratory experiments.

See: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5492147/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5492147/)

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-05T16:22:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-confimed-cold-origin-of-life-2017.20990/post-236335>

Second one:

**The Possible Emergence of Life and Differentiation of a Shallow Biosphere on Irradiated Icy Worlds: The Example of Europa**

Irradiated ice-covered ocean worlds with rocky mafic mantles may provide the conditions needed to drive the emergence and maintenance of life. Alkaline hydrothermal springs—relieving the geophysical, thermal, and chemical disequilibria between oceans and tidally stressed crusts—could generate inorganic barriers to the otherwise uncontrolled and kinetically disfavored oxidation of hydrothermal hydrogen and methane. Ionic gradients imposed across these inorganic barriers, comprising iron oxyhydroxides and sulfides, could drive the hydrogenation of carbon dioxide and the oxidation of methane through thermodynamically favorable metabolic pathways leading to early life-forms. In such chemostatic environments, fuels may eventually outweigh oxidants. Ice-covered oceans are primarily heated from below, creating convection that could transport putative microbial cells and cellular cooperatives upward to congregate beneath an ice shell, potentially giving rise to a highly focused shallow biosphere. It is here where electron acceptors, ultimately derived from the irradiated surface, could be delivered to such life-forms through exchange with the icy surface. Such zones would act as “electron disposal units” for the biosphere, and occupants might be transferred toward the surface by buoyant diapirs and even entrained into plumes. Key Words: Biofilms—Europa—Extraterrestrial life—Hydrothermal systems. Astrobiology 17, 1265–1273.

See: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5729856/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5729856/)

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-07T14:13:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-confimed-cold-origin-of-life-2017.20990/post-236543>

**How might CT be possible without increasing stress?
**
See: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3725077/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3725077/)

Quote:

"Classic brown fat and inducible beige fat both dissipate chemical energy in the form of heat through the actions of mitochondrial uncoupling protein 1. This nonshivering thermogenesis is crucial for mammals as a defense against cold and obesity/diabetes. Cold is known to act indirectly through the sympathetic nervous systems and β-adrenergic signaling, but here we report that cool temperature (27–33 °C) can directly activate a thermogenic gene program in adipocytes in a cell-autonomous manner. White and beige fat cells respond to cool temperatures, but classic brown fat cells do not. Importantly, this activation in isolated cells is independent of the canonical cAMP/Protein Kinase A/cAMP response element-binding protein pathway downstream of the β-adrenergic receptors. These findings provide an unusual insight into the role of adipose tissues in thermoregulation, as well as an alternative way to target nonshivering thermogenesis for treatment of obesity and metabolic diseases."

Deep CT vs Regular CT...

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-07T14:36:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-confimed-cold-origin-of-life-2017.20990/post-236545>

Another one: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19737029](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19737029)

**I've underlined the important pieces:

ABSTRACT**

**OBJECTIVE:**
Considering the adverse effects of exercise-induced cortisol secretion on health in athletes, it is important to determine the environmental and individual factors that contribute to the variations in exercise-induced cortisol secretion. In this study, the effects of cold environment exposure and cold acclimatization on exercise-induced salivary cortisol responses were investigated.

**METHODS:**
**Short track skaters (n = 11), who usually practice under cold conditions, and inline skaters (n = 11), who usually practice under room temperature conditions, participated in a randomized crossover study. All participants cycled for 60 minutes at 65% Vo2 max under cold (ambient temperature: 5 +/- 1 degrees C, relative humidity 41% +/- 9%) and room temperature (ambient temperature: 21 +/- 1 degrees C, relative humidity 35% +/- 5%) conditions.** The participants had a 120-minute bed rest recovery phase at room temperature after both exercise bouts. Cortisol levels were measured in saliva samples collected pre-exercise and postexercise at 1 minute, 30 minutes, 60 minutes, and 120 minutes.

**RESULTS:**
Both short track and inline skaters exhibited clear cortisol responses to exercise under cold and room temperature conditions. The magnitude of the cortisol response, however, was different between skaters and conditions. The inline skaters exhibited significantly higher cortisol values under cold conditions than under room temperature conditions (7.6 nmol/L and 4.2 nmol/L, respectively). However, the short track skaters exhibited significantly higher cortisol values under cold conditions compared to room temperature conditions (8.7 nmol/L and 5.4 nmol/L, respectively).

**CONCLUSIONS:**
**The effects of cold environment exposure on exercise-induced cortisol response were different between skaters who usually practice under cold or room temperature conditions. These results can be interpreted as acclimatization to either cold or room temperature conditions attenuating the cortisol response, suggesting that acclimatization may be beneficial in reducing the exercise-induced cortisol response.
**
\-----------------------

**This probably means that the human body will has less and less on cortisol response when becoming cold adapted.**

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-08T09:11:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-confimed-cold-origin-of-life-2017.20990/post-236659>

**Even more shocking (2017):**

(highlights are again added by me).

**Four-week cold acclimation in adult humans shifts uncoupling thermogenesis from skeletal muscles to brown adipose tissue.**
Muscle-derived thermogenesis during acute cold exposure in humans consists of a combination of cold-induced increases in skeletal muscle proton leak and shivering. **Daily cold exposure results in an increase in brown adipose tissue oxidative capacity coupled with a decrease in the cold-induced skeletal muscle proton leak and shivering intensity.** Improved coupling between electromyography-determined muscle activity and whole-body heat production following cold acclimation suggests a maintenance of ATPase-dependent thermogenesis and decrease in skeletal muscle ATPase independent thermogenesis. Although daily cold exposure did not change the fibre composition of the vastus lateralis, the fibre composition was a strong predictor of the shivering pattern evoked during acute cold exposure.

**ABSTRACT:**
We previously showed that 4 weeks of daily cold exposure in humans can increase brown adipose tissue (BAT) volume by 45% and oxidative metabolism by 182%. Surprisingly, we did not find a reciprocal reduction in shivering intensity when exposed to a mild cold (18°C). The present study aimed to determine whether changes in skeletal muscle oxidative metabolism or shivering activity could account for these unexpected findings. Nine men participated in a 4 week cold acclimation intervention (10°C water circulating in liquid-conditioned suit, 2 h day-1 , 5 days week-1 ). Shivering intensity and pattern were measured continuously during controlled cold exposure (150 min at 4 °C) before and after the acclimation. Muscle biopsies from the m. vastus lateralis were obtained to measure oxygen consumption rate and proton leak of permeabilized muscle fibres. **Cold acclimation elicited a modest 21% (P < 0.05) decrease in whole-body and m. vastus lateralis shivering intensity. Furthermore, cold acclimation abolished the acute cold-induced increase in proton leak.** Although daily cold exposure did not change the fibre composition of the m. vastus lateralis, fibre composition was a strong predictor of the shivering pattern evoked during acute cold. **We conclude that muscle-derived thermogenesis during acute cold exposure in humans is not only limited to shivering, but also includes cold-induced increases in proton leak**. The efficiency of muscle oxidative phosphorylation improves with cold acclimation, suggesting that reduced muscle thermogenesis occurs through decreased proton leak, in addition to decreased shivering intensity as BAT capacity and activity increase. These changes occur with no net difference in whole-body thermogenesis.

See: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28025824](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28025824)

**Science on cold thermogenesis is catching up on the role of fundamental particles of physics on biology.
**
**Tensegrity 9:**
"This is why ice expands when water cools. It also points out why cold thermogenesisworks fundamentally because cold increase heat release from mitochondria naturally. This makes you more energy efficient"

and:

"As electron current flow slows, the resultant magnetic field generated within the mitochondria also shrinks. This loss of magnetism is also felt big time in the FAD moiety 3D molecular arrangement. When ECT slows it needs the higher powered photon to set the stage to make superoxide. Eukaryotic “magnetic sense” is built directly into their mitochondria everywhere mitochondria are found in tissues. This means any free radicals you do make, can be used for signaling that is yoked, fine tuned and coupled properly to physiologic functioning. When superoxide is missing, cell signaling declines, which destroys its metastability. If a cell is not metastable it can not read or react the environment it is in. _A cell needs to be metastable to entangle electrons and protons in their mitochondria. That is what classic leptin resistance is at its core."_

Link: [https://www.jackkruse.com/tensegrity-9-magnetic-mitochondria-memory-creates-coherence/](https://www.jackkruse.com/tensegrity-9-magnetic-mitochondria-memory-creates-coherence/)

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-08T15:02:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-confimed-cold-origin-of-life-2017.20990/post-236665>

Damn, another one.

(emphasis mine).

**Dynamic changes in the mitochondrial electron transport chain underpinning cold acclimation of leaf respiration.**

We examined the effect of short- and long-term changes in temperature on gene expression, protein abundance, and the activity of the alternative oxidase and cytochrome oxidase pathways (AOP and COP, respectively) in Arabidopsis thaliana. The AOP was more sensitive to short-term changes in temperature than the COP, with partitioning to the AOP decreasing significantly below a threshold temperature of 20 degrees C. **AOP activity was increased in leaves, which had been shifted to the cold for several days, but this response was transient, with AOP activity subsiding (and COP activity increasing) following the development of leaves in the cold.** The transient increase in AOP activity in 10-d cold-shifted leaves was not associated with an increase in alternative oxidase (AOX) protein or AOX1a transcript abundance. By contrast, the amount of uncoupling protein was significantly increased in cold-developed leaves. In conjunction with this, transcript levels of the uncoupling protein-encoding gene UCP1 and the external NAD(P)H dehydrogenase-encoding gene NDB2 exhibited sustained increases following growth in the cold. The data suggest a role for each of these alternative non-phosphorylating bypasses of mitochondrialelectrontransport at different points in time following exposure to cold, with increased AOP activity being important only in the early stages of coldtreatment.

\--------------------------------------------------------------------

Why is the response "transient"?! Maybe because the biophysical effects ends once the cold is removed. Again, an indication that cold is not just biochemical but has physical consequences as well.

Link: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed...rpinning+cold+acclimation+of+leaf+respiration](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Dynamic+changes+in+the+mitochondrial+electron+transport+chain+underpinning+cold+acclimation+of+leaf+respiration)

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-05-13T17:45:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-confimed-cold-origin-of-life-2017.20990/post-241120>

**Another one (emphasis mine):

**Quantum Dynamics of Self-Assembly of Minimal Photosynthetic Cells**
The dynamics of quantum mechanical self-assembly of two separate supramolecular systems, i.e., two minimal cells each containing two different photosynthetic centres (1) and (2) were investigated by means of semiempirical and density functional theory methods (DFT) using the electron correlation approach.** These two bi cellular systems consist of 717 atoms (3) or 696 atoms (4). The minimal cells studied were based on different photoactive sensitizer molecules, precursor of a fatty acid molecule (pFA) attached via Van der Waals forces, all surrounded by fatty acid (FA) and water molecules. The electron correlation interactions responsible for the weak hydrogen and Van der Waals chemical bonds increased due to the addition of polar water solvent molecules. The distances between the separated sensitizer, FA, pFA, and water molecules are comparable to Van der Waals and hydrogen bonding radii. As a result, the overall systems become compressed, resulting in photo-excited electron tunnelling from the sensitizer (bis(4-diphenylamine-2-phenyl)-squarine or 1,4-bis(_N_,_N_\-dimethylamino)naphthalene) to the pFA molecules. **Absorption spectra as well as quantum entangled electron density transfer trajectories associated with the different excited states were calculated using time dependent density functional theory methods (TD-DFT). The results allow separation of the photosynthetic transitions within the same minimal cells (1) and (2). Presence of photosynthesis in nature and in our artificial cells quantum mechanical investigations is questionlessly the most important biochemical reaction on Earth and probably most the important in the emergence of life in the Isua Greenstone Belt in Greenland aged 3.7–3.85 billion years.** We address the idea that quantum entanglement could have played an important role in the first stages of origins of life and evolution of biospheres because it enhances the photosynthesis, leading to faster growth and self-replication of minimal living cells. The quantum mechanically modelled possibility of synthesizing artificial self-reproducing quantum entangled prebiotic minimal cells also impacts the possibility of the most probable path of emergence of protocells on the Earth or elsewhere.

Source:

[http://www.ingentaconnect.com/content/asp/qm/2016/00000005/00000001/art00002](http://www.ingentaconnect.com/content/asp/qm/2016/00000005/00000001/art00002)

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-05-16T10:22:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ct-4-confimed-cold-origin-of-life-2017.20990/post-241326>

**Another paper on the origins of life, which contradicts the earlier assertion in the first post of this thread.**

Mind-boggling paper. I'm not emphasizing, because every line in this abstract is so interesting:

****Cause of Cambrian Explosion - Terrestrial or Cosmic?****
We review the salient evidence consistent with or predicted by the Hoyle-Wickramasinghe (H-W) thesis of Cometary (Cosmic) Biology. Much of this physical and biological evidence is multifactorial. One particular focus are the recent studies which date the emergence of the complex retroviruses of vertebrate lines at or just before the Cambrian Explosion of ∼500 Ma. Such viruses are known to be plausibly associated with major evolutionary genomic processes. We believe this coincidence is not fortuitous but is consistent with a key prediction of H-W theory whereby major extinction-diversification evolutionary boundaries coincide with virus-bearing cometary-bolide bombardment events. A second focus is the remarkable evolution of intelligent complexity (Cephalopods) culminating in the emergence of the Octopus. A third focus concerns the micro-organism fossil evidence contained within meteorites as well as the detection in the upper atmosphere of apparent incoming life-bearing particles from space. In our view the totality of the multifactorial data and critical analyses assembled by Fred Hoyle, Chandra Wickramasinghe and their many colleagues since the 1960s leads to a very plausible conclusion - life may have been seeded here on Earth by life-bearing comets as soon as conditions on Earth allowed it to flourish (about or just before 4.1 Billion years ago); and living organisms such as space-resistant and space-hardy bacteria, viruses, more complex eukaryotic cells, fertilised ova and seeds have been continuously delivered ever since to Earth so being one important driver of further terrestrial evolution which has resulted in considerable genetic diversity and which has led to the emergence of mankind.

Link: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/29544820](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/29544820)

Full text: [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079610718300798?via=ihub](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079610718300798?via%3Dihub)

# Thread: Just moved, slow adaptation to new light cycles
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-moved-slow-adaptation-to-new-light-cycles.20994/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 5

### Imogen Skye — 2018-03-05T23:58:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-moved-slow-adaptation-to-new-light-cycles.20994/post-236369>

We were living in Yukon at 60\*N for ~9 yrs, and we moved to 48\*N 3 months ago. In doing so, we gained hours of twilight that do not exist in Yukon, and of course sunlight during winter that we didn't have (right now the sun time is approx equal to there, although it is getting to the 24 hr sun in Yukon, so it changes quickly). My 5 children and I have spent nearly a decade in a very extreme light cycle that messed up our circadian rhythms very obviously. Since relocating, we only need to eat about half of what we did before, have dropped nearly all of the supplements we needed there just to remain stable health-wise, and my children have been in a growth spurt since about two weeks after arriving here, all outgrowing their sizes within two months and now about to go up another size. They are already tall (and slender), but this rate of growth is unprecedented since they were infants. My eldest is 14, so they have all been at 60\*N for most/all of their childhood. I have a traumatic brain injury from a collision 3 yrs ago and still have symptoms of this that are complex, so I may include these in responses if it seems relevant.

My question is about sleep times, given whatever clues you can glean from what I shared above. We are 3 hrs ahead of Yukon now. It has been 3 months. My 12-14yo children are having a very hard time getting to sleep before 11pm, which has come down from our arrival of 2am sleeptimes But, 3 hr time difference to consider)! We still observe all the nighttime protocol for no screens and low-no light after sunset. My 7 and 10yo and I can all fall asleep easily between 9:30 and 10:00pm. But NONE of us can wake up before 9am- at all. Some days we even sleep right through the morning alarm until 10am! If we happen to get up, we're sleepy and sleep-drunk for an hour or two before we're really alert. I don't know if this is an adjustment period, if this is actually a normal developmental sleep time, or what to think about it. I get concerned because I would like us to be up at sunrise, but this is so impossible for me or them. I noticed that if I am woken at 6am, I feel fine to be up for the day, but if I do this, I need a 2-3hr nap in the afternoon. I don't know if this is healthy and just inconvenient, or a problem.

Some more info is that we have no other symptoms of unwellness that are obvious. The circulation problems we had in Yukon are resolved or in the case of my 14 yo, nearly- looks like he is about to done with toe infections for the first time in years in the next few days. I have been sunbathing daily for the past few days down to -3C. My children are outside everyday for at least an hour but usually several hours, and our windows are mostly very old single pane- atrocious for insulation and great for sunlight, or old double-pane also letting in lots of light and heat. There are streetlamps here, the closest is about 100 metres and it is a low-lumen LED. There are two lamps visible from my house. I live in a remote town, north of Lake Superior, cell service is spotty because the towers are far, and there is no industry here. I do not have wifi- only ethernet wired in (no gaming systems, just one computer and one cell phone in the home), but we do have a smartmeter outside our kitchen, and the mast for our electricity comes in the second floor in a bedroom, opposite the beds. I am suspicious of these and hope to figure out a way to deal with these asap.

Any insights very welcome and I appreciate them very much!

### Erin Gaiser — 2018-07-22T11:00:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-moved-slow-adaptation-to-new-light-cycles.20994/post-246400>

Im new here, but smart meter is terrible.....

Right now my kids are out everyday in just a swimming suit - but Im trying to figure out what to do when winter comes. How are you doing the sunlight in winter with the kids?

### JanSz — 2018-07-22T14:54:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-moved-slow-adaptation-to-new-light-cycles.20994/post-246408>

We were living in Yukon at 60\*N for ~9 yrs, and we moved to 48\*N **3 months ago.**

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Imogen Skye — 2018-07-23T17:55:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-moved-slow-adaptation-to-new-light-cycles.20994/post-246522>

Erin, we just made sure we were outside every day that it was possible, and wore as little clothing as possible. Then once it was tolerable, we started going out with our skin exposed. I started sunbathing at -3, by carving a "chair" out of the snow on my deck. I sunbathed everyday until it was warm, and now I do several times each week, but I am outside nearly everyday for many hours. Exceptions are rainy days that are also cold. If it is warm and rainy, I'm out still.

Of note: My children are home-educated, and I am not employed outside the home, so we have a flexible schedule.

### Imogen Skye — 2018-07-23T18:03:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/just-moved-slow-adaptation-to-new-light-cycles.20994/post-246523>

JanSz, it has now been 8 months and we're still adapting, haha. But much better than we were before. I realised that I expect adaptation to new things to happen much faster than is likely or even possible at times. Thanks for the reminder/confirmation! I was talking with a friend about my unrealistic expectations being fixed by this experience just before I saw your reply!  Takin' it easy now....

### kris90 — 2018-03-07T17:18:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trouble-with-blacklights.20999/post-236564>

> **[Blacklight Blue Bulbs](http://www.lightingsupply.com/blacklight-blue-tubes.aspx)**
> A blacklight blue bulb is a light bulb that purposely blocks almost all visible light from leaving the bulb, so you're left with only a dim purple (or violet) glow coming from the bulb. The rest of what comes from the bulb is beyond violet, or what we call "ultraviolet" -- wavelengths that are too short for us to see with our eyes. But they can cause items to fluoresce, or glow, and this is why we get a fluorescent effect from these bulbs, which are often used in night clubs or at parties especially during Halloween.
> So if you remember seeing your shoe laces glowing from a black light, this is why. And now you know that was really a blacklight blue light bulb.
> Since ultraviolet or UV light is infamously associated with skin cancer and potentially other health problems, it's worth mentioning that there are three types of UV light: UVA, UVB, and UVC. UVA, which is closest to the visible light spectrum, is the safest of the three, and this is primarily the type of UV light emitted by a black light. So occasional exposure to a blacklight blue bulb is not expected to cause any health problems.
>
> **[Black Lights](http://www.lightingsupply.com/blacklight-tubes.aspx)**
> While we typically refer to blacklight blue bulbs as "black lights," now you know that's not the correct term, at least in the lighting industry. Black lights, ironically, not only produce more visible light than blacklight blue bulbs, but the light from a black light is actually bluer. (The blacklight blue bulb's light is more purple, or violet.)
> The light from a black light is much brighter than that from a blacklight blue bulb, because it doesn't attempt to block all visible light from the bulb. These bulbs are perhaps most famously used in bug lights, or bug zappers. You may recall the bluish glow coming from these devices. Insects can see the emitted UV light and are attracted to it.
> Of course if you're looking to avoid bugs, [yellow lights](http://www.lightingsupply.com/catalogsearch/result/index/cat/951/lsc_base/Medium%20\(E26\)/?q=yellow) work best. So you can keep your bug zapper at one end of the yard while you and your friends sit closer to a yellow or "bug repellant" light. (These are just harder for bugs to see.)
> We hope this helps you to understand the difference between black lights and blacklight blue bulbs -- a somewhat confusing topic until you've seen the facts! Of course if you need black lights or any kind of lighting at all, we're here to assist by answering your questions and helping to take your order.

[http://www.lightingsupply.com/articles/black-lights-blacklight-blue-bulbs/](http://www.lightingsupply.com/articles/black-lights-blacklight-blue-bulbs/)

### Jan Christer — 2018-03-07T19:42:13+0200

**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AdamF — 2018-03-06T06:37:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pomc-satiety.21000/post-236399>

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25707796](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25707796)

### Jack Kruse — 2018-03-21T23:15:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pomc-satiety.21000/post-237696>

The Leptin Rx gets another win. See every sunrise every day of your life from here on out. Jasin says, "Unsurprisingly, researchers found those exposed to moderate or higher intensity light earlier in the day had lower body fat than those with more of their light exposure later in the day." [https://theweighwewere.com/mean-light-timing-may-influence-body-mass-index-body-fat/](https://theweighwewere.com/mean-light-timing-may-influence-body-mass-index-body-fat/)

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-28T11:32:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pomc-satiety.21000/post-238101>

> **Short-term feeding at the wrong time is sufficient to desynchronize peripheral clocks and induce obesity with hyperphagia, physical inactivity and metabolic disorders in mice.**
> **BACKGROUND:**
> The circadian clock regulates various physiological and behavioral rhythms such as feeding and locomotor activity. Feeding at unusual times of the day (inactive phase) is thought to be associated with obesity and metabolic disorders in experimental animals and in humans.
>
> **OBJECTIVE:**
> The present study aimed to determine the underlying mechanisms through which time-of-day-dependent feeding influences metabolic homeostasis.
>
> **METHODS:**
> We compared food consumption, wheel-running activity, core body temperature, hormonal and metabolic variables in blood, lipid accumulation in the liver, circadian expression of clock and metabolic genes in peripheral tissues, and body weight gain between mice fed only during the sleep phase (DF, daytime feeding) and those fed only during the active phase (NF, nighttime feeding). All mice were fed with the same high-fat high-sucrose diet throughout the experiment. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to examine the metabolic effects of time-imposed restricted feeding (RF) in mice with free access to a running wheel.
>
> **RESULTS:**
> After one week of RF, DF mice gained more weight and developed hyperphagia, higher feed efficiency and more adiposity than NF mice. The daily amount of running on the wheel was rapidly and obviously reduced by DF, which might have been the result of time-of-day-dependent hypothermia. The amount of daily food consumption and hypothalamic mRNA expression of orexigenic neuropeptide Y and agouti-related protein were significantly higher in DF, than in NF mice, although levels of plasma leptin that fluctuate in an RF-dependent circadian manner, were significantly higher in DF mice. These findings suggested that the DF induced leptin resistance. The circadian phases of plasma insulin and ghrelin were synchronized to RF, although the corticosterone phase was unaffected. Peak levels of plasma insulin were remarkably higher in DF mice, although HOMA-IR was identical between the two groups. Significantly more free fatty acids, triglycerides and cholesterol accumulated in the livers of DF, than NF mice, which resulted from the increased expression of lipogenic genes such as Scd1, Acaca, and Fasn. Temporal expression of circadian clock genes became synchronized to RF in the liver but not in skeletal muscle, suggesting that uncoupling metabolic rhythms between the liver and skeletal muscle also contribute to DF-induced adiposity.
>
> **CONCLUSION:**
> Feeding at an unusual time of day (inactive phase) desynchronizes peripheral clocks and causes obesity and metabolic disorders by inducing leptin resistance, hyperphagia, physical inactivity, hepatic fat accumulation and adiposity.

Keep in mind that mice are noctural animals. They mostly feed themselves during the night...

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-28T12:09:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pomc-satiety.21000/post-238102>

Another one on humans:

**Later circadian timing of food intake is associated with increased body fat.**
Background: Weight gain and obesity have reached alarming levels. Eating at a later clock hour is a newly described risk factor for adverse metabolic health; yet, how eating at a later circadian time influences body composition is unknown. Using clock hour to document eating times may be misleading owing to individual differences in circadian timing relative to clock hour.Objective: This study examined the relations between the timing of food consumption relative to clock hour and endogenous circadian time, content of food intake, and body composition.Design: We enrolled 110 participants, aged 18-22 y, in a 30-d cross-sectional study to document sleep and circadian behaviors within their regular daily routines. **We used a time-stamped-picture mobile phone application to record all food intake across 7 consecutive days during a participant's regular daily routines and assessed their body composition and timing of melatonin release during an in-laboratory assessment.Results: Nonlean individuals (high body fat) consumed most of their calories 1.1 h closer to melatonin onset, which heralds the beginning of the biological night, than did lean individuals (low body fat) (log-rank P = 0.009).** In contrast, there were no differences between lean and nonlean individuals in the clock hour of food consumption (P = 0.72). Multiple regression analysis showed that the timing of food intake relative to melatonin onset was significantly associated with the percentage of body fat and body mass index (both P < 0.05) while controlling for sex, whereas no relations were found between the clock hour of food intake, caloric amount, meal macronutrient composition, activity or exercise level, or sleep duration and either of these body composition measures (all P > 0.72).**Conclusions: These results provide evidence that the consumption of food during the circadian evening and/or night, independent of more traditional risk factors such as amount or content of food intake and activity level, plays an important role in body composition.**

Emphasis mine.

Link: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28877894](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28877894)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pomc-satiety.21000/post-238116>

# Thread: Qauntum Vacation journal- Isla Mujeres
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 29
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-06T19:45:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236456>

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

What happens when two mitochondria's team up? Friendships are made, new places are discovered and New possibilities uncovered. I spent 10 days on the island of Isla Mujeres with a quantum Sherpa to show me the way. My name is Paul, Im the dude on the right, I've been here on the forum for just over a year. I've been following jacks work for awhile but like most of you it took a severe health crisis to get some skin in game and give crazy a shot. 2 years ago I burned myself out and I had a huge laundry list of symptoms I won't bore you with. I learned from jacks wisdom and reintegrated myself back into the natural environment as best I could. I have managed to reverse the majority of my symptoms over the last year using the principals of light water and magnetism. One of jacks prescriptions from our consult was to get down to the Yucatan as much as I could throughout the winter months. I live in Central Ontario Canada where the winter Sun is scarce and the snowbirds fly south. I took his advice and flew south myself several times so far this winter. This is the Journal of my second adventure to the crater Basin of quintaroo Me-hico.

I arrived On a cool overcast day finding locals dressed in sweaters but it felt perfectly toasty, I myself escaping from -20 degrees Celsius conditions. The ferry ride from Cancun to Isla was quick, it rained for the duration of the trip preventing me from riding on the top deck. We cut through the choppy weather with ease and in no time I was ashore on the "Island of women". Loyd greeted me and had no problems picking me out of a crowd of locals, my suitcase wheeling behind me. It was the first time Id met Loyd in person having only spoken through the Forum and Facebook. We exchanged pleasantries and soon were swiftly on our way to a local organic market, which only appeared once a week on the island. We navigated our way down the busy main strip, down a narrow street dodging scooters and golf carts. The market was located in the red Buddha a local yoga studio located just off the main drag of downtown Isla. Inside, fresh organic tropical fruits and veggies were spread out on a fold up table. Loyd grabbed his pre-ordered bundle while I stood overwhelmed for a few minutes trying to decide between the odd-looking choices in front of me. I have been in seasonal Ketosis at home and the sight of fresh mangoes and yucca threw me for a spin. I managed to gather together a bag and between the 2 of us we lugged groceries, luggage and hunted down the closest cab. I grabbed shotgun so as to take in the island and ocean breeze. Nothing is far here as the island is quite small, only 7 kilometres from end to end. We left the busy tourist area of Playa Centro and headed for the eastern rocky coast.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-06T19:55:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236457>

We arrived at what was to be my home for the next week and a half. " La Casa Del Mar". along yellow building with a large mural of a marlin decorating one side. The front door also had an engraving of the same ocean fish carved into the wood. The apartment was a small two bedroom with kitchen, bath, living room, hammock, decked out with Red and Purple Lights. Hanging on the key rack was an assortment of evening blue blocking accessories. My kind of joint! it was simple and practical for all my mito-hacking needs. I came to the the Yucatan to Feel the quantum yield once again, not to lounge around in a fancy resort. I decided it was best If we hit the local grocery store to stock up on the rest of the items needed for my stay while the clouds still loomed over. We both grabbed our blue blockers and I chuckled at the notion that this was the first time I would be with someone else in public who didn't give a damn about the strange looks one gets from orange and red lenses. Our selection of Items was limited due to the addition of fluoridated salt to almost everything. Luckily my quantum sherpa already had most of the safe items Identified, saving us precious time. I marvelled at all the cheap tropical produce, not to mention how fresh everything was, compared to the out of season irradiated selection of the local grocery stores back home. I was excited to get all this stuff back, to see what kinda of exotic cuisine I could conjure up under ruby lights. I was exhausted from all the excitement of travel and turned in quite quickly for the evening once landing back at la casa.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-06T20:18:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236461>

On day 2 We both rose early before the sun and made our food for the day, packed up breakfast and headed down cliffside to await the arrival of the morning sun. Loyd had a perfect spot to spend the morning on the rocky east-side of Isla not 5 mins from home. Upon arriving I took off my shoes and socks and climbed down the wind-carved path to a rocky enclosure facing east. The winds were fierce stirring up a constant crashing of waves to the shore. The clean crisp ocean breeze whistled through the rocky outcropping carrying with it all the negative Ions one could ever want as the waves smashed endlessly below. I settled down cross-legged in a sandy spot with my toes touching some rock. We chatted as the sun rose and then made good work of breakfast. I finished off my morning sun getting my feet planted in the water and a quick look around at the beautiful scenery. Once I had my fill we headed homeward, grabbed lunch and made our way to the beach wasting no time.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-06T20:28:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236465>

We arrived at Playa Lancheros a cozy beach restaurant located on the calmer west side of Isa. We of course came for the beach out front. I've learned that pretty much all the beaches are public property so you don't have to worry about getting hassled in front of resorts, as long as you don't trespass to get to them. I immediately got in the ocean to grab some magnetism and then promptly parked my self in the midday sun putting my solar callus to the test. Loyd and I hit it off well, chatting endlessly as two well-seasoned mitochondriac's do. I spent the afternoon getting in and out the water cooling my surface and making the most of UV assimilation. The first afternoon in the sun flew by and before we noticed, a wedding party began to arrive behind us at the restaurant. The wedding planner politely informed us and we gathered up our beach gear and decided to move on. We had our sun for the day, so we decided to walk home along the Salinas, a freshwater river that runs right through the middle of the island. The sunset through the clouds as we made our way through town observing the light and shadows dancing on the tattered, but charming, colourful buildings of Isla.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-06T20:39:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236472>

We landed home and got to cooking again for the next day. I was really there to eat the sun so It made no sense to cook during precious daylit hours, so we got down to work again.I also took the opportunity to completely black out my room with some electrical tape I purchased the night before. I taped some cardboard to the window frame and blocked out some stray light from the air conditioner on the wall. One of the most potent sleep hacks I've given to people is to get your bedroom pitch black with zero light entering in and I wasn't about to settle for anything less here. We both retired early looking forward to having another long of day soaking up the sun. I learned quickly that here getting to sleep was as easy as putting your head to pillow.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-06T20:51:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236481>

On day 3 I got down cliffside early to sit listening to the waves as they endlessly bombarded the shoreline. At dawn, feeling limber after having a good nights rest, I decided to free climb the rocks and boulders as the morning sun made its way through the cloudy pink horizon. After I had my fill of grounding, I rendezvous back for breakfast shoreside with my Quantum Sherpa, he advised me of north winds for the day so we had better make for another beach on the west side again. We made our way back into the downtown core, this time hitching a ride with a local on his way to work on a golf cart. One thing you will learn quickly here is the locals are friendly and generous, Lloyd offered three times to pay for the ride but driver would have none of it. We arrived beachside early having pretty much the whole damn place to our self. I wasted no time Diving right on into the drink, getting some laps in and exploring the western shoreline. I rinsed and repeated ocean sun, ocean sun as the tourists and locals unloaded off the ferries for their afternoon excursions. The first few days were easily spent wandering the various beaches drinking water and building my redox basking under that wonderful yellow ball of fire.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-06T20:52:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236482>

More to come stay tuned.....

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-06T21:14:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236487>

Day 5 was my cenote excursion to the mainland. For those of you not familiar with Cenotes, they are a underground system of freshwater rivers that flow from the epicentre of the Chixilub crater.The water is endowed with strong magnetism and it is also cold, creating a perfect healing menagerie when combined with the hot sun. I had planned to meet up with my good friend Phil and his girlfriend, as by happenstance they were also staying on another Island called Cozumel. They were not wise to the quantum ways and I had to have a sit down chat before our respective trips to assure them that their excursion off the resort would be a safe endeavour.
I planned to leave early and to time my ferry ride so as to make it a "sunrise cruise". As I was climbing up the steps to the top deck I heard two people behind me questioning whether or not to follow me up. I assured them that it was indeed a good idea because the sunrise was in T-minus 5 minutes upon departure. They followed me up and we began to chat under the pinkish-orange glow of the spiralling horizon.

One of them turned out to be quite interesting. She was a young woman about my age, she had just returned from week working as head chef on a private Yaught for a month. She was American but lived in Panama with her boyfriend and It turns out they are both professional free-divers. I started talking about the Yuma pearl divers from Japan and the health benefits of the un-assisted deep dive. She concurred and spoke of the local indigenous free- diving tribes of Panama and expounded on how they were the most robust, fit, hearty peoples she had ever seen. She also made the point of how dangerous modern competitive free diving is, as they had lost a few friends to the sport in recent years. The sunrise turned out to be one of the best of my trip. The clouds parted just in time to let the glorious orange rays reach across the ocean to us.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-07T00:54:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236511>

Dockside waiting with a big grin was my Good friend Señor Cruz. He would be our guide for the day and I wouldn't think to have anyone else show me around the Yucatan. We decided to stand at the edge of the dock to share the remainder of the rising sun, before departing on our adventure. It was an hour drive to Playa del Carmen to pick up my friends but it was a pleasant drive down the coast, as Señor Cruz and I caught up from our last encounter 2 months prior. We arrived at the ferry port, quickly grabbed our other two passengers and continued south in the direction of Tulum. About 20 minutes down the highway we landed at a Mayan reserve called Tanka.
The entrance was hidden from the highway without any sign that I could see. Señor Cruz informed us that Tanka was now a private facility that only operated with a small number of tourists. He had an "in" because he is old friends with the owners son and we would most likely have the park to ourselves. We rolled our down a bumpy windy road cut through the Jungle eventually landing at the parking lot.
I took off my shoes and shirt grabbed my towel and we made our way down the dirt road to the first of 4 Cenotes.

Here we found the zip line portion of the tour and we climbed up the wooden stairs to the first platform that stood just above the tree line, overlooking the jungle water below. My friends volunteered me to go first so I grabbed a brake carved out of a tree to apply to the steel cable. The break was more for manoeuvring your self or to stop any unwanted spin, as they assured us they installed a new spring based breaking system in recent years. Looking down on the Cenote water, It reminded me of the local freshwater ponds and rivers back home in the summertime, full of lily pads, water flowers and half-submerged trees. I stepped up to the platform and allowed my zip line operator to adjust my harness and I was whisked off sailing atop the sparkling waters below.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-07T16:56:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236556>

Seeing that I survived the flight my friends boarded and flew across to join me with big smiles painted across their faces. We stepped up on the platform once more which took us down the length of the river to a set of docks. Señor Cruz was there waiting for us and we manned 2 canoes for our trip back. The waters were calm and the sun was out in full force as it lit our way back sparkling on the surface. We took our time investigating the exotic flora along the shoreline. We spotted 8 turtles scattered about with their heads popped fully erect towards the sun, finishing up their own morning sun gazing routine. We paddled our way back towards the north shore where our Mayan guides were waiting to tie our vessels back up.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-07T20:39:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236618>

Señor Cruz announced it was officially Siesta time, as he showed us to a sandy rest area filled with hammocks. We climbed in and gently swayed as the violet rays blinked in and out through the bright fluffy clouds overhead. We grabbed some water from the cooler and made our way to cenote number 2, It was a medium-sized swimming hole carved out of limestone. We entered on the east side down a set of wooden stairs to a dock and I wasted no time jumping in. The water was crystal clear and you could see straight to the bottom, where large prehistoric caved in stones were laid to rest. I swam quickly over to the north side to a small cave where I found a nice flat submerged ledge that I could hop up onto, putting my torso in the sun as I investigated the jagged cavern. My friends soon followed suit after taking in the scenery with several splashes. Señor Cruz then pointed our attention to the south side, to a ledge where we could dive in. I made my way back up and climbed up to the ledge. Looking down into the clear cenote fresh water the depth is always deceiving to the untrained eye. Objects always appear closer then they seem but I trusted my friend and wasted no time getting airborne and diving into the Azure cool water before me. I continued to float around and soak up the midday sun, my friends were eager to proceed as we had much more to explore, so begrudgingly I dragged my self back on the dock and gathered my stuff.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-08T00:12:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236640>

We made our way back onto the jungle path where half way up we found a square hole cut into the ground through the limestone about 9 foot on each side roughly. There were a set of stairs carved right into the rock leading into the underground river, which I'm assuming fed our last stop. I climbed halfway down when one of the Mayan guides stopped me. I asked him why and his English was not good so he just shook his head. I made the universal finger across the neck gesture and he nodded. I looked at my friend and laughed as I complied with his request before getting a quick picture. On the way my friend Phil told me how rejuvenated he felt after coming out of the water. He said he suffered mild heat stroke the day before on the beach and that his energy levels this morning were quite low. I smiled and patted him on the back and said "oh we're just getting started my friend".

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-08T00:23:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236642>

The third cenote seemed unassuming at first, it was a small but deep circular cavern cut into the ground. As señor Cruz walked by he threw a pebble into the pit and made a comment of Mayan sacrifice to the pirañas. The stone was swarmed by hungry tiny little fish. I chuckled and explained to my friends that they were really harmless "the worst you'll get from them is a good pedicure on the house" I remarked. The fresh waters are home to small fish that eat the dead skin off your feet if you remain still in the water. You will also find the odd crayfish and miniature catfish that also call the cenote home . Señor Cruz pointed to another ledge safe for jumping , so I decided to start off this time with a walk off the plank. I landed with a mighty splash and as I emerged to my surprise there was quite a large underground cavernous area not visible from the top. Deep in the cave a huge sunbeam shot in like some scene from Indiana Jones. As usual, I am quickly drawn to all things solar so I quickly breast stroked my way over to investigate. It turns out there was another square hole carved out of the ceiling of the cave. This one was definitely man-made, as the right angles on the corners were too perfect. The sun, on an angle, in the winter sky shone in from the corner and entered illuminating the dark cave as bats flew around the ceiling from all the excitement. This eerie beam of light cast a large flashlight right to the bottom, illuminating the rocks below. I imagined to myself that in the summer months when the sun being more directly overhead, would reveal something more interesting directly below my position. The Mayans are known for building monuments that take advantage of the heavenly bodies. For instance at Chichen Itza, at the autumnal equinox, a snake is revealed by sunlight casting shadows on the pyramid steps.
After a little more exploration I decided to make my way up and have a look through another angle. I ended up Jumping through after consulting with one of the Mayan guides on site.

When we had our fill of the cave we made our way onto the final stop at Tanka, the zip line plunge!. After another stroll down the jungle path, we emerged standing on a cliff above the last cenote. This one looked like a combination of all the previous cenotes rolled into one.It had an open sun swimming area, a small cave with a small hole in the ceiling and access to a marshy area on the north side. Over the top of the cave entrance hung a zip line that transports you out over the water where it stops dead in the middle, so your only way off is to let go and plunge into the emerald green water below. I was the only volunteer for this last activity. My first attempt ended with a horrible belly flop followed quickly by a second go utilizing a less painful pencil dive. I spent the next half hour exploring and eventually just floating in the sunlight before being dragged out. It was time to move onto the next adventure for the day.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-08T16:58:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236676>

Fifteen minutes up the highway back towards Playa we entered Dos Ojos park. This is a facility again operated by a local Mayan community but this one was open to the public. I visited a few months before to check out the park in full but today we had one place on our bucket list. Sac-actun is probably one of the most incredible cenotes you may ever encounter. No pictures or words can do this place justice you really have to come and experience it for yourself to get the full gravity of it. It is an underground river cenote where the water is cold because very little sunlight hits the surface only at the entrance. You must have a guide, it is mandatory because the tunnel itself is very sensitive to human interactions. Inside the tunnel hang thousands of stalagmites that have been growing at an alarmingly slow rate, something like a millimeter every century. You can stunt their growth by touching them with your bare hands due to the oily nature your skin. It's about a 45-minute tour down an underground lazy river where you are mesmerized by sparkling minerals on the ceiling and walls, giant prehistoric fossils and bones, flickering shadows, beautifully colored lights strobing off every surface and eerie limestone formations like something out of H.R. Giegers worst nightmare. My friends were absolutely captivated , as I was, on my first trip down below. It is also a popular destination for master divers as it boats a huge underwater cavern system. This is only for the hardcore enthusiast as it takes 7 or 8 hours to navigate. I was told that they plant extra tanks along the way in case of emergency.

We left the caves feeling reenergized and hungry from the 45 minutes of CT. I ran to the parking lot to warm my bones in the afternoon sun where Señor Cruz was waiting patiently. I waived to the next band of adventurers and warned them of the magic that awaited them below. We eagerly piled into the car our hunger wanting to ride faster than our mode of transportation could muster. Headed eastward with my arm out the window catching every last golden ray, I could smell the salty ocean breeze as we neared our beachside destination. There it was Chamicos! We could see the smoke and licking flames that burned on the open fire pit as we rolled into the parking lot. The outdoor kitchen was buzzing with activity everyone hopping around in the fresh sea breeze. I once again kicked off my shoes and made my way to a table in the sand. This was paradise, with the sun beginning its descent back down to Hades and our bellies grumbling falling into our seats. The ocean tripped over itself as the waves tumbled along to the sandy beach in front of us. We ordered ceviche and lobster and we breathed a sigh of relief as our fast-paced day was finally winding down. The restaurant was completely outside beside a few small huts and bathroom facilities. Our table sat in the waving shade of the palm trees and we all sat content, reviewing the day's many excitements. Our food arrived and we chowed down like pigs at the troff. It was as good as they warned it would be, maybe even better. After stuffing ourselves we lounged in the setting sun watching families walk the beach and sharing some laughs. Sadly our day had come to an end and we crawled back into the car. It was a silent trip back to our respective destinations as we had nothing left to say, fully content and filled to the brim with fine cuisine and lasting memories.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-03-08T19:07:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236694>

Looks like you hit all my spots......

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-08T19:19:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236698>

Absolutely, got to get that skin in the game my friend. Big shout out to Senor Cruz. Anyone else on here who wants to put the quantum yield to the test for themselves call him up you wont be disappointed.

### drezy — 2018-03-08T20:20:38+0200

Awesome chronicle of your adventures. People here should be loving this.

This is such a great glimpse at how this can be done right and seeing is believing.

> [Sun Disciple said:](/goto/post?id=236676)
>
> eerie limestone formations like something out of H.R. Giegers worst nightmare.

+50 cool cultural points for the Gieger reference.

You know the woman who would become my wife asked to meet for dinner and halfway through showed me one of those blurry as shit medical images taken of women parts. All I could see was some lump so I started telling her that I'm sorry that it appeared her uterine fibroids were back. The woman let me go on for minutes until she told me to look again. On second look all I had, but didn't say, is almost exactly what you say above. It looked like some H.R.Geiger painting. Luckily she interrupted my thinking to inform me that I had offspring.

Every once in a while my boy will drag ass or not help when requested and I'll inform my wife that I still think my first guess was right and that my supposed son is just an extremely advanced uterine fibroid.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-09T16:41:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-236766>

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

On day 6 I found out Loyd had been busy while I was gone. He managed to locate a very special excursion for the day. He had found a restaurant on the west side of the island that had a meat locker converted into a sculpted ice bar that boasted 150 tons of frozen cubes.
He couldn't have picked a better day as it was the hottest of my trip. We laughed on the way imagining ourselves as hired sideshow exhibits The "Isla Icemen" they will call us. We gladly paid 100 pesos each to access the ice bar all day with no drinks. She opened the large steel door with a creek, condensation and fog billowed out as we stepped into what seemed like another dimension. It was at least 50 degrees cooler than outside, lit by black lights, flashing multicolored strobes and 70's dance music.following Behind us was barmen decked out in a snowsuit who stood guard while we cooled our bones. I was really feeling sluggish on our walk but after a few minutes sitting half-naked on a block of Ice, I was rejuvenated. The restaurant itself sat on the beach so we set up shop in the sand for the day. I made a trip inside every hour or so to cool my skin temp down and then hop back out into the blistering sun. While inside I would lay down on a block of ice carved into a bench, don some blue blockers and tap my feet to the repetitive disco boogie. (Note to self-bring earplugs next time) Needless to say, I attracted the curious attention of neon shot glass shooting tourists as they watched me getting cozy amongst the mammoth ice blocks. I also drew the attendance of the poor Mexican bartender who rubbed his mittens together frantically, moving like some desperate Arctic castaway, trying to light some kindling. Literal boatloads of tourists filed off off catamarans onto the restaurant docks while tour guides blasted directions through thudding bass heavy loudspeakers guiding them to the buffet for lunch. They had quite the operation going, turn styling herds of people in and out with great efficiency. We stayed until they finally closed around 5, which was fine by us the clouds were closing in making our chances of catching the sunrise very small.

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-12T15:39:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-237039>

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-12T16:43:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-237044>

I spent my last few days on the island perfecting my quantum routine. Mornings were spent worshipping the rising sun, grounded to the rocky western shore. In the afternoon you could find Loyd and I lounging on the world-class beaches, building our redox. . I did this as you can see all the way to last few minutes of my trip before boarding back on to the ferry one last time. "A mitochondriac to the bitter end" I thought to myself. Feeling fully recharged now I return to the tundras, the land of Ice and snow, where I will stay frosty until the spring sun returns and wakes me from my sleepy hibernation.
I've been down to the Yucatan 3 times now. At first I was a bit afraid of traveling alone but Its just another fear I have faced and overcome. Part of being a mitochondria for me has been overcoming fears and getting comfortable with being uncomfortable. For those of you on the fence about planning a trip of your own, don't hesitate it's well worth the visit. You have to experience the quantum yield of this area for yourself to really get it though. I encourage you to reach out to other members and to connect in person whether it's in the Yucatan or where ever you are in the world. Talking on the forum and facebook is nice but you really need to entangle with like-minded people in person , It's very important to have community and support this is something that is shrinking in the modern age. Remember sunshine and cold isn't the only way to build dopamine.

### Neil Taylor — 2018-03-12T17:29:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-237052>

@[Sun Disciple](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19565/) amazing trip. I'm looking to do something similar after quitting my job and life in Manchester, England. Any recommendations on how to organise? Do you have links to the places you stayed?
Cheers

### Sun Disciple — 2018-03-12T18:09:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/qauntum-vacation-journal-isla-mujeres.21002/post-237056>

> [Neil Taylor said:](/goto/post?id=237052)
>
> @[Sun Disciple](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19565/) amazing trip. I'm looking to do something similar after quitting my job and life in Manchester, England. Any recommendations on how to organise? Do you have links to the places you stayed?
> Cheers

On this trip I stayed with a forum member Shah78. He is always looking to have some company so reach out to him here with a private message. If he doesntanswer Ill connect you on facebook. Add Senor Cruz on facebook and let him know when your heading down as hes becoming quite popular theses days. Agustin Cruz Uscanga. Tell them both Paul from canada sent ya.
On my previous trip I stayed right next to all the major cenotes at the Barcelo Maya just south of PDC. Its usally quite expensive but I got a killer last minute deal. See if you can find somthing on sale close by if your looking for a resort.

# Thread: Info on 5G
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/info-on-5g.21003/>

@[taiyang](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/20771/), that’s a very generous offer! And I'd love to engage in such open minded discussion. Right now, I need to conserve energy, am going through some heavy duty symptoms (journal installment coming soon). And in any case, I think it’s more important for each of us to look at our own patterns and the ways that our beliefs limit us.. so we can engage our intuitive minds in ways that have been blunted.

To illustrate the point.. I remember years ago having a vehement argument with my boyfriend about the validity of homeopathy. He was very much in the trenches of the medical establishment. The debate was not going well.. at one point I realized that it was not the subject matter we were debating to try to come to a clearer understanding of, but we were both simply defending our own biases until we got to a very frustrating impasse. Then I was finally able to get free from that morass and say, What are we Really arguing about? Where are we each getting triggered, and what’s behind that?

In other words, there was no way to take a shortcut to what viewpoint wins the debate, without exploring the belief systems that are controlling how we view “evidence.” Not that we have to take a big detour for all this inner work first, but I think it’s an important part of the discussion.

### Sheddie — 2018-03-11T02:39:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/info-on-5g.21003/post-236904>

I have a Philology... It includes, but is not limited to, a love of Philosophy and Psychology. In high school I was assigned to debating club, which I did not enjoy. Along the path, later, I came to a place where, not being in Mathematics, I decided I would not seek to prove anything to anyone else. I don't ask others to prove their perceptions either. I may request further information. Neither, does it suit me to set out to influence another if this was not their bailiwick. (Guess what -- I would never do well in sales!) So, I concluded that anything expressed and communicated is essentially True, if not in this 2D dimension then Somewhere. If 'it' can be imagined, then it attains proof of existing, whether to one mind or many. I tend to feel if our Creator wanted us to function with a 'majority-mindedness' we shouldn't have each been designed, unlike perhaps bees or sheep, with a Mind potentially of our own. This has been of great help to me not to have the ego-need to think from judgment, or defend opinions, or presume I can influence Anyone. That's reserved for the individual's intra-personal processes to manage and organize, and play with from where they are in their development. If someone is ready, his/her teacher (influence) will arrive. And, after formal schooling, it will be andragogical learning, not pedagogical. Having a good background in social sciences theory, and some areas of interest studied in depth (I could teach college level) I also understand people have different personal 'styles' of learning, and as a result, I daresay different 'styles' of exploring their worlds. What is proof to one individual is not necessarily anything useful to another. Despite that, following the 'rules,' a formal debate does entail wins & losses. It can be an invigorating exercise when the other is equally up for it. Similarly, there are 'rules' for entering into and functioning in legal systems. There used to be ideas like a Golden Rule... For me, I am deeply satisfied to find interesting new and useful information, as in being parched and coming across a cool refreshing pool of water. As well, I am curious about others' appreciation for and use of information. Some of which is in a consensus with what I know. Then, too, I always learn a great deal that I didn't know, hadn't heard before, never thought before from others; just listening or in interactions I have certainly come to be marvelously influenced and changed. There are infinite fascinating points to perceive, and very often a single point is what barely shows of an unknown enormity beyond. There is a huge difference between education and indoctrination; often difficult to separate, but well worth the ongoing effort. Similarly, deductive-style thinkers have a lot to offer for their orientation to specifics, and details. We are all potentially capable of 'whole-brain' functioning, just as we are given to leaning more to the inductive, or deductive 'styles.' Metaphorically, the Big Picture absolutely needs those pixel-sized points. Don't many points have to come together, with light, and grace, to project full understanding?
