### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-27T14:29:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238061>

Wallace seems to think that haplotypes themselves are protective against aging (2005):

> That these adaptive mutations are clinically relevant has become apparent from studies that correlated mtDNA haplogroups with longevity and degenerative disease. In an Italian study, mtDNA haplogroup J was found to be overrepresented in centenarians ([50](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2821041/#R50), [189](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2821041/#R189)). Similarly, in an Irish centenarian study, J2 was overrepresented ([191](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2821041/#R191)), and in a Finnish study of individuals over 90 years J2, Uk, and WIX were enriched ([163](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2821041/#R163)). In Japanese centenarians a sublineage of haplogroup D was enriched ([210](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2821041/#R210), [211](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2821041/#R211)). Hence, specific mtDNA lineages from Europe and Asia are protective against the ravages of aging.
>
> Some of these same mtDNA lineages have also been found to be protective against neurodegenerative diseases. Haplogroups J and Uk are underrepresented in Parkinson disease (PD) ([225](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2821041/#R225)) and haplogroup T is underrepresented in AD patients ([32](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2821041/#R32), [224](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2821041/#R224)). The repeated association of haplogroups J1 and Uk with longevity and neuro-protection is particularly illuminating because both haplogroups encompass the same cytb mutation at np 14,798. Such convergent evolution provides strong support for the functional importance of the cytb mutations.

It's more probable that the haplotype itself is not protective against aging, but the location in which a given haplotype is placed, and perhaps the location _per definition_.

Why might location protect? Because I do not see many centenarians above the 30th latitude...

They probably don't have a lot of data on L0 - L3 (the African types) either, which might bias the results towards concluding that Italy and Japan are the most protective against aging.

Edit: "Since mtDNA haplogroups that harbor adaptive mtDNA uncoupling mutations have a reduced rate of aging and neurodegenerative disease"

This favors Eurasian populations again..

Edit again. Already found Wallace's reason for this conclusion a few pages further into the document:

> These processes are ameliorated in individuals harboring partially uncoupled mitochondria. In loosely coupled mitochondria, inefficient proton pumping and(or excessive proton leakage though the ATP synthase keeps ΔP submaximal, irrespective of the caloric availability. This keeps electrons flowing through the ETC and generating heat, diminishes mitochondrial ROS production, decreases the rate of cellular apoptosis, and thus increases longevity and inhibits the progression of the age-related diseases.

Too bad that story no longer works out, as with blue light producing ROS 24/7 in northern haplotypes...

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-27T16:05:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238066>

**@[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/),** I would look at Doug Wallace's articles. He does talk about different haplotypes. I've not yet looked into very specific types yet, to be honest.
**Another observation that might interest you.
**
In the brain gut series, Jack states that our "junk DNA" might be an evolutionary advantage to humans, because this DNA allows for greater adaptability regarding changing environments.

There might be another such mechanism (emphasis mine):

> **Mitochondrial DNA sequences of primates: tempo and mode of evolution.**
> We cloned and sequenced a segment of mitochondrialDNA from human, chimpanzee, gorilla, orangutan, and gibbon. This segment is 896 bp in length, contains the genes for three transfer RNAs and parts of two proteins, and is homologous in all 5 primates. The 5 sequences differ from one another by base substitutions at 283 positions and by a deletion of one base pair. **The sequence differences range from 9 to 19% among species, in agreement with estimates from cleavage map comparisons, thus confirming that the rate of mtDNA evolution in primates is 5 to 10 times higher than in nuclear DNA.** The most striking new finding to emerge from these comparisons is that transitions greatly outnumber transversions. Ninety-two percent of the differences among the most closely related species (human, chimpanzee, and gorilla) are transitions. For pairs of species with longer divergence times, the observed percentage of transitions falls until, in the case of comparisons between primates and non-primates, it reaches a value of 45. The time dependence is probably due to obliteration of the record of transitions by multiple substitutions at the same nucleotide site. This finding illustrates the importance of choosing closely related species for analysis of evolutionary process. The remarkable bias toward transitions in mtDNA evolution necessitates the revision of equations that correct for multiple substitutions at the same site. With revised equations, we calculated the incidence of silent and replacement substitutions in the two protein-coding genes. The silent substitution rate is 4 to 6 times higher than the replacement rate, indicating strong functional constraints at replacement sites. Moreover, the silent rate for these two genes is about 10% per million years, a value 10 times higher than the silent rate for the nuclear genes studied so far. In addition, the mean substitution rate in the three mitochondrial tRNA genes is at least 100 times higher than in nuclear tRNA genes. Finally, genealogical analysis of the sequence differences supports the view that the human lineage branched off only slightly before the gorilla and chimpanzee lineages diverged and strengthens the hypothesis that humans are more related to gorillas and chimpanzees than is the orangutan.

And another study:

> **Sequence analysis of cDNAs for the human and bovine ATP synthase beta subunit: mitochondrial DNA genes sustain seventeen times more mutations.**
> We have cloned and sequenced human and bovine cDNAs for the beta subunit of the ATP synthase (ATP-syn beta), a nuclear DNA (nDNA) encoded oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) gene. The two cDNAs were found to share 99% amino acid homology and 94% nucleotide homology. The evolutionary rate of ATPsyn beta was then compared with that of two mitochondrialDNA (mtDNA) ATP synthase genes (ATPase 6 and 8), seven other mtDNA OXPHOS genes, and a number of nuclear genes. The synonymous substitution rate for ATPsyn beta proved to be 1.9 x 10(-9) substitutions per site per year (substitutions x site-1 x year-1) (SSY). This is less than 1/2 that of the average nDNA gene, 1/12 the rate of ATPase 6 and 8, **and 1/17 the rate of the average mtDNA gene.** The synonymous and replacement substitution rates were used to calculate a new parameter, the "selective constraint ratio". This revealed that even the most variable mtDNA protein was more constrained than the average nDNA protein. Thus, the high substitution mutation rate and strong selective constraints of mammalian mtDNA proteins suggest that mtDNA mutations may result in a disproportionately large number of human hereditary diseases of OXPHOS.

So, basically, human mitochondrial DNA mutates 15-20 times as fast as nuclear DNA. In primates, that mutation rate is only 5-10 times greater in mDNA than nDNA.

BUT, because in humans, the mother selects for the best eggs, which entail high energy production, we're able to reject very bad mutations, while keeping the good ones. In this instance, the higher mutation rate might be an evolutionary advantage, because bad mutations are eliminated, while the frequency of good mutations is increased compared to primates...

Edit: Although, Wallace delivers different numbers:

> The mtDNA genes have a very high sequence evolution rate, on the order of 10–20 times that of comparable nDNA genes. This is the product of both an exceptionally high mutation rate, perhaps 100- to 1000-fold higher than nDNA genes, times an mtDNA mutant fixation rate: _E_ = µ_F_ where _E_ is the sequence evolution rate, µ the mutation rate, and _F_ the fixation rate.

I'm confusing the the difference between sequence evolution rate and mutation rate, but nevertheless, mDNA mutation rates do seem to be higher in humans than primates...

"The mammalian brain is one of the organs with the highest energy demands, and mitochondria are key determinants of its functions. This is why circadian control of deuterium in neurons is a big deal for the energy side of the equation. Cellular disorganization always manifests in diseases before death; illness comes before death in most living sequences unless we are talking acute trauma. Remember mice are nocturnal and not diurnal so I do not believe this research translates one to one to humans who are diurnal. But it is instructive on what the ECS does in mitochondria. ### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-27T18:01:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238069>

Can't stop spamming here. Found the reason why mutations are higher in mDNA:

(2017)

**Alterations of mitochondrial DNA in common diseases and disease states: aging, neurodegeneration, heart failure, diabetes, and cancer.**
It has long been considered that mitochondrial DNA disease is a rare genetic disorder causing neuromyopathy. However, alterations of mitochondrial DNA recently have been recognized to play an important role in the pathogenesis of so-called common diseases such as heart failure, diabetes, and cancer. Although some of these alterations are inherited, more and more attention is being focused on the accumulation of mitochondrial DNA mutations in somatic cells, particularly terminally differentiated cells such as cardiomyocytes and neurons that occurs with age. **Mitochondrial DNA is more vulnerable to alteration than nuclear DNA, mainly for two reasons. First, mitochondria are a major source of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS). Therefore mitochondrial DNA is under much stronger oxidative stress than is nuclear DNA. Second, mitochondria have a matrix-side negative membrane potential for oxidative phosphorylation. This membrane potential concentrates lipophilic cations inside mitochondria up to approximately 1,000-fold.** Unfortunately, some therapeutic reagents are lipophilic cations, and such exogenously added chemicals are prone to damage mitochondria. AZT, an anti-HIV drug, causes mitochondrial myopathy as a side effect, which is a typical example of how chemotherapeutics adversely affect metabolism of mitochondrial DNA. In this review, we focus on ROS and chemical damage of mitochondrial DNA in common diseases.

Emphasis mine again...

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238071>

Endocannabinoid release from neurons has been shown to cause presynaptic inhibition of transmitter release at many different sites in the body. So this gives you an idea how the body deals with energy and information based on incident light rays. Why? CB1 receptor and endogenous EC are tied to POMC function from the retina and hypothalamus. This tells We know that hypothalamic proopiomelanocortin (POMC) neurons release endocannabinoids continuously under basal conditions in sunlight. Endocannabinoids are produced in and released from many types of neurons, generally in response to specific stimuli, and act via Gi/o-coupled receptors which are all linked to the circadian mechanism recently given the 2017 Nobel Prize for.

Endogenously released cannabinoids appear to be quantized to the light stimulus and exogenously applied CB1 receptor agonists can have markedly different effects on synaptic inputs that the ones the brain makes. The reason should be obvious.......plants and drugs do not have the same quantum signal as morning sunlight via the retina. Furthermore, research has suggested a novel form of endocannabinoid- mediated retrograde inhibition, whereby the regulation of a subset of inputs requires either the removal of tonic presynaptic inhibition caused by endocannabinoids or the engagement of a mechanism that actively inhibits endocannabinoid production which appears to be light-mediated. This is why weed and CBD oil can lead to uneven results. Why do these things work in some people with crappy mitochondrial function? Simple.......they slow ECT in bad engines. Why are they bad to use long term? Because they do not provide the signal for mitophagy to get rid of the bad engines.

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-28T09:07:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238096>

**To me, this is one of the keys to the kingdom:
**

> Mitophagy contributes to mitochondrial quality control not only by removing damaged mitochondria but also by promoting biosynthesis of new mitochondria. It has been demonstrated that there is crosstalk between the mitophagy pathway and mitochondrial biosynthetic pathway ([10](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5498140/#b10-bmb-50-299)). Specifically, Plaikaras _et al_. established that mitophagy and mitochondrial biosynthesis are interfaced with each other to maintain mitochondrial homeostasis using a _C. elegans_ model ([14](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5498140/#b14-bmb-50-299)).

There's no need to take PQQ, and other supplements, if your sleep is sub-par. **Sleep and sunlight remain king and queen.
**
When you're taking supplements for mitochondrial biogenesis you're probably missing the point. Mitochondrial biogenesis and mitophagy are interrelated.

Edit: quoted from: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5498140/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5498140/)

Here's the study they quote (emphasis mine):

> **Coordination of mitophagy and mitochondrial biogenesis during ageing in C. elegans.**
> Impaired mitochondrial maintenance in disparate cell types is a shared hallmark of many human pathologies and ageing. How mitochondrial biogenesis coordinates with the removal of damaged or superfluous mitochondria to maintain cellular homeostasis is not well understood. **Here we show that mitophagy, a selective type of autophagy targeting mitochondria for degradation, interfaces with mitochondrial biogenesis to regulate mitochondrial content and longevity in Caenorhabditis elegans.** We find that DCT-1 is a key mediator of mitophagy and longevity assurance under conditions of stress in C. elegans. Impairment of mitophagy compromises stress resistance and triggers mitochondrial retrograde signalling through the SKN-1 transcription factor that regulates both mitochondrial biogenesis genes and mitophagy by enhancing DCT-1 expression. Our findings reveal a homeostatic feedback loop that integrates metabolic signals to coordinate the biogenesis and turnover of mitochondria. **Uncoupling of these two processes during ageing contributes to overproliferation of damaged mitochondria and decline of cellular function.**

**To give a metaphor: biogenesis and mitophagy are two sides of the same coin. Erase the mitophagy side of the coin, and the coin loses it's entire value - in the same way a euro is worthless when one side is blank.
**

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-28T09:44:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238098>

**Next step in the argument: the inability for mitophagy to occur is closely tied to inflammatory processes in the body:
**

> **Mitophagy and the release of inflammatory cytokines.**
> Mitophagy is a selective form of autophagy in which damaged or dysfunctional mitochondria are specifically targeted by autophagosomes for lysosomal degradation. **Studies have demonstrated that loss of autophagy/mitophagy can lead to a build-up of cytosolic reactive oxygen species and mitochondrial DNA, which can, in turn, activate immune signalling pathways that ultimately lead to the releases of inflammatory cytokines, including IL-1α, IL-1β, IL-18 and macrophage migration inhibitory factor (MIF).** Moreover, release of these cytokines can subsequently promote the release of others, including IL-23 and IL-17. Thus, as well as being essential for normal cell homeostasis and mitochondrial health, mitophagy may represent an important regulatory mechanism controlling inflammatory responses in immune cells. This review discusses our current understanding of the mechanisms through which mitophagy regulates inflammatory cytokine release.

What else do we know? Inflammatory processes underlie almost every modern disease...

Link: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/29107116](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/29107116)

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-28T10:11:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238100>

> **The Mitochondrial Basis of Aging.
> A decline in mitochondrial quality and activity has been associated with normal aging and correlated with the development of a wide range of age-related diseases. Here, we review the evidence that a decline in mitochondria function contributes to aging.** In particular, we discuss how mitochondria contribute to specific aspects of the aging process, including cellular senescence, chronic inflammation, and the age-dependent decline in stem cell activity. Signaling pathways regulating the mitochondrial unfolded protein response and mitophagy are also reviewed, with particular emphasis placed on how these pathways might, in turn, regulate longevity. Taken together, these observations suggest that mitochondria influence or regulate a number of key aspects of aging and suggest that strategies directed at improving mitochondrial quality and function might have far-reaching beneficial effects.

Emphasis mine. Link: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/26942670/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/26942670/)

**I think these researchers have it backwards. The shocker here: is disease merely a symptom of aging?**

So let me step on the gas for you a bit:

We are designed to eat in the sunlight in food form because all foods link to photosynthesis; therefore all food can really be at its core is light information from the sun codified in some atomic way that nutrition researcher are yet to figure out. Our mitochondria decipher the energy and information in every electron, and proton in foods. Why do I believe that? Let us start with hydrogen the first atom on the periodic chart. Foods contain two forms of hydrogen, protium, and deuterium. Life forms in plants and animals favor light hydrogen over deuterium. In fact, your mitochondria have 3 dehydrogenases that strip hydrogen from foods to be used in energy and information building in cells. Many people ask me if sunburn was a nutrient deficiency. I am going to ask you to turn around your question and not focus on the tissue being irradiated, but the sun's power to make nutrients.

I want you to think about how sunlight varies as the nutrient for you. The light from the sun is a **mosaic.** You could think about it as a nutrient if you are focused on food but I choose not to. I focus on the biophysics of light. Anyone who has looked at the cover of the old Pink Floyd album cover where a crystal glass prism separates visible light into 7 colors knows this is a true statement. SO every ray of light contains a specific nutrient. All the colors do very specific things that control the 100,000 biochemicals that work per second inside of you. For example within IR light 4 frequencies control the 4 red light chromophore proteins in cytochrome c oxidase of every mitochondria on EARTH.

What you do not realize is that how the mosaic is used is sensitive and specific in living systems on Earth. So sensitive we call the process that describes it 'quantized.' Each photon from the sun has a specific and precise job. If you do anything to block the stimulus you ruin the specificity in the system. Plants use one liquid crystal to do it and animals use another crystal that changes the light to a DC electric current for cells to improve mitochondrial function via autophagy and apoptosis. The key to the crystal called mitochondria, is the mosaic it creates or reemits to the surrounding in cells to control the things in biochemistry via resonance. So the sun has a compound pharmacy of nutrients for us. What has happened since 1874 when the light bulb showed up and in 1893 when Tesla made electricity with an AC current?

There is a “revolution on the surface of the earth” called technology that is causing a new evolution in your mitochondria. Reality manifests from alien light frequencies colliding with your animal crystals to make a mosaic our cells have never experienced before. This provides you with another possible explanation for why observations don’t match predictions is that humans aren’t very observant about the mosaic of light around us and we 've changed it since 1874. Beautiful mosaics are made of broken or torn material. That is what your cells do. They partition the nutrients in the light. Your ability to see is made from broken down colors in light to bring you a unique reality. In every sound, the hidden silence sleeps. Life is a series of adversities: It's a strong wind tearing away from us all but the things that cannot be torn and ripped so that we see ourselves as we are now. Food is an electromagnetic barcode mosaic of seasonal power densities from the sun. We're all mosaics of many things design to fall apart under the forces of nature so we do not come fully come undone. Pieces of light, love, history, stars; cemented together with love, music, and ideas.

The key is transforming the environmental energies/information to something different and new.

We are all made up of broken pieces of light buried in the subatomic particles of food and our mitochondria are the glue that makes life work..........that is heteroplasmy in a nutshell. Once you understand how heteroplasmy occurs you understand quickly why modern humans are getting ill from technology. We don't suffer from a food nutrient problem with respect to sunburn........we do not realize that sunburns come from when we do not get enough red light from the sun and we overdose on UV-A and UV B light because we spend too much time inside on technology's blue light screens become nutrient deficient of red light. As a result, people get sunburns and falsely believe the sun is toxic. It is a horrible meme.

People think they need a balanced diet of food. What they really need is a balanced diet of light. We not only "eat" sunlight--we can actually digest it and break it into its component frequencies to power specific neural processes using light mosaics. ### Jack Kruse — 2018-03-28T18:58:23+0300

You, Lane, Mae Wan-Ho, Wallace, Einstein, critics/skeptics, and ex-members who did not stick around to hear me out, believe as Eddington did, that nothing breaks the second law of thermodynamics. This is why CICO is so hard to shake.......it is why we have food over light and why we have big pharma.

Examine why.

Ludwig Boltzmann showed that energy disperses, and entropy increases, as a simple matter of statistics: There are many more ways for energy to be spread among the particles in a system than concentrated in a few, so as particles move around and interact, they naturally tend toward states in which their energy is increasingly shared. Maxwell described a thought experiment in which an enlightened being — later called Maxwell’s demon — uses its knowledge to lower entropy and violate the second law. The demon knows the positions and velocities of every molecule in a container of gas. By partitioning the container and opening and closing a small door between the two chambers, the demon lets only fast-moving molecules enter one side while allowing only slow molecules to go the other way. The demon’s actions divide the gas into hot and cold, concentrating its energy and lowering its overall entropy. The once useless gas can now be put to work.

Today, quantum thermodynamics has separated information and energy and this has changed the entire game for mitochondriacs. Most of the people mentioned above have no idea why. This is part of their Dunning Kruger effect and why I am so confident and smirk when I hear their words and listen to their current understanding. Information is now a physical thing. Information maybe MORE IMPORTANT than energy flux in UNDERSTANDING how heteroplasmy really works in a cell. The more information you have, the more physiologic work a mitochondrion can extract. This is why Wallace has made the observation that oscillations and alignment of fat around mitochondria seem to occur in mitochondria when energy flux from beta-oxidation is fully operational. He has never looked at the urea cycle function during this observation. He should consider what he knows about mTOR and the Warburg shift. So you can see here, he is missing a big part of the story. Could the missing part be how information is transferred? What happens when "Kreb's bicycle" is not yoked properly? Do you even know what Kreb's bicycle is? If the answer is no.........sit back and relax. I got a ton of bombs coming for you. If you chose to remain outside the tribe, my tribe of misfits will run you over. When you marry energy and information you become a black swan. Now with the insights from information theory, we wouldn’t say entropy is a property of a system, but a property of an observer who describes a system.

That has huge implications for the wisdom built into the organization of nature.

Let's say we assume Nick Lane was correct on the symbiosis of Archaea and Bacteria. I think the eventual creation of the mitochondrion, and the increasing complexity of life, is a direct contradiction of the Second law of Thermodynamics.

F.W.J. Schelling, philosopher, who is very influential on my philosophical thinking, already posited an "orderly" force of life in the late 1700's and early 1800's. Modern physics thinks his "naturphilosophie" is bullshit. I think it's empirical observations are bullshit too, but the underlying philosophical principles are extremely important. Schelling posits the existence of two primordial forces, which are not in an absolute equilibrium, but a relative equilibrium. The "dark" force is gravity, while the other force is light. These forces are metaphysical principles, that are imagined as the originator of actual physical and chemical forces that can be empircally observed.

Over time, the force of light has gained more predominance, which he deduces from the emergence of the plant kingdom, animal kingdom, and finally humanity. What's really intersting in Schelling's work, is that he already accepts the idea of evolution before Darwin--but does not know it's mechanism. What's more intersting, however, is that Schelling already accepts that _some parts_ of the universe can move towards order (and therefore, from a 21st perspective, negate the Second Law).

I think you cannot even understand life without breaking the second law. However, we should also be careful not to read any teleology into my argument above. The orderly processes of life can fail. One meteor, and everything may be over. One red button, and the same happens...

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-29T09:34:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238195>

**I was thinking Jack, how do we prove the thesis that light carries both information and energy to the public, and that light information is informed in the foods we eat?
**
The energy part of the equation is simpler to argue for, at this point in time. The light = information thesis might be correct, but its acceptance is harder for many people. To be honest, it was harder for me accept as well, until I started digging into this proposition...

**Find my full argument below:**

To me, your physics paradigm in medicine - at this point - looks like a quarter completed puzzle. About 25% of the puzzle pieces are used at the current moment. These 25% of puzzle pieces, however, do allow you to clearly see the pattern of the whole. The whole, if you look at all the puzzle pieces together, is E = MC2.

How can you clearly see a pattern? Well, imagine a puzzle of a person. With just 25% of the pieces, you can clearly see that the puzzle will end up being a person. But you cannot yet see all the details.

Because only a quarter of the puzzle is filled, the individual pieces denote all the biochemical evidence we have right now. It's problematic that there are a lot of holes between individual pieces--the biochemistry is not fully linked at this moment. The holes between the pieces denote the absence of experimental proof for specific propositions / predictions of the physics paradigm in medicine.

However, if you look at the image of the whole - the picture displayed by the 25% of puzzle pieces together, you nevertheless see a pattern between the pieces. The biochemical connections _should be there_. Your mind can fill up the gaps, but only through the imagination, not through linear connections (yet).

Moreover, we do have more puzzle pieces in place now, compared to the past. A few years ago, only 24% of the puzzle's pieces were filled. But right now, for example, a lot of experimental evidence has been coming out on the LeptinRx (members, look at the forum there, to see what I posted there), that confirm the LeptinRx on meal-timing and circadian biology. Some pieces now full fit together. The puzzle is filling out. Because the puzzle is filling out, the whole is also better visible.

So, where do problems emerge in the puzzle? At two specific places.

Let's consider the first problem: right now, there's no direct experimental evidence that your thesis of eating a banana at December 21st at the 55th latitude is harmful. The absence of direct evidence does not mean that your thesis is not true--it only means that more confirmation is needed (just like the LeptinRx in the past). We need all puzzle pieces to be in place, to definitively confirm the biochemical connection.

(Karl Popper would be proud of you - because you posited a daring theory, that is easily falsifiable. The LeptinRx studies that have been coming out, do show that we cannot ignore your physics paradigm anymore. You cannot ignore the puzzle from emerging. The image becomes clearer, and harder to ignore. Up until now, your theory has not been falsified, and is standing tall.)

**So, I decided to investigate the thesis that light is information which affect circadian biology--one missing puzzle piece that has not yet been _fully_ experimentally confirmed.
**
To return to my analogy here, we had two puzzle pieces in place, which are:

1) Food is not only energy (electrons protons), but also informed / programmed by the light information inside it (in Platonic language, food is literally "In-Formed").
4) The light information in your food affects your circadian biology.

If you read correctly, we only have puzzle pieces 1 and 4 at this moment. The puzzle pieces between 1 and 4 - which are 2 and 3 - are currently not yet in place.

**I'm drilling down because I do demand proof at both sides of the equation: the physics side, and the biochemical side of the coin.
**
Why? This leads to the second problem...

We might not be able to rely _exclusively_ on physics experiments to do medicine. Physics is revised all the time too - just take a look at how we just questioned the Second Law. I also know that you question other main dogmas of modern physics, due to your adherence to the electric universe theory.

The fact that we do not have a final physics theory - and I think we never will have a final physics theory - makes a mere reliance on physics not the best strategy, IMO. If we can have proof at both sides of the coin (physics and biochemistry), then your argument in favor of your physics paradigm can be made stronger.

I think I somewhat differ from you in this regard--I'm not willing to accept outcomes on _just a physics basis_, if there's currently no biochemical experimental outcome that gives extra credentials to the physics experiment (I am, of course, fully aware with the problems you have with RCTs).

Why do I not accept physics blindly?

Because the physics perspective might change over time. History of science and technology demonstrates that this physics perspective does indeed change. We had a change from an Aristotelean worldview, to a Newtonian worldview. And a change from a Newtonian worldview, to an Einsteinian worldview. Between these paradigm shifts, there were many smaller changes as well. It's lunacy to think that in 2300, our conception of physics is not totally different than today. To assume that E=MC2 is the final proposition that explains everything, is delusional IMO.

Why does a changing physics perspective matter? Because a paradigm shift in physics - to me - denotes that the puzzle is bigger than we thought it was. Einstein demonstrated that the whole is different than Newtonian physics thought it was. Einsteinian physics can also explain more than Newtonian physics (hence, a bigger puzzle). This then, denotes the second problem - the whole image might be different than we thought it was.

Let's return to the biochemistry part of the equation: the link between the puzzle pieces.

**Deciding to dig deeper, I looked whether I could fill in the blanks of puzzle pieces 2 and 3...
**
What did I find?

The full text below is AMAZING ([https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5389023/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5389023/))

(emphasis mine)

**Circadian clocks, diets and aging (2017):**

Quote:

Glucose is a well-documented circadian clock regulator. In fibroblasts exposed to high glucose concentration the expression of _Per1_ and _Per2_ genes is down regulated \[[45](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5389023/#ref045)\]. In cell culture experiments, the phase and period of the circadian rhythms in expression of clock genes such as _Per2_ depends on glucose concentration in the media. **_In vivo_ glucose affects both central and peripheral clocks. Glucose infusion during the daytime strongly induces expression of _Per2_ in the SCN and suppresses _Per2_expression in the liver \[[46](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5389023/#ref046)\];** thus, the effects of glucose are also tissue-specific. **Glucose availability attenuates photic phase response \[[47](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5389023/#ref047)\].** Insulin and glucose mutually regulate levels of each other, and some of the _in vivo_ effects of glucose might be the effects of insulin and _vice versa_; however, cell culture data strongly argue that glucose and insulin have independent effects on the clocks. Several intracellular systems may be involved in the glucose sensing by the clocks. Glucose availability indirectly regulates adenosine monophosphate-dependent protein kinase (AMPK), which phosphorylates and controls stability of the CRY proteins \[[48](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5389023/#ref048)\]. Transforming growth factor B-inducible early gene (TIEG1) and Vitamin D3 upregulated protein (VDUP1) are glucose responsive immediate early genes and upregulation of TIEG1 and VDUP1 contributes to glucose-dependent clock gene expression \[[45](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5389023/#ref045)\]. Glucose may also regulate cellular clocks through hexosamine/O-GLcNAc pathway; O-GlcNAcylation of BMAL1 and CLOCK inhibits their ubiquitination and degradation regulating the amplitude and phase of clock oscillation \[[49](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5389023/#ref049)\].​

Now, this is as close to a smoking gun as it gets. Why? Because you can combine the study above with the following evidence on plant physiology and light (emphasis mine again):

**Solar ultraviolet radiation is necessary to enhance grapevine fruit ripening transcriptional and phenolic responses.**
**BACKGROUND:**
Ultraviolet (UV) radiation modulates secondary metabolism in the skin of Vitis vinifera L. berries, which affects the final composition of both grapes and wines. The expression of several phenylpropanoid biosynthesis-related genes is regulated by UV radiation in grape berries. However, the complete portion of transcriptome and ripening processes influenced by solar UV radiation in grapes remains unknown.

**RESULTS:**
Whole genome arrays were used to identify the berry skin transcriptome modulated by the UV radiation received naturally in a mid-altitude Tempranillo vineyard. UV radiation-blocking and transmitting filters were used to generate the experimental conditions. **The expression of 121 genes was significantly altered by solar UV radiation.** Functional enrichment analysis of altered transcripts mainly pointed out that secondary metabolism-related transcripts were induced by UV radiation including VvFLS1, VvGT5 and VvGT6 flavonol biosynthetic genes and monoterpenoid biosynthetic genes. Berry skin phenolic composition was also analysed to search for correlation with gene expression changes and UV-increased flavonols accumulation was the most evident impact. Among regulatory genes, novel UV radiation-responsive transcription factors including VvMYB24 and three bHLH, together with known grapevine UV-responsive genes such as VvMYBF1, were identified. A transcriptomic meta-analysis revealed that genes up-regulated by UV radiation in the berry skin were also enriched in homologs of Arabidopsis UVR8 UV-B photoreceptor-dependent UV-B -responsive genes. Indeed, a search of the grapevinereference genomic sequence identified UV-B signalling pathway homologs and among them, VvHY5-1, VvHY5-2 and VvRUP were up-regulated by UV radiation in the berry skin.

**CONCLUSIONS:**
Results suggest that the UV-B radiation-specific signalling pathway is activated in the skin of grapes grown at mid-altitudes. The biosynthesis and accumulation of secondary metabolites, which are appreciated in winemaking and potentially confer cross-tolerance, were almost specifically triggered. This draws attention to viticultural practices that increase solar UV radiation on vineyards as they may improve grape features.
​

Link: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Solar ultraviolet radiation is necessary to enhance grapevine fruit ripening transcriptional and phenolic responses](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Solar+ultraviolet+radiation+is+necessary+to+enhance+grapevine+fruit+ripening+transcriptional+and+phenolic+responses)

And another one (emphasis mine again):

**Correlation between Strawberry (_Fragaria ananassa_ Duch.) Productivity and Photosynthesis-Related Parameters under Various Growth Conditions**
In the present study, we investigated changes in chlorophyll fluorescence, photosynthetic parameters and fruit yields, as well as fruit phytochemical accumulation of strawberry (_Fragaria ananassa_ Duch.) that had been cultivated in a greenhouse under different combinations of light intensity and temperature. In plants grown with low light (LL) photosystem II chlorophyll fluorescence was found to increase as compared with those grown under high light (HL). When strawberry plants were grown with temperature higher than 5°C in addition to LL, they showed decrease in non-photochemical quenching (NPQ), photochemical quenching (qP), as well as chlorophyll fluorescence decrease ratio (RFd) when compared with other combinations of light and temperature. Moreover, fruit yield of strawberry was closely correlated with chlorophyll fluorescence-related parameters such as NPQ, qP, and RFd, but not with the maximum efficiency of PS II (Fv/Fm). Although plant groups grown under different combinations of light and temperature showed almost comparable levels of photosynthesis rates (Pr) when irradiated with low-intensity light, they displayed clear differences when measured with higher irradiances. Plants grown under HL with temperature above 10°C showed the highest Pr, in contrast to the plants grown under LL with temperature above 5°C. When the stomatal conductance and the transpiration rate were measured, plants of each treatment showed clear differences even when analyzed with lower irradiances. **We also found that fruit production during winter season was more strongly influenced by growth temperature than light intensity. We suggest that fruit productivity of strawberry is closely associated with chlorophyll fluorescence and photosynthesis-related parameters during cultivation under different regimes of temperature and light.**​

See: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5080357/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5080357/)

The combination of the studies above gets us closer to puzzle piece number 3. We still need number 4? What would you need to fill out all the missing pieces? To me, you would need the effect size of circadian mismatches created by food, the specific fructose contents of fruits that are created by UV and blue light exposure (summertime) in foods, human studies on fructose consumption and CLOCK genes, _etcetera_.

So, I would like experimental biochemical evidence of how big the effect size is of eating a banana at Dec 21st at the 55th latitude.

**Why do I keep hammering on the biochemical side of the equation? Because I think that side of the coin is needed to convince the public..**.

We cannot just point to the whole, and expect the public to fill in all the missing pieces.

Remember, most of the public has low dopamine levels. That's a sad fact of life. Without dopamine, you can only think linearly. With low dopamine, you can only connect two puzzle pieces at a time. With low dopamine, you cannot connect 1 puzzle piece with another piece 5 steps away...

But by telling people about all the individual puzzle pieces, and telling them about how all pieces connect, they can increase their dopamine levels. And with higher dopamine levels, they begin to see the whole, and the interconnection of the whole and all the parts.

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-30T11:57:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238307>

You assume I haven't thought long and hard about this Mayuri. But I have.

**First of all**, I do not think you need to remove all PubMed studies from the repertoire. You just need to see them in context. Some PubMed studies are great, some are not so great. I don't hear any criticism when I post studies that favor Jack's thesis. An example is the study I posted in the LeptinRx forum:

> **Later circadian timing of food intake is associated with increased body fat.**
> Background: Weight gain and obesity have reached alarming levels. Eating at a later clock hour is a newly described risk factor for adverse metabolic health; yet, how eating at a later circadian time influences body composition is unknown. Using clock hour to document eating times may be misleading owing to individual differences in circadian timing relative to clock hour.Objective: This study examined the relations between the timing of food consumption relative to clock hour and endogenous circadian time, content of food intake, and body composition.Design: We enrolled 110 participants, aged 18-22 y, in a 30-d cross-sectional study to document sleep and circadian behaviors within their regular daily routines. **We used a time-stamped-picture mobile phone application to record all food intake across 7 consecutive days during a participant's regular daily routines and assessed their body composition and timing of melatonin release during an in-laboratory assessment.Results: Nonlean individuals (high body fat) consumed most of their calories 1.1 h closer to melatonin onset, which heralds the beginning of the biological night, than did lean individuals (low body fat) (log-rank P = 0.009).** In contrast, there were no differences between lean and nonlean individuals in the clock hour of food consumption (P = 0.72). Multiple regression analysis showed that the timing of food intake relative to melatonin onset was significantly associated with the percentage of body fat and body mass index (both P < 0.05) while controlling for sex, whereas no relations were found between the clock hour of food intake, caloric amount, meal macronutrient composition, activity or exercise level, or sleep duration and either of these body composition measures (all P > 0.72).**Conclusions: These results provide evidence that the consumption of food during the circadian evening and/or night, independent of more traditional risk factors such as amount or content of food intake and activity level, plays an important role in body composition.**
>
> **Emphasis mine.**

Have these studies fully controlled for the light that these people use at their home? No. But still, there is no criticism on me posting _that specific study_.

**Secondly**, I'm painfully aware of the methodological problems of doing medical studies under artificial blue light in hospital wards. Let's call that thesis the "Jack Kruse artificial light thesis A regarding RCT's".

I can assure you, that the problems with RCT's run far deeper than this. Just from the top of my head, I can give you a few theses that are similar to Jack's:

a) A redox thesis. The general population has lower redox levels than ancestral human beings. What implications does that difference in redox have? For example, if you do an RCT on 5-day water fasting, the outcomes will be different with low redox or high redox populations. If you take a modern population living in a big city, _both_ the control group and intervention group will already be skewed towards a different outcome than a population that has a high redox - because almost all modern city-dwelling populations have low redox. Conclusion: almost any outcome of an RCT carried out today, cannot be generalized towards high-redox people on this forum.

b) A generalization thesis. Statistical tests take averages over a control group, and an intervention group. An ANOVA test, or a logistical regression test, that look for statistically significant differences between a control or intervention group, do not take _any_ n=1 perspective into account. Statistic significances are only calculated on the basis of _groups_ of people, instead of _individuals._ For example, if testing zinc supplements in both an intervention group and control group, the outcome might entail a reduction in heart disease at a statistically significant level in favor of zinc supplements (intervention group). But _some_ people within that intervention group might have increased their chances of heart disease - they might have been eating a lot of oysters, and overdosed on their zinc levels with the supplement. The people for whom the zinc supplements increase their risk for heart disease have their n=1 cancelled out by the group average (which points at a different direction). Conclusion: statistically significant RCT outcomes pertaining to a populations does not mean a statistically outcome for your individual n=1.

c) A "Wallace thesis". If our mitochondria are coupled differently, in an L or H haplotype, then we can no longer just generalize the outcome of studies towards a general population of human beings. Studies on CT on an L haplotype will have different outcomes than CT studies on a H haplotype. Conclusions: haplotypes need to be corrected for in many RCTs.

d) A temperature thesis. Biochemistry works differently in cold and warm environments. RCTs carried out in warm environments, cannot be generalized towards cold environments, and _vice versa_. (Jack might have already explicitly stated this thesis himself, but I have not seen it (but, remember, I've not read all his work either!)). Conclusion: modern RCTs do not control for temperature, in the same way they do not control for light, which makes RCTs much less useful.

e) A biochemistry thesis. As far as I remember, you, and all people out here, are still measuring health outcomes through your biochemistry. Maybe you're measuring your blood pressure, maybe your fasting glucose levels, your DHEA and cortisol in a lab test, your deep sleep levels with an Oura ring, _etcetera_. As far as I'm concerned, you cannot fully leave the biochemical perspective aside as of this moment, because we do not generally measure health from purely a physics perspective (although, Dr. Kruse has given indications that one can do this with MRI scans--my point is, however, that health is not generally measured this way right now).

With your post, you just assumed that I wasn't aware of Jack's argument on how blue light skews outcomes of medical RCT studies. _I am, at a painfully deep level._

How am I aware of these methodological issues with RCTs? Because next to completing a Master's in Philosophy, I also completed a Masters in Clinical Health Sciences, and Philosophy of Science and Technology. All at the same time.

You say:

> With all due respect to you there is not a single study that you could produce to negate this new ground breaking work because in truth the studies do not exist.

I'm not trying to _negate_ Jack. Instead, I'm going through his entire argument, and look for myself. I'm not taking anything on faith. I'm trying to make sense of the data myself. That's why I [posted the studies I did in the CT forum](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/ct-4-confimed-cold-origin-of-life-2017.20990/), when doing my research into that subject. These studies 2017 studies confirmed Jack's thesis on cold--whihc demonstrates that I was not trying to _negate_ Jack, as you claim.

I'm just making sure my own argument is rigorous, which any scientist should do.

Which leads to my third point:

**Thirdly,** I do not follow anyone. I do not follow Dave Asprey. I do not follow Alexander Wunsch. I do not follow Nick Lane. I do not follow Doug Wallace. I do not follow Jack Kruse. I alone, am fully responsible for the conclusions I arrive at, and the logic by which I arrive at them. Blind obedience, and not checking all the facts yourself, is the antithesis what science is all about for me.

So finally, I don't think there's anything wrong with posting PubMed studies _per se_. I just think you need to see these studies in context. I assure you, I'm doing my job to see these studies in context, and think critically _myself_.

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-30T12:16:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238309>

Maybe one more RCT problem to top it off:

f) "The systematic review isolation problem" In medicine, RCTs, and a collection of RCTs into a systematic review, are seen as the gold standard of medicine. The problem of this view is that they lead to the false assumption that the human body - and therapies placed upon the human body - can be understood by isolation. For example, a physical therapist might compare two different types of movement (two interventions), and no movement (control), for arthritis, and accumulate 10 different RCTs into the "gold standard" of a systematic review. According to medicine, that systematic review is fully valid, and consists in a gold standard. The problem of that systematic review, however, is that there is no incentive to take _all variables_ that might influence arthritis into account. The physical therapist may call his systematic review a gold standard, without taking sunlight, or red light therapy, or fish-consumed DHA into account _at all,_ even though these therapies might be just as effective. A true systematic review, to me, should take every physical and biochemical variable that could influence a pathology - or health variable - into account. Conclusion: the current conception of systematic reviews lead to a false sense of security in modern medicine's conclusions.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-03-30T14:06:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238317>

[Normalization of deviance can occur in all paradigms, corporations, organizations, even scientific groups.](http://www.hmmrmedia.com/2018/03/the-normalization-of-deviance/#) This is perhaps how bad science begins. Once one study with bad methodology gets accepted by PEER review in a major journal it is a signal to others they can get away with the same thing to get their work in that journal. One person starts duping the editorial board, and eventually more people become comfortable with the idea, seeing it as normal. If it is allowed to persist it can drive negative lifestyle behaviors downstream as collateral damage of disease management ideas. then it gets slapped with a tag called Evidence-based medicine.

Clearly, normalization of deviance is something that we want to avoid in science, as it can lead to the misreading of signals. We lose focus of what matters and what does not.

Guarding against this normalization requires deep analysis. [Often, you need to consider whether you were successful because of what you did, or in spite of what you did](http://www.hmmrmedia.com/2018/03/the-normalization-of-deviance/#). Culture requires care because it erodes medical science and allows us to make horrendous recommendations for patients. See statin RCT's or any discussion of dietary guidelines as examples.

**_For these reasons, most published medical literature is actually false and no one realizes it._** They believe when I say this, it is hyperbole until I show them the data of why I might be correct (below). Then the mouth drops open. It is time for a complete system overhaul to REALLY help patients and clinicians. Clinicians are not even aware of how the RCT system is a detriment to the scientific method because of what we have learned from Dr. Doug Wallace’s work on mitochondrial biology. Everything is an N=1 game. RCT = studies in mediocrity because they look for average results of a mixed sample of haplotypes and SNP’s. And when you consider that haplotypes and SNP's are dynamic forces under light frequencies then all hell breaks loose in a wise mind. It is a joke when you see it for the first time.

People create their worlds with the tools they have directly at hand. Faulty tools produce faulty results. Repeated use of the same faulty tools in nutrition and medical research has produced decades of the same faulty results. It is in this manner that those who fail to learn from the past doom themselves to repeat it. It’s partly fate. It’s partly inability. It’s partly… unwillingness to learn? Refusal to learn? Motivated refusal to learn? It defines the how the Dunning Kruger effect was born in medicine. The tools we are using directly at hand in a medical context are now unrelated to science. It is really pseudoscientific. [http://journals.plos.org/plosmedicine/article?id=10.1371/journal.pmed.0020124](http://journals.plos.org/plosmedicine/article?id=10.1371/journal.pmed.0020124)

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

> Edit: the symbolism is thick. You need to be able to "see" purple and red for the message to appear in the first place...

For those who are wondering why I posted this: Kruser's Razor is a proposed variation of "Occam's Razor" - perhaps its twin brother. Why?

**Occam's Razor** entails that the explanation - or answer to a problem - that has the fewest assumptions, is to be preferred over explanations or answers that make more assumptions.

**Kruser's Razor** - as the joke goes - is somewhat the same, but located at the level of the hierarchy of sciences. More fundamental sciences, such as physics, naturally have to make fewer assumptions than less fundamental sciences, such as biochemistry. Hence, physics may more adequately explain medical phenomena than biochemistry, and a physical explanation trumps a biochemical one if both are available.

Which shows, again, that I'm not firmly located in the biochemical paradigm...

And of course, jokes can be dead-serious...

Here are 2 statements to consider:

1) Blue (artificial) light, especially at night, causes cancer.

2) nnEMF (non-native electromagnetic fields - think cell phones, x-box, tablets, Wi-Fi routers, cell towers, smart meters, smart homes, Apple watches, Fitbit, Bluetooth or anything that operates on wireless technology) causes cancer.

We've known from recent research these 2 things to be true. This is not new information. For anyone interested, I highly recommend you read the book “light’s out” by TS Wiley. Makes a strong case, backed by DECADES of studies done at the NIH, for why artificial light at night causes obesity and many diseases including cancer.

But here is the connection that you must make to help yourself understand it fully:

What is the hallmark trait of cancer in cell biology? - dysfunctional cells multiplying and growing out of control in the body.

Here’s the rub: we ALL have cancer cells growing in our bodies. All of us. What makes it that one person doesn’t develop deadly cancers while others do?

One word Rx is called "apoptosis." This describes the body’s innate ability to recognize a cell as dysfunctional and potentially cancerous, and “orders” the cell to shut down and die.

If apoptosis is working properly for you, you will not develop cancer, because your body is able to recognize those dysfunctional cells and neutralize them right away by using the recycling program called autophagy over apoptosis. Problem solved. What controls Autophagy? AM sunlight. How? The creation of melatonin in the retina. You might want to go back and re-watch my Vermont 2017 video on that biophysical process.

Here’s the key thing about melatonin you never hear except me: MELATONIN, the hormone that our pineal gland releases from aromatic amines in our retina and gut, in the absence of LIGHT, is what regulates apoptosis/autophagy. Remember AM light creates melatonin in the eye but the biophysical action does not occur until it is dark. In the daytime, there are more electric fields from sunlight so the pineal knows not to release it. At night time there should be no electric fields from light so the magnetic flux increases because at night the geomagnetic flux of Earth is more dominant. This is the signal the pineal works with at night. Let me make this plain as day for you (even if it is a crude description of a very complex biological process):

No melatonin creation or proper release = no apoptosis.

No apoptosis = dysfunctional cells growing out of control = CANCER.

No apoptosis means electron chain transport is uber functional going from the input of electrons at cytochrome 1 or 2 to oxygen. Cells use this process to become immortal.

Artificial light at night and nnEMF BOTH individually signal to your pineal gland that it is daytime because light has both an electric and magnetic field. You pineal needs the electric field to vanish to operate. In response, your pineal gland will dramatically reduce melatonin synthesis and output and this will disable autophagy. This favors a rise in bad/poorly function mitochondria called heteroplasmy. If this sticks around long enough, autophagy ceases to work and mitochondria undergo a Warburg shift to make sure apoptosis can never happen. This occurs because apoptosis supports energy flux. Autophagy supports information flux in mitochondria. This points out that quantum information transfer is more important than energy flux is as mitochondrial become impaired.

In 1874, the light bulb was invented, a few years later the power grid was invented and homes across America changed the way they live permanently using light 24/7. What do we do when the sun goes down? We flip in the light switch and turn the TV/computer on. We sit on our phones. On our laptops and tablets.

All of this is lowering our melatonin and arresting the process of apoptosis and making autophagy worse.

We are literally handcuffing our bodies natural and brilliant defense mechanism against cancer!

THINK ABOUT THIS FOR A MOMENT!

In our lifetime's research shows that 1 in 2 of us will develop cancer. This is a VERY sharp rise from even 50 years ago. In 1874 cancer was very rare.

We give money to cancer research charities who then give that money to cash-rich pharma companies who don’t need our money so they can create drugs that don’t cure cancer and make us go broke using them. We run, walk, and bike for the cure.

What if the cure for cancer was right under our noses?

What if it’s as simple as shutting off the damn lights when the sun goes down, or using low amber lighting, wearing Raoptics blue-blocking glasses, shutting off your Wi-Fi router, turning off your wireless devices, and instead of watching tv, hang out and talk with your family and then go to be early?

First of all, yes, I agree these studies cannot be trusted. Specifically, I would say that they cannot be _blindly_ trusted. That added word is a small difference wording, but a huge difference in semantics.

To choose my words very carefully: we should be a) very skeptical towards studies; b) always see any medical study within its proper context.

What is that context? Well, these are potential biases: 1) utter fraud, like the article on WiFi you just posted; 2) bias due to studies being carried out under artificial (blue) light; 3) bias do to not correcting for haplotypes; 4) bias due to not correcting for redox; 5) the absence of physics testing as opposed to biochemical testing; 6) another one not mentioned before: publication biases.

Then there is your point that doctors don't even read these studies, _etcetera_.

If you do not take these 6 (and possibly many more) points of context into account, then PudMed studies are _useless_. I 100% agree with you on that.

Maybe I'm an optimist, thinking _we_ - as careful readers of these studies - can positively get some data or information out of studies. Wallace's 2005 study is an example of a very useful PubMed study. The study on the LeptinRx on the previous page is another useful study. Mitochondria select and order electrons and protons by spin........that is how it happens. Atomic [spectra](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spectroscopy) measure radiation absorbed or emitted by electrons ["jumping"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atomic_electron_transition) from one "state" to another, where a state is represented by values of n, l, and m. These are called the 4 quantum numbers associated with every particle. The so-called "[Transition rule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transition_rule)" limits what "jumps" are possible. In general, a jump or "transition" is allowed only if all three numbers change in the process. There are exceptions to the rule (Zeeman effect). This is because a transition will be able to cause the emission or absorption of electromagnetic radiation (photons has spin number of 1) only if it involves a change in the electromagnetic [dipole](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dipole) of the atom. This is how energy and information are transferred from the sun to us.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-03-30T22:09:05+0300

Straight from a blog on RCT's
The critical difference between the “past” and “future” times only exists if heat is present. Heat is a red frequency of light and it makes atoms and molecules move in certain ways. Red light makes things with a mass move. The directions of these motions are critical to understanding probability and to understanding time. Quantum mechanics is the science of probability. In the 21st century, statistical mechanics of heat movement has now been extended to electromagnetic and quantum phenomena by QED. This is called thermodynamics. Today, because of Boltzmann, we know both space and time must vibrate or move, but we still do not know how to describe this process well with words of mathematics. Einstein tried to do this and came up with a geometric solution of the idea of curved space/time. The Standard Model of physics has embraced this idea as gospel 100%. Heat, as we all know from observations, **always** moves from hot things to cold things. This should make you ask yourself, why does heat always seem to go from hot to cold and not vice versa? Is there a universal law that says this is axiomatic? It turns out there is no law that tells us this. So this raises the question why does it always appear this way in reality?

_**Does heat or hot temperature contain some "information" in it that directs motion? I think it does.**_

Many of you might not realize the significance of this idea at first glance of this blog. Most of science today is built on the belief that experiments have to be reproducible in time to be "evidence-based". In all western schools of science, it is taught that if an experiment is not reproducible it is false. It turns out, this idea is true for a machine, computer, or a math problem but it certainly false for ecologic and biologic systems built are far from equilibrium. Our own observations tell us the time matters in life. A dropped piano from a height cannot go back to what it was before we dropped it, even though the laws of physics say it is possible. All living things obey the laws of biologic evolution that at any one time, they are quite different then they were an instant before. _**The difference may be small, but that difference always exists. In others words, information is always being shared.**_ This is the basis of epigenetic modifications to nucleic acids. This is what gives life its essence. In biology and ecology, reproducible experiments really do not exist, contrary to our stance on randomized control trials (RCT's). RCT's require reproducibility for science today. The irony is for the classical science of Descartes, Newton, Einstein, time does not exist in their theories. _So why is it that we require reproducibility in modern biologic RCT's?

Science has to invent concepts to describe nature because they do not yet fully understand it. "The stability of science (like a game) is wholly subservient to its participant's voluntary willingness to acquiesce to certain fixed boundary conditions of the construction and operation of the said game." Yes, modern science is a shell game, in my opinion. "Otherwise, the game is no longer worth playing. Once the participants fully comprehend and/or transcend the game's truly limited and arbitrary nature, they can no longer effectively suspend disbelief and the game is over. This makes science and the society that support it a game as well. Dogma provides the cultural rule-book that people mutually agree to play by. Dogma is a function of truth. Functions in the science of math are called derivatives." Derivations are slippery slopes used by paradigms to control masses of people's beliefs._

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-03-31T10:32:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238397>

> [Mayuri said:](/goto/post?id=238363)
>
> Come on now, no one thinks badly of you. The studies are just half truths, if not a complete fabrication. Life can be very difficult without a thumb.
>
> We see statins, and so many other medications, even over the counter drugs, completely destroy people's lives and health - and yet the so called 'studies' are in full support...
> Cheers-

With your current phrasing: "\[t\]he studies are just half truths, if not a complete fabrication", it's easier for me to agree. That phrasing is different than calling them "worthless", because in the latter case, PubMed studies would not play a vital role in any situation at all.

I know you don't think badly of me. That's was a joke, to signify the fact that you must become tired from a person who accepts nothing on face value.

An example, again. This one is on mitochondrial light signalling:

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5495261/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5495261/)

> **Optogenetic control of mitochondrial metabolism and Ca2+ signaling by mitochondria-targetedopsins.**
> Key mitochondrial functions such as ATP production, Ca2+ uptake and release, and substrate accumulation depend on the proton electrochemical gradient (ΔμH+) across the inner membrane. Although several drugs can modulate ΔμH+, their effects are hardly reversible, and lack cellular specificity and spatial resolution. Although channelrhodopsins are widely used to modulate the plasma membrane potential of excitable cells, mitochondria have thus far eluded optogenetic control. Here we describe a toolkit of optometabolic constructs based on selective targeting of channelrhodopsins with distinct functional properties to the inner mitochondrial membrane of intact cells. We show that our strategy enables a light-dependent control of the mitochondrial membrane potential (Δψm) and coupled mitochondrial functions such as ATP synthesis by oxidative phosphorylation, Ca2+ dynamics, and respiratory metabolism. By directly modulating Δψm, the mitochondria-targeted opsins were used to control complex physiological processes such as spontaneous beats in cardiac myocytes and glucose-dependent ATP increase in pancreatic β-cells. Furthermore, our optometabolic tools allow modulation of mitochondrial functions in single cells and defined cell regions.

Let me turn the question around to you Mayuri. Do you still think (and I'm directly quoting here) that "\[t\]hey just don't hold truth", that "\[t\]hese studies are all inconclusive", that "\[t\]hese studies can support whichever side you're on".

I hope not.

Again, it's not that we disagree. In the last 3-5 posts, I've really emphasized over and over again that I think a blind belief in PubMed studies is dangerous, and that they contain many possible biases and/or levels of fraud.

I wouldn't go as far as calling them worthless. They have value, _[if and only if](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/If_and_only_if)_ you understand their proper context.

Edit: before people jump on the PubMed study I posted above, it does give credence to Jack's notion that Wallace only gets half the equation right. Wallace misses the informational part of the equation - the light signalling. Do I therefore believe that the posted PubMed study has absolute value, and that it demonstrates an absolute truth? No. I try to be nuanced...

Edit 2: So, I'm not accepting an either-or distinction, between a) seeing PubMed studies at the be-all-end-all; b) completely worthless. I go for option c: PubMed studies have value, _if and only if_ you properly understand their context, and know what to look for, etcetera.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-03-31T14:40:13+0300

By virtue of the origins of clinical data with individual patients, and because these data are often compiled with public funds, they have many characteristics of a public good or public utility. This situation suggests implicitly that these data should be shared widely and used for the common good of improving the nation’s health and healthcare system. However, private entities also collect and analyze clinical data, often at great expense; place a proprietary value on clinical data; and protect these data as their own intellectual property. In fact, there is a big controversy in the UK now about releasing the raw data on statin trials and it is being played out in the BMJ. It is shocking how we have allowed Big Pharma to control how the data is used. It is one of the big issues why the process has broken and failed the public. It explains why biochemical Rx cannot solve biophysical problems.

Privacy is your right. So is access to food, water, humanitarian response. The challenge is that we see a lot of regulatory frameworks which don't have the right litmus test.— Robert Kirkpatrick, Head of UN Global Pulse

The ethics of not acting is the ethics of being afraid of doing something wrong.

— Geoffrey Canright, Head of Data Analytics Group at Telenor Research.

Kalos, your blog is great!! I appreciate the sources and cites you have. Having a study to support my view has helped me in more than one conversation to get a point across to open a conversation that otherwise would not have been taken seriously. It is the catching someone offguard effect when you are meant to be quoting from Cosmopolitan magazine and you instead say:Well, Smith et al in 2014 have decisively concluded that...

However it requires that you have an educated counterpart who will not just take you on face value. Think about this sequence.
He : Hey your DR says to drink wine. Wine raises Breast cancer risk .
You: you dutifully cite study on the benefits of wine to help heart health and the other one citing link between CV risk and breast cancer meds.( Your counterpart could now escape into a subgroup of medications who have not got "proven" adverse CV health risks)
He: But you have not made my point about breast cancer risk
You: You find the study that predates his from 2013 which says no convincing corelation has been found
He: "But this is from 2017 and not a cohort study. We have much better data now that we are all connected ( ) "
You: But yours is self reporting . This study shows that your studies based on self reporting have a bias of 15% to underreport if the answer could potentially socially stigmatise you.

Pub med gives you BOTH more ammunition to support your point of view. Both make money separately. Both of you assume that only one can be true. Maybe both can be true if one looks at the wider context?

When trying to get people to think about what you are saying, asking WHY is a much better way to mix it up a little. Why do we get such different resuts on alcohol and breast cancer if our (known) physiology is so clearcut? Can Resveratrol trump acetaldehyde production? Should all studies be done with the same brand of drink? Think that with what we know now about a certain isotope I could probably make the case for breast cancer risk being increased by x % just by carefully selecting the country/county to prove my point.

And then there is this problem. [https://www.medpagetoday.com/blogs/revolutionandrevelation/72029](https://www.medpagetoday.com/blogs/revolutionandrevelation/72029)

### taiyang — 2018-04-01T16:39:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238453>

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalos\_kagathos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalos_kagathos)
_**"Kalos kagathos**_ ([Ancient Greek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language): καλὸς κἀγαθός [\[kalos kaːɡatʰós\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Help:IPA/Greek)),[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalos_kagathos#cite_note-1) of which _[kalokagathia](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/kalokagathia)_ (καλοκαγαθία) is the derived noun, is a phrase used by [classical Greek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greece) writers to describe an ideal of [gentlemanly](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gentleman) [personal conduct](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/personal_conduct), especially in a military context.

Its use is attested since [Herodotus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Herodotus) and the classical period.[\[2\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalos_kagathos#cite_note-2) The phrase is adjectival, composed of two adjectives, [καλός](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BA%CE%B1%CE%BB%CF%8C%CF%82) ("beautiful") and [ἀγαθός](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%80%CE%B3%CE%B1%CE%B8%CF%8C%CF%82) ("good" or "virtuous"), the second of which is combined by [crasis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crasis) with καί "and" to form κἀγαθός.

[Werner Jaeger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Werner_Jaeger) summarizes it as "the [chivalrous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chivalry) ideal of the complete human [personality](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Personality), harmonious in mind and body, foursquare in battle and speech, song and action".[\[3\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kalos_kagathos#cite_note-3)

since we are exploring member names:
my screen name 'taiyang' means 太陽, or sun in mandarin chinese.
