### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-04-02T18:08:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238519>

As an interesting perspective, I would like to add Immanuel Kant's _What is Enlightenment_ from 1784.

Today, in physics and astronomy, Kant is still known as one of the first persons to develop the "[Nebular Hypothesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nebular_hypothesis)". His treatment on the importance _independent thinking_, however, _What is Enlightenment_, is equally important for me. You can find the full text [here](http://braungardt.trialectics.com/philosophy/early-modern-philosophy-16th-18th-century-europe/kant/enlightenment/).

More than two hundred years after its publication, the text may be even more important than even in Kant's time. In the text, Kant describes how each and every citizen has a duty to develop a critical _rational_ attitude. Kant's opening lines read:

“Enlightenment is man’s emergence from his self-imposed immaturity. Immaturity is the inability to use one’s understanding without guidance from another. This immaturity is self-imposed when its cause lies not in lack of understanding, but in lack of resolve and courage to use it without guidance from another. _Sapere Aude_! \[dare to know\] “Have courage to use your own understanding!”–that is the motto of enlightenment."

The most important thing to emphasize, is that the inability to use one's understanding is "self-incurred", _not_ through a lack of intellectual ability, but through lack of _courage_.

In Kant's time, people blindly followed the church and the state. That way, these people did not have to think for themselves. For most people, it was very convenient to let the preacher tell you how you should conduct your marriage. It was convenient to blindly follow the church. It was very convenient to be complacent.

Today, look at how Jack questions some of the major tenets of Darwinism. Also look at how Jack questions the current understanding of the second law of thermodynamics. Questioning authority - in _all_ it's forms - is not only the basis of proper science, but also of a proper tasks of citizens of republics in general.

Take a look at society today:

Through blue-lit environments, 9-5 jobs without any sunlight, exponentially growing nnemf exposure, and a standard american diet (I purposefully don't capitalize these words, as they don't deserve it), our questioning attitude - _and our ability to question -_ is weaker than ever.

You start to wonder: what came first? The blue light, and SAD? Or the loss of questioning ability - indeed, specifically our _courage_ to pose question? I think we'll never know...

Kant questioned the state, the church, and above all, himself and his fellow man. We should hold true to this maxim as well--especially now the lights are slowly going out in the West.

I leave that last sentence with no further comment...

Note that Kant's text is short, and a delight to read. "_Sapere Aude_! \[dare to know\]"!

My opinion is that Kant was a mystical religious apologist dressed up in intellectual clothing. In his own words, he admitted in his Critique of Pure Reason, that he found it necessary to limit knowledge (reason) in order to make room for faith. And in so doing laid the groundwork for the destruction of enlightenment thinking while posing as its great champion. > [KalosKaiAgathos said:](/goto/post?id=238519)
>
> As an interesting perspective, I would like to add Immanuel Kant's _What is Enlightenment_ from 1784.
>
> Today, in physics and astronomy, Kant is still known as one of the first persons to develop the "[Nebular Hypothesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nebular_hypothesis)". His treatment on the importance _independent thinking_, however, _What is Enlightenment_, is equally important for me. You can find the full text [here](http://braungardt.trialectics.com/philosophy/early-modern-philosophy-16th-18th-century-europe/kant/enlightenment/).
>
> More than two hundred years after its publication, the text may be even more important than even in Kant's time. In the text, Kant describes how each and every citizen has a duty to develop a critical _rational_ attitude. Kant's opening lines read:
>
> “Enlightenment is man’s emergence from his self-imposed immaturity. Immaturity is the inability to use one’s understanding without guidance from another. This immaturity is self-imposed when its cause lies not in lack of understanding, but in lack of resolve and courage to use it without guidance from another. _Sapere Aude_! \[dare to know\] “Have courage to use your own understanding!”–that is the motto of enlightenment."
>
> The most important thing to emphasize, is that the inability to use one's understanding is "self-incurred", _not_ through a lack of intellectual ability, but through lack of _courage_.
>
> In Kant's time, people blindly followed the church and the state. That way, these people did not have to think for themselves. For most people, it was very convenient to let the preacher tell you how you should conduct your marriage. It was convenient to blindly follow the church. It was very convenient to be complacent.
>
> Today, look at how Jack questions some of the major tenets of Darwinism. Also look at how Jack questions the current understanding of the second law of thermodynamics. Questioning authority - in _all_ it's forms - is not only the basis of proper science, but also of a proper tasks of citizens of republics in general.
>
> Take a look at society today:
>
> Through blue-lit environments, 9-5 jobs without any sunlight, exponentially growing nnemf exposure, and a standard american diet (I purposefully don't capitalize these words, as they don't deserve it), our questioning attitude - _and our ability to question -_ is weaker than ever.
>
> You start to wonder: what came first? The blue light, and SAD? Or the loss of questioning ability - indeed, specifically our _courage_ to pose question? I think we'll never know...
>
> Kant questioned the state, the church, and above all, himself and his fellow man. We should hold true to this maxim as well--especially now the lights are slowly going out in the West.
>
> I leave that last sentence with no further comment...
>
> Note that Kant's text is short, and a delight to read. "_Sapere Aude_! \[dare to know\]"!

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-04-02T19:13:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238534>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=238524)
>
> My opinion is that Kant was a mystical religious apologist dressed up in intellectual clothing. In his own words, he admitted in his Critique of Pure Reason, that he found it necessary to limit knowledge (reason) in order to make room for faith. And in so doing laid the groundwork for the destruction of enlightenment thinking while posing as its great champion. Much like Keyenes later did in the field of economics and Ancel Keys did in nutrition. All intelligent wolves in sheep's clothing, advancing their dogmatic agendas.

I've actually written one of my three Master thesis projects on Kant, in part, his Critique of Pure Reason, but also including many of his other works (Critique of Judgment; his Metaphysical grounds of Physics, and his late Opus Postumum, where he transitions away from his Critique of Pure Reason).

First of all, I do agree with you that Kant limited the role of reason. However, Kant, mostly limited the use of reason to concepts that could be given in the understanding It's important to note that reason and the understanding are two separate faculties of the mind for Kant. Let's explore that difference. For Kant, the faculty of the understanding _automatically_ judges on all sensory perceptions that enter the human mind. These sensory perceptions (Kant calls them sensible intuitions) are derived from your human senses. For Kant, the human mind automatically judges on sense perceptions, which are then automatically transformed into concepts.

So: sense perception (sensible intuition) -> logical judgment (inherent in the human mind, through "categories" that filter all sense perceptions) -> a concept in the human mind. Note that this entire process is _automatic_.

These concepts that originate from the understanding (which are thus _limited_ to the human senses), can then be used by reason to integrate knowledge into a unity.

Now, finally, we can explore why did limited the role of reason. Kant's predecessors, such as G.W. Leibniz, and Christian Wolff, assumed that the faculty of reason could be used to acquire _certain_ knowledge about the nature of god, the soul, and the afterlife. For Kant, these concepts were never d_erived for sensory perception_, and therefore Kant believed that reason ventured beyond its bounds. Kant eliminated these options as a possibility for the faculty of reason, by (mainly) limiting reason to concepts that originated from the understanding first.

I'm describing Kant from memory here, and the reality is FAR more complex. However, this should give you a pretty good notion of my perspective on this issue

In that thesis, I try to build a philosophy of science on Schelling's early and middle works. Schelling is interesting for me, because he derives his _entire_ epistemology _from nature herself._

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-04-03T20:03:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238612>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=238559)
>
> "all sensory perceptions that enter the human mind. These sensory perceptions (Kant calls them sensible intuitions) are derived from your human senses."
>
> His theory and all collateral ideas from it blow because mitochondria are the sensor of the environment we all use to build instinct/consciousness using information quanta.

Yes. This is a point where you obviously would disagree with Kant. Kant uses a strict dualism, that is akin to Descartes, but a bit different.

For Descartes mind _(res cogitans)_ and body (_res extensa_) are strictly separated. This creates a mind-body dualism, that is (accepted by most philosophers) unbridgable.

Kant turns this around, and basically separates _res cogitans_ into two separate domains of the mind. On the one hand, the structures of the mind, and on the other hand, _appearances_ to that mind. The problem is, that you can no longer have true knowledge of nature (_res extensa_) _-_ even though the strict body-mind dualism is no longer there.

I would expect you to have a more emergentist conception of the interaction between mind and body Jack...

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-04-03T20:19:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238613>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=238560)
>
> God is light and light is God and we get the sense of this process from protons..........via information processing. Did two webinars on that now.

Where have I heard this before?

Well:

[http://journals.library.mun.ca/ojs/index.php/analecta/article/viewFile/1363/988](http://journals.library.mun.ca/ojs/index.php/analecta/article/viewFile/1363/988)

The first quote:

"Light is already a completely ideal activity that deconstructs and reconstructs objects just as the light of idealism always does— and so _Naturphilosophie_ provides a physical explanation of idealism, which proves that at the boundaries of nature there must break forth the intelligence we see break forth in the guise of humanity \[Person des Menschen\]. Schelling, General Deduction of Dynamic Process, § 63"

If we look at my previous answer, Schelling tries to _avoid_ the dualism Kant created, by attempting to demonstrate how nature herself can give rise to "mind" (or conscious human beings)

The concept of light - and its relation to God - also plays a major role in Schelling's philosophy:

For Schelling, light is the _highest_ force, that counteracts lower forces in nature. Through history, that force of light has progressively caused an evolution in the universe. Note that for Schelling, that evolution includes a development from the universe's first moments, to the formation of planets, an ecosystem (on Earth), to life, and finally humans. During all these periods, the force of light gains a higher and higher expression. Schelling's evolution is thus very different from Darwin's evolution.

The force of light comes to its highest _current_ expression in humanity (that expression is not final, but further developments are possible). God is only "born" in humanity, and was unconscious before that. Why?

Only human beings can imagine the creation (or origin of the universe), and its subsequent development, under the influence of light. In the end, from the perspective of nature, there's no _hard_ distinction between God and humanity.

> **• For social justice, focus on symmetry and risk sharing.** You cannot make profits and transfer the risks to others, as bankers and large corporations do. You cannot get rich without owning your own risk and paying for your own losses. Forcing skin in the game corrects this asymmetry better than thousands of laws and regulations.
> **• Ethical rules aren’t universal.** You’re part of a group larger than you, but it’s still smaller than humanity in general.
> **• Minorities, not majorities, run the world.** The world is not run by consensus but by stubborn minorities imposing their tastes and ethics on others.
> **• You can be an intellectual yet still be an idiot.** “Educated philistines” have been wrong on everything from Stalinism to Iraq to low-carb diets.
> **• Beware of complicated solutions (that someone was paid to find).** A simple barbell can build muscle better than expensive new machines.
> **• True religion is commitment, not just faith.** How much you believe in something is manifested only by what you’re willing to risk for it.

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-04-06T09:26:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-238829>

> I don't ignore it. I think there are no good philosophers today. Taleb might be one but he hates them as much as I do. Maybe you'll start a Patreon philosophy site?

I also think that modern philosophy is "sick" in a sense, with its mind-body problem, and its problematic in acceptance that there is an outside world.

Contrary to to people who find the existence of an outside world _self-evident_, such as Jack Kruse (or Aristotle!), I do think that a proof is needed.

Ayn Rand is actually pretty close to Jack Kruse, because she - just like Jack Kruse - tends towards realism. Ayn Rand also claims to give a proof of this realism:

> Existence exists—and the act of grasping that statement implies two corollary axioms: that something exists which one perceives and that one exists possessing consciousness, consciousness being the faculty of perceiving that which exists.
>
> If nothing exists, there can be no consciousness: a consciousness with nothing to be conscious of is a contradiction in terms. A consciousness conscious of nothing but itself is a contradiction in terms: before it could identify itself as consciousness, it had to be conscious of something. If that which you claim to perceive does not exist, what you possess is not consciousness.
>
> Whatever the degree of your knowledge, these two—existence and consciousness—are axioms you cannot escape, these two are the irreducible primaries implied in any action you undertake, in any part of your knowledge and in its sum, from the first ray of light you perceive at the start of your life to the widest erudition you might acquire at its end. Whether you know the shape of a pebble or the structure of a solar system, the axioms remain the same: that _it_ exists and that you _know_ it.
>
> To exist is to be something, as distinguished from the nothing of nonexistence, it is to be an entity of a specific nature made of specific attributes. Centuries ago, the man who was—no matter what his errors—the greatest of your philosophers, has stated the formula defining the concept of existence and the rule of all knowledge: A _is A_. A thing is itself. You have never grasped the meaning of his statement. I am here to complete it: Existence is Identity, Consciousness is Identification.

You can replace the word "existence" in the paragraph above with the word "nature", to translate the quote into "Kruserism" (  ) without a loss of meaning. According to Rand, the denial of existence (or, in Kruserism terms: nature), has horrendous consequences.

What's interesting, is that both Kruse, Rand, and Kant, allocate a grossly similar role to reason: integration of individual concepts.

In a sense, that leaves postmodern philosophy with two problems for me: 1) denial of nature; 2) denial of reason.

Disclaimer: this argument is very loosely posited, without having gone through the rigorous reflection that is necessary for making a proper philosophical argument, and does not reflect the actual deeper analytical position of the author (Kalos).

Taleb: "The Hammurabi’s Law, if you cause harm to others, you ought to be penalized and compensate your victims. That is the idea. In others words I shall not hide risk. In the past how was it ascertained that a scientist was causing harm to others? in the current 'publish or perish' environment, quantity of scientific work is rewarded over quality. it seems funding agencies which are neutral (that is-leaving aside industry funding of science) do not have the ability to judge the quality of scientific work, so bad science is never punished. how to punish bad science? the results of bad science are not as clear as a building collapsing because the architect/builder cut corners. >
> What's interesting, is that both Kruse, Rand, and Kant, allocate a grossly similar role to reason: integration of individual concepts.
>
> In a sense, that leaves postmodern philosophy with two problems for me: 1) denial of nature; 2) denial of reason.
>
> Disclaimer: this argument is very loosely posited, without having gone through the rigorous reflection that is necessary for making a proper philosophical argument, and does not reflect the actual deeper analytical position of the author (Kalos).

Rand's quoted statement above is an extension/correction of Aristotlianism and of the pre-Socratic Parmenides,
with which I agree. An axiomatic declaration of existence and the primacy of existence over consciousness that is a prerequisite of conceptual knowledge.

The major issue with Kant was that he drew a line in the sand with his so-called Copernican Revolution and noumenal and phenomenal world and established subjectivity as the basis for scientific knowledge and declared that actual knowledge of the world we experience was impossible to obtain through reason. This paved the way, not only for the resurgence of religious mysticism but also for the collapse of objective science and philosophy, which as a Pius Christian, was actually Kant's stated objective. It is in my opinion no exaggeration to say this man single handedly destroyed the enlightenment, and he did it on purpose.

Colorado is bad.

QED says that a "single thought" can alter your reality, your health, and your DNA. Many of you have not accepted that today. (non-linear action)

The moral of this quantum lesson is simple. QED runs our belief paradigms, whether we perceive it or not. What we believe to be true is true, within certain limits, which are, themselves, beliefs. In the province of the mind, there are no real limits. How did I start this blog? I asked you this question, " What if I told you a core set of beliefs shared by enough people could actually set up an alternative reality and force you to live by its rules." Look around at modern healthcare or life now in the United States. Do you think that is far off base now? You still think this idea is folly? Consider this statement from Marcia Angell, former editor of the New England Journal of Medicine made in 2009, this echoes the fact:

**“It is simply no longer possible to believe much of the clinical research that is published or to rely on the judgment of trusted physicians or authoritative medical guidelines. I take no pleasure in this conclusion, which I reached slowly and reluctantly over my two decades as an editor of The New England Journal of Medicine.” -** Marcia Angell, MD, The New York Review of Books, January 15, 2009

Ms. Angell has been an insider to the world of research, PEER review publication, and the reporting of research to clinicians she is saying that her perception of reality has changed because health care decisions rest on cataclysmic fraud, a scientific fraud tied to poor research data.

See, quantum mechanics tells us that we can transcend what one thinks is possible, by examining and transcending one's beliefs.

This ability **creates** our reality as well, as hard as that may be for you to accept. **It implies what others say or does matter little to your conditions of existence.**

Our capacity for direct knowledge, for immediate insight, without observation or reason is called instinct. Instinct is as much a component of our thinking as is analytical knowledge. Instinct is built on information transfers. The key point in QED is that intellect and intuition are not complementary. They require each other to work congruently with our perception of reality. They are like Cooper pairs in QED theory.

**Without information collection, intellect and intuition drive us into chaos.** **Without intuition, we are unable to resolve issues that are too complex or that are happening too quickly for logical analysis.** Intellect is driven by intuition. But, intuition is directed by information quanta, in my view of reality. This clearly is not the belief system in medicine or in the paleosphere today.

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-05-29T08:56:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-242268>

Came across this gem (emphasis mine):

**Nutrition education in U.S. medical schools: latest update of a national survey.**

**PURPOSE:**
To quantify the number of required hours of nutrition education at U.S. medical schools and the types of courses in which the instruction was offered, and to compare these results with results from previous surveys.

**METHOD:**
The authors distributed to all 127 accredited U.S. medical schools (that were matriculating students at the time of this study) a two-page online survey devised by the Nutrition in Medicine Project at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. From August 2008 through July 2009, the authors asked their contacts, most of whom were nutrition educators, to report the nutrition contact hours that were required for their medical students and whether those actual hours of nutrition education occurred in a designated nutrition course, within another course, or during clinical rotations.

**RESULTS:**
Respondents from 109 (86%) of the targeted medical schools completed some part of the survey. Most schools (103/109) required some form of nutrition education. **Of the 105 schools answering questions about courses and contact hours, only 26 (25%) required a dedicated nutrition course**; in 2004, 32 (30%) of 106 schools did. **Overall, medical students received 19.6 contact hours of nutrition instruction during their medical school careers (range: 0-70 hours)**; the average in 2004 was 22.3 hours. Only 28 (27%) of the 105 schools met the minimum 25 required hours set by the National Academy of Sciences; in 2004, 40 (38%) of 104 schools did so.

**CONCLUSIONS:**
The amount of nutrition education that medical students receive continues to be inadequate.
​

Only 20 hours of nutrition education on average to become a doctor...

Edit: link: [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20736683](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20736683)

### KalosKaiAgathos — 2018-10-01T20:01:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mitochondria-are-not-captive-bacteria.21091/post-252093>

New interesting study. Just came out a few months ago

[https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/29928190](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/29928190)

(emphasis mine)

**Mitochondria and Mood: Mitochondrial Dysfunction as a Key Player in the Manifestation of Depression.**

Human and animal studies suggest an intriguing link between mitochondrial diseases and depression. Although depression has historically been linked to alterations in monoaminergic pharmacology and adult hippocampal neurogenesis, new data increasingly implicate broader forms of dampened plasticity, including plasticity within the cell. Mitochondria are the cellular powerhouse of eukaryotic cells, and they also regulate brain function through oxidative stress and apoptosis. **In this paper, we make the case that mitochondrial dysfunction could play an important role in the pathophysiology of depression. Alterations in mitochondrial functions such as oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) and membrane polarity, which increase oxidative stress and apoptosis, may precede the development of depressive symptoms.** However, the data in relation to antidepressant drug effects are contradictory: some studies reveal they have no effect on mitochondrial function or even potentiate dysfunction, whereas other studies show more beneficial effects. Overall, the data suggest an intriguing link between mitochondrial function and depression that warrants further investigation. Mitochondria could be targeted in the development of novel antidepressant drugs, and specific forms of mitochondrial dysfunction could be identified as biomarkers to personalize treatment and aid in early diagnosis by differentiating between disorders with overlapping symptoms.​

Surprise?

Not at all...

Shows you that just taking SSRIs to up certain neurotransmitters equals myoptic thinking.

"Patients producing anti-AChR antibodies had significantly lower levels of the 25(OH)D form of vitamin D than controls, but the same levels of the 1,25(OH)2D form, researchers discovered...
Although four patients taking immunosuppressive agents had insufficient levels of 25(OH)D, there is no significant difference in 25(OH)D levels between patients under only symptomatic therapy and immuno-suppressive agents,” the researchers wrote."

Just Googling around with my non- doctor knowledge, I found that 25(OH) is a precursor to 1,25(OH)2D, so how could 25(OH) levels be low and 1,25(OH)2D be normal?

# Thread: Greetings from California
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-california.21104/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RobertR — 2018-03-29T02:35:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-california.21104/post-238178>

I have been a reading, you tubing, explorer of mitochondrianism for about 8 months. I am extremely grateful for these resources and can see there is an endless amount of ever increasing information. I am introducing myself to the forum so that I can more effectively participate and access the information.

I am 68 yo and living on the central California coast. I have had a large garden for about 45 years, which has taught me a lot. I have always greeted medically/socially recommended behaviors with more than a bit of skepticism hence: I have never worn sunscreen or sunglasses, never gave up butter, meat, eggs or fat. Never owned a microwave No pharmaceuticals beyond a very occasional antibiotic. Tried vitamins a few times...tasted bad and did not digest. If I am looking for a super food it is always underfoot right where I live. I eat seasonal and locally. Pretty easy to do on the central coast. As I gardener I have always had a great connection with the sun. I am barefoot as much as possible. I live in the mountains in a tiny house in the trees with minimal electric, amber lights there is wifi from a neighbor and 3 bar cell signal. No light pollution. My cabin has a wood fire radiant floor so early evening til 10 pm it is delicious radiant heat. At night and early morning it gets down to 40-50 degrees and gives me some CT. I am also doing cold showers and the pond which is quite cold. Since leaving an emergency room job 12 years ago my vision returned to 20/20. I know first hand how emf toxic hospitals are. The morning after particularly bad ER shifts I would find myself watering bare foot in the garden it always cleared my head. My lighting is 2 low watt amber incandescents and candles. The electric is on about 1/3 the outside perimeter with a kill switch. I wear blue blockers when the lights or computer is on.

Things that I have added since becoming mitochondriac are the blue blockers, more seafood, timing of meals, sunrises ( I have seen way more sunsets than sunrises) now I am catching about 3 out of 4 sunrises, much more seafood. Searching for better water and Malbec. Most importantly I am better understanding the gravity of the current and future environmental light degradation.

The Entanglement webinar and the Clear Impact podcast have inspired me to be more vocal and involved. I have lived a lot of my life through intuition and observation ("really. so all of a sudden" the sun and UV light are poison and I need to slather your expensive chemicals on my skin hmmmm). I need to be more diligent about learning the science so I can talk to anyone. I have grandchildren I want them and all children to live their lives in a healthy environment.

So I am going to get my DNA analyzed and start some lab work. I am very interested in blocking emfs in the design of my tiny house (and every where and anywhere) I know due to my stubborness and frugality I am going to try deuterium deplete water. Leather soled shows / moccasins. Much more.

My 98 year old mother is still living her mother and all her sisters live to mid ninety.
I am meandering so I will stop.

After I give thanks to Jack for his vision, intelligence and passion. And to everyone I have and will interact with on the forum for their courage knowledge and kindness.

Robert

### mrc — 2019-05-23T11:28:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-california.21104/post-271091>

G'day from the west coast.
I was up in central California for a day of work this week & its insane how Sacramento is being geoengineered like crazy on a Monday day/night.

### caroline — 2019-05-24T06:31:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-california.21104/post-271172>

welcome Robert....

Please start a journal in the optimal journal section and tell us more.

You are building a tiny House??? I love them....

### drezy — 2019-05-24T17:49:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greetings-from-california.21104/post-271194>

> [RobertR said:](/goto/post?id=238178)
>
> Since leaving an emergency room job 12 years ago my vision returned to 20/20.

Wow! That's great.

> My 98 year old mother is still living her mother and all her sisters live to mid ninety.

### Dean6789 — 2018-03-29T06:29:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/california-proposition-65-warning.21106/post-238189>

I received a bag of whole organic rosemary leaf in the mail. Origin: Morocco

I called the supplier, Starwest Botanicals and they told me that it was because some soil has more lead than other soil and California required this warning sticker on the package. Naturally, Starwest said that the lead was at a "safe level".

Do you think the herb is safe to eat or would you throw it out?

### Karlos Amigos — 2018-03-29T20:15:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/california-proposition-65-warning.21106/post-238250>

Don't throw it out! I live in CA and buy a lot of herbs on a regular basis from various suppliers. I have never received a shipment that didn't have this warning on it. The California standards for lead are much higher than the federal standards and i believe this prop 65 statement is a CYA move to avoid potential lawsuits. The same product is sold in other states without the warning.

# Thread: Melatonin Absence Leads to Long-Term Leptin Resistance -  study
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-absence-leads-to-long-term-leptin-resistance-study.21108/>
**Subforum:** Mitochondrial Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2018-03-29T14:38:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-absence-leads-to-long-term-leptin-resistance-study.21108/post-238208>

another confirmation after the interstitium discovery

[https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fendo.2018.00122/full](https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fendo.2018.00122/full)

"Our findings reveal a previously unrecognized interaction of Mel and leptin in the hypothalamus to regulate the energy balance. These findings may help to explain the high incidence of metabolic diseases in individuals exposed to light at night."

### Jack Kruse — 2018-03-29T20:51:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melatonin-absence-leads-to-long-term-leptin-resistance-study.21108/post-238258>

Looks like my Vermont 2017 talk just gets more wise by the day.

As many of you probably know. I am behind on my research as I have only been here for just under a year. However I am working diligently to catch up. I just finished the 1/15 webinar on Circadian Biology trumping food and molecular hydrogen water came up. I am not understanding something here because I can’t think of what situations one would use this in. I get hydrogen causes unusual responses in the environment and body when it is present but I am not sure how this water works or if it is recommended in certain instances. Is it just for free radical support?

### JohnnyAZ — 2018-03-29T20:54:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/molecular-hydrogen-water.21110/post-238259>

Could anyone provide a recommendation on the best type of this water? I see dissolvable pills, pre-made waters, and machines. Any suggestions?

### RyanMSauce — 2018-03-29T21:19:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/molecular-hydrogen-water.21110/post-238262>

By far the best and cheapest way to do it is to get your own hydrogen tank and regulator and rig it up to fit the co2 carbonation tools for plastic bottles. What I do is fill a liter bottle 3/4 full of cold water and pressurize to 60psi. I shake the bottle for 30 seconds and let it sit for 10 min. I also add modified citrus pectin to get the ppm higher. I can easily get 5-6 ppm this way. The most expensive machine only does 2.5ppm. I got a tank from praxair.

Right now I drink about 4mg of hydrogen ( 1ppm works out to 1mg per liter apparently) 1-3 times a day

Jack is there a way we could get your thoughts on how to use hydrogen water with out going to a meeting? I would love to go to one but can't for a bit probably.

# Thread: Terrible heartburn lately from sardines
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/terrible-heartburn-lately-from-sardines.21116/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hbuttel — 2018-03-29T22:09:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/terrible-heartburn-lately-from-sardines.21116/post-238268>

I'm eating one can of Wild Planet sardines every day for about a month now and they are beginning to give me heartburn lately. It's getting worse and worse, lemon and ACV help a little, and I just started some Swedish bitters today, but what's this being caused by? I've never had problems with fish before, is it the oxidation from the canning process?

I'm considering ordering them frozen and in bulk now.

My friend has been eating the same ones and is also noticing the same problem.
And I just bought 4 cases!'

### Jack Kruse — 2018-03-29T23:28:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/terrible-heartburn-lately-from-sardines.21116/post-238272>

Maybe it is not the fish? I doubt it is.

### Hbuttel — 2018-03-30T03:13:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/terrible-heartburn-lately-from-sardines.21116/post-238290>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=238272)
>
> Maybe it is not the fish? I doubt it is.

Alright thanks, deeper observations forthcoming

### Maggie P — 2018-05-03T16:41:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/terrible-heartburn-lately-from-sardines.21116/post-240411>

I live in AZ, and it's hard to find good fish and seafood, and I was also thinking of turning to the canned Atlantic sardines they sell at Costco, so this is interesting to me. If you're wondering if it's the sardines, why not test it by backing off and seeing if the symptoms ease? I no longer have food allergies, but when I did, taking a particular item off the menu for two weeks then adding it back in pretty much proved whether I was allergic/sensitive or not. Of course, it's better to address the cause, but elimination can be a good way to test.
And look at where the sardines are processed, because the sardines themselves are only one element. My recollection is that in looking at tinned fish labels, which I did a lot of when first learning how important seafood is, is that most on the shelves, even in health food stores, even Wild Planet, are processed in Asia, places like Vietnam and Korea and such. There could be something about processing, something not related at all to the fish themselves, that is bothering you. I won't even go into the video I saw about Vietnamese processed shrimp and what they are putting into them. So another test would be switch to sardines that are processed somewhere far from where Wild Planet does theirs and see if you have the same reaction. The sardines I get in Costco are Israeli processed, I believe.
Anyway, eliminate or switch sources. It probably isn't sardines themselves.
Another tip is that when looking for raw food diet for our pets, I found someone who sells frozen sardines whole in bulk. You might research raw pet food in your area and see if you can find a source of raw sardines, if that would be an option you'd consider.
I hope you find the problem and resolve it soon.

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-03T16:48:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/terrible-heartburn-lately-from-sardines.21116/post-240414>

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/seafood-query.9562/page-2](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/seafood-query.9562/page-2)

# Thread: Light Exposure Potential vs nnEMF in Cars

**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Greenie — 2018-03-30T10:25:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-exposure-potential-vs-nnemf-in-cars.21118/post-238302>

The triumvirate of light, water, and magnetism are all interconnected; however, when it comes to selecting an automobile, does having a convertible that allows more exposure to the sun better than having a low nnEMF vehicle without the option of letting in the sun other than by rolling down the windows?

(I should add that I am not talking about electric cars that produce copious amounts of nnEMF.)

### Trev Douglas — 2018-04-04T15:12:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-exposure-potential-vs-nnemf-in-cars.21118/post-238668>

I have an early Porsche Boxster which is maybe best of both...being a '97 car it is very analogue, and being a convertible great for sun exposure.

All I know is it feels fantastic driving it early morning and late afternoon/early evening with the top down!
I'll often go for a drive at those times for no other reason than it feels good to...not very scientific, but that is my experience.

### Stian Van Zweden — 2018-04-04T21:21:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-exposure-potential-vs-nnemf-in-cars.21118/post-238708>

hi

> [Greenie said:](/goto/post?id=238302)
>
> The triumvirate of light, water, and magnetism are all interconnected; however, when it comes to selecting an automobile, does having a convertible that allows more exposure to the sun better than having a low nnEMF vehicle without the option of letting in the sun other than by rolling down the windows?
>
> (I should add that I am not talking about electric cars that produce copious amounts of nnEMF.)

Hi,
I just bough E-mountain-bikes for my family, have had them for a month now. And gotta say it the best think ever, never been this much outside and in nature. Biking everywhere now, they are fun and very functional at the same time. You get some level of exercise on them because they are only pedal assist.

### Greenie — 2018-04-04T21:28:19+0300

### Greenie — 2018-04-04T21:30:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-exposure-potential-vs-nnemf-in-cars.21118/post-238710>

> I'll often go for a drive at those times for no other reason than it feels good to...not very scientific, but that is my experience.

### ObstreperousPolarBear — 2018-04-06T08:23:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-exposure-potential-vs-nnemf-in-cars.21118/post-238824>

I've been thinking a lot about this one.

Older cars are definitely better, since they are less wired. I just bought a brand new jeep wrangler, however, and they are keeping them true to form and not adding all the garbage like seat warmers and hand holders. I haven't tested the magnetic fields in my car, but there is essentially no RF radiation difference in it with the car on or off. And yes, being able to get sun is a major benefit.

The caveat to all of this is simply that when you are in the car, you are neither grounded, nor active, plus most of your time on the road is going to be spent along a major thoroughfare what is loaded with RF radiation. So you can't really win in the car. You have to win where you live, where you play, and where you work.

tl;dr: I will always own a jeep and you should consider one too.

### Phosphene — 2018-04-09T21:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-exposure-potential-vs-nnemf-in-cars.21118/post-239078>

Not sure how old is old enough, but I recently (and very sadly) had to junk a 1997 Jeep Cherokee Sport. With a “split differential” manual transmission (I think that’s the fancy term) it averaged around 20 MPG, which isn’t great but not terrible. It was 2 wheel drive most of the time but easily switched to 4WD when needed. The electronics seemed relatively simple and I always felt great driving it. Unfortunately (at least for me) my husband mainly drove that and I had a tricked out minivan with all the stupid bells and whistles. Would buy another Cherokee in a heartbeat if it’s Kruse-approved, and take better care of it next time...

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-08-02T19:47:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-exposure-potential-vs-nnemf-in-cars.21118/post-247314>

I am not sure you can ground at all on an asphalt (blacktop) road-- that pavement is not conductive like concrete.

With regard to top-down convertibles being health-wise advantageous from a nnEMF power density standpoint-- the only way to know is to measure levels with a reliable RF meter. I suspect MW RF levels would be significantly higher in a convertible on a microwave tower saturated roadway, but not necessarily. There are a lot of variables here-- including how much the car wi-fi and cell sourced RF radiation (and other nnEMFs that car is generating itself-- and gets ricochet-reflected around and around in the metal cocoon we call cars). Car windows are essentially transparent to RF waves, but metal as we know is a reflector.

### drezy — 2018-08-02T20:54:23+0300

> I am not sure you can ground at all on an asphalt (blacktop) road-- that pavement is not conductive like concrete.

Good point and worth testing and great practice to test a vehicle.

> but metal as we know is a reflector.

Until it isn't aren't. It depends on the frequencies and the metals. The first time I had to stand in for an ill RF engineer I saw his glass cube with 3um of gas assisted silver deposition which was aa low loss waveguide. My 5-10 GHz worldview changed when I saw his 30+GHz world and had to explain it to our customers for a day.

# Thread: buy local to get the correct light information in your food?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buy-local-to-get-the-correct-light-information-in-your-food.21120/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### taiyang — 2018-03-30T23:52:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buy-local-to-get-the-correct-light-information-in-your-food.21120/post-238362>

1\. Am i correct in assuming that one should optimally only consume local meat/seafood/dairy/vegetables/fruits in order to get the correct light information to your mitochondria?
2\. does anyone on this forum do that? if yes, where do you live and what sorts of local food do you eat in the winter?

when i lived in taiwan, i dropped a lot of weight. i pretty much ate everything locally sourced. i ate a lot of seafood that was caught around the island, and produce in taiwan is grown during all four seasons. when we went to traditional markets, fish were swimming in tubs and chickens were live in cages. now here in new jersey, the only thing i get locally sourced in winter is maybe some meat, eggs, and cheese from Lancaster, Pennsylvania. There are no vegetables or fruit growing here after frost. my long-lived grandparents who lived nearby did eat a lot of canned and frozen fruits and vegetables grown on their farm. i wonder if the fact that that produce was grown in their environment helped keep them healthy even though they were eating it out of season.

So it is very possible, even if I do not do it 100%... as I add some nuts, greens and veggies in summer etc.
I do feel the best tho, when I do it!

Nothing feels better than eating a bunch of oysters on the beach or by the lake.. naked.. with some wild greens... and then some wild berries or rosehips to end the meal...
That is for me _the best healing_.

### Inger — 2018-03-31T15:15:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buy-local-to-get-the-correct-light-information-in-your-food.21120/post-238402>

Well, as I said I am not always 100% so I sometimes do organic raw nuts... and occasionally some salad leaves or so or a cheat with dark chocolate! (which does feel a little weird!). But most days I eat 100% carnivore - all winter, that is what feels the best and what I know is best for me here. I also sometime use organic balsamic vinegar. That is not ideal either but I do love it over my carpaccio with olive oil... so those are my "cheats". Oh, another cheat is whipped egg yolk with less than 1/2 teaspoon raw honey and lots of butter whipped into a egg/butter cream... this is a real cheat..lol I do not cheat with these often in winter though. But I can totally leave them out and eat 100% from here, that is very practically possible!

I just eat the oysters raw and plain, or use a little fresh lemon squeezed over them. Sometimes Crystal Sauce

### taiyang — 2018-03-31T15:27:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/buy-local-to-get-the-correct-light-information-in-your-food.21120/post-238406>

"Oh, another cheat is whipped egg yolk with less than 1/2 teaspoon raw honey and lots of butter whipped into a egg/butter cream... this is a real cheat..lol"
the raw whipped egg yolk with honey sounds like a traditional russian food my mom used to have us eat as kids called 'gawgl mawgl'.
[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kogel\_mogel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kogel_mogel)
My haplotype is U4a, so this food really suits me as its origin seems to be in line with the origin of my haplotype.
i think we used the whole egg, though (not sure seeing that the traditional recipe only uses yolks). my mom used to put raw egg in our morning hot cereal (kasha) as well. she never did buy into low fat, no sun, tylenol, artificial sugar etc. that is the advantage of having a mom who stuck to the ways of the old country in many things.

i think i need to eat 100% carnivore (except for maybe some celery, peppers, romaine??) as well until my brother/father start to harvest their produce on the family farm nearby. i think we will be picking zucchini and cucumbers first (end of june). tomatoes don't come until around july 4. a little further south of them they will buy some other freshly grown produce i can add in. i am excited to try this way of eating!!

Bingo!

Below is a picture of the only fruit I will be eating since last Spring/Summer unless pests get to it. I also have some fig trees and some natural grapes. I'm fencing them all in now too because:

1.  I'm done sharing with the D-bag deer that took half my fruit last year
2.  My fenced in orchard will double as a nudist haven where one can air one's junk, jump in an outdoor cold tub, and start a pit fire for extra IR

I also grow a whole bunch of spicy peppers and almost all of the herbs I use in sausage making. @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) knows why I'm increasing my pepper crop this year and doc has alluded to it in the past.

@[Inger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1057/) is a master in this domain. My own internal meme is: See Inger Be Inger.

I'm studying how to grow turmeric roots and will prep a bed for turmeric and ginger this year.

On a side note: Now that I'm growing more produce the full gravity of some of the history I've read hits me. When the Ancient Greek city states would battle the victor would often ride in and cut all fig trees to the ground in opposing cities. Certainly far more gruesome acts can occur in war, but until you've developed even a little orchard like me it's hard to understand how devastating and demoralizing that act would be.

# Thread: surfing thru the CA EMF fields of joy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surfing-thru-the-ca-emf-fields-of-joy.21121/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dane Jacobsen — 2018-03-31T02:43:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surfing-thru-the-ca-emf-fields-of-joy.21121/post-238391>

Howdy,
I'm very excited to be part of this forum. I've been residing in Santa Barbara County and various parts of Northern and Southern CA for most of my life with a few years in Hawaii & Indonesia. Being a life long surfer and having a long term relationship with the Pacific Ocean has kept me close to nature and helped offset the damage from decades shackled to the computer as a Graphic Designer.

I recently made some serious life changes, left a farily lucrative job to work with local farmers / viticulturists, and became a ranch hand where I tend to the needs of horses, dogs, chickens on my client's semi-rural ranch. I now spend the majority of my time outdoors with fairly limited EMF exposure. I still design for various companies from startups to fortune 500 but limit my time on the computer and will not settle for a full time "blue light life."

Dr. Jack recently replied to one of my comments on Facebook - about CA wine - which made me realize that I'm just scratching the surface and need to dig into the details. I immediately signed up for a membership and started to read through various posts on the forums. I'm very grateful for every piece of Dr. Jack's relentless "no bullshit" message. I look forward to interacting with this community!!!!

Best,
Dane

This webinar was beyond pretty epic and is amazing to see how these predictions from 4 years ago are playing out in real-time. I'm in the midst of updating some wine labels for a client, whom I've been interacting with for 5 years, and hope to get some insights from a local viticulturist as to the current state of affairs in CA. From an investment standpoint this webinar is utterly priceless for anyone in or out of the wine industry. My viticulturist client also has a beautiful pasture of Yorkshire Hogs. ### Jack Kruse — 2018-04-01T02:24:40+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2018-04-01T04:53:20+0300

### Penny — 2018-04-02T20:43:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tumeric-the-quantum-story-linked-to-the-auger-effect.21122/post-238540>

[http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Atomic/auger.html](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/Atomic/auger.html)
The Auger effect is a process by which electrons with characteristic energies are ejected from atoms in response to a downward transition by another electron in the atom. In Auger spectroscopy, the vacancy is produced by bombardment with high energy electrons, but the Auger effect can occur if the vacancy is produced by other interactions. It is observed as one of the methods of electron rearrangement after [electron capture](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/nuclear/radact2.html#c3) into the nucleus.

If an inner shell electron is removed from an atom, an electron from a higher level will quickly make the transition downward to fill the vacancy. Sometimes this transition will be accompanied by an [emitted photon](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/mod5.html#c2) whose quantum energy matches the energy gap between the upper and lower level. Since for heavy atoms this [quantum energy](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/mod2.html#c4) will be in the [x-ray](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/ems3.html#c4) region, it is commonly called x-ray fluorescence. This emission process for lighter atoms and outer electrons gives rise to [line spectra](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/quantum/atspect.html#c1).

In other cases, the energy released by the downward transition is given to one of the outer electrons instead of to a photon, and this electron is then ejected from the atom with an energy equal to the energy lost by the electron which made the downward transition minus the binding energy of the electron that is ejected from the atom. Though more involved in interpretation than optical spectra, the analysis of the energy spectrum of these emitted electrons does give information about the atomic energy levels. The Auger effect bears some resemblance to [internal conversion](http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/nuclear/radact2.html#c5) of the nucleus, which also ejects an electron.

Curcumin fills in the holes left by the downward transition of the electrons in AD - those electrons would be in ground state and therefore have no energy - turmeric excites these electrons...

### drezy — 2018-04-05T18:14:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-before-sunrise.21124/post-238756>

I continue tinkering with my routine about every 3 months. I'm doing morning CT and still throw in an evening CT here and there.

The only rule I seem to remember that I may be bending is the "fatty meal before CT" unless I'm not remembering that correctly. Doing the cold in the morning seems to supercharge my productivity.

Have you found any upsides to morning CT?

### kris90 — 2018-04-05T21:39:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/waking-up-before-sunrise.21124/post-238781>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=238756)
>
> I continue tinkering with my routine about every 3 months. I'm doing morning CT and still throw in an evening CT here and there.
>
> The only rule I seem to remember that I may be bending is the "fatty meal before CT" unless I'm not remembering that correctly. Doing the cold in the morning seems to supercharge my productivity.
>
> Have you found any upsides to morning CT?

I think if you are quite lean, the fatty meal before CT will help keep you warmer due to the effects of mitochondrial uncoupling when you eat a high fat meal. I think if you are fasted you may have a lower tolerance to the cold due to down-regulation of the metabolism, especially if you are already lean. To me, being lean and doing fasted CT is just badass. It's learning to love the cold. It's never "fun" per se, but addictive in some weird way. Last night I was feeling quite cold before bed, and needed to shower. I thought maybe I would take my first hot shower in years, and then when I went to go in, I couldn't. Just could not abandon my CT. I am seriously a slave to it.

First thing in the morning is probably the best wakeup aside from sunlight. I think it probably does something similar: definitely a cortisol boost (like blue light from AM sun) and then red light being released from our fat. CT before bed = amazing sleep.

The way I see it is that if we are lean, and not basking in the sun, we should feel cold period. When we do not have an external energy source to tap into (i.e. sun) we have to tap into the energy stored within us. It's somewhat uncomfortable, but the animals don't seem to complain.

# Thread: Grounding mat in car
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-mat-in-car.21125/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jenny S — 2018-04-01T05:57:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-mat-in-car.21125/post-238440>

Anyone know if these are a good idea or not? Thanks

### ElectricUniverse — 2018-04-01T20:10:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-mat-in-car.21125/post-238473>

Jenny,

I know there are claims for using grounding mats in vehicles to release static charges on occupants bodies and relieve driver fatigue-- but I am skeptical.

Since you can't ground to earth in a rubber-tired vehicle the actual claim is that the car mat grounds to the metal frame of car and so works that way. Technically, if you could wire a mat to a discharge wire dragging on pavement that might work for earthing-- but only for concrete roads not asphalt roads.

Really, the only way to prove or disprove a car static charge grounding notion is to conduct a personal field test with a body voltage meter and see if your body does indeed discharge voltage with a mat.

But even so, that would not deal with all other EMF pollution found in modern cars-- including WiFi, Bluetooth, traffic sensing radars on board, and ELF radiation from batteries and electronics, and so forth. Not to mention exposure to death towers (cell base stations) that are so conveniently located in dense patterns very close along major arteries and highways so vehicle users can always have complete access to cell signals.

The best thing you can do right now to protect yourself is to drive as little as possible.

But since we know so little about EMFs in our daily environment, and even less about safe mitigation techniques I say the jury is out on the whole question of grounding safely in our urban, built-up environments. For example, some people say that grounding in the house via the grounding plug of outlets or a wired
metal stake in our backyards will do the trick.

Because the electric grid in US was built economically by using the ground to send excess electric currents back to the grid (ever notice the guy wire-looking attachments to your local power pole-- that's its purpose) I personally believe you can't ground with complete safety anywhere there is potential dirty electricity lurking around in built-up areas.

But if you have some evidence-based science to prove otherwise please share.

### Jenny S — 2018-04-02T05:12:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-mat-in-car.21125/post-238497>

> [ElectricUniverse said:](/goto/post?id=238473)
>
> Jenny,
>
> I know there are claims for using grounding mats in vehicles to release static charges on occupants bodies and relieve driver fatigue-- but I am skeptical.
>
> Since you can't ground to earth in a rubber-tired vehicle the actual claim is that the car mat grounds to the metal frame of car and so works that way. Technically, if you could wire a mat to a discharge wire dragging on pavement that might work for earthing-- but only for concrete roads not asphalt roads.
>
> Really, the only way to prove or disprove a car static charge grounding notion is to conduct a personal field test with a body voltage meter and see if your body does indeed discharge voltage with a mat.
>
> But if you have some evidence-based science to prove otherwise please share.

I asked how they could ground from place bought them from (I'm in Oz) & they said it's because they are steel belted they still ground.
Did have grounding sheets etc for house but after buying a stetzer meter & asking guy where bought it from have stopped using them all.

### John Saf — 2018-04-01T22:23:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-eyes-in-the-sun.21127/post-238480>

The sun is back here at 62'N. We are spending all day outside. My kids love the sun.
My oldest girl (4.5years and blue eyes) get red eyes after some hours outside in the sun/snow. No pains, complains or problems. It is ok the next morning. My son (2years brown eyes) dont get red eyes.
Shold i put on sunglasses mid day? She wears a cap without any less red eyes.

### WalterNL — 2018-04-02T13:06:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-eyes-in-the-sun.21127/post-238503>

Yeah ease it in the sun, especially with younger ones who have been inside (?) for winter. We are not all like @[JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) who walk around with golden tanned skin and can adjust immediately to the equator sun.

### John Saf — 2018-04-02T21:04:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/red-eyes-in-the-sun.21127/post-238543>

Thank you for your comments. Today they had sunglasses 3 hours in the middle of the day, and her eyes was not red before bed. She did not like them at all, but we will ease in to it so we can put them away.
We are always outside when we can, but the wintersun is weak. Mid winter we only have 1 hours sun over the mountain tops. (Now we have 12 with a different strength). So the difference is huge.

### John Saf — 2018-04-02T21:12:26+0300

### ElectricUniverse — 2019-02-01T18:35:37+0200

I use a lot of turmeric in cooking-- and stopped taking curcumin supplements entirely.

I tended to follow the adage "if a little bit is good then a lot is better" but I am a little wiser now. Herbs and spices have many substances (phytochemicals) that can have very powerful effects on body-- good and potentially bad...depending. We should respect that and practice careful moderation.

# Thread: Hannah's optimum journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hannahs-optimum-journal.21133/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HannahS — 2018-04-03T05:08:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hannahs-optimum-journal.21133/post-238571>

I’m in the process of implementing lifestyle changes, I have a few questions that I would like some help with

1\. how do i get vitamin C in the winter if I eat a seasonal diet ? i had been getting vitamin C from Camu berry and acerola cherry powder which are not seasonal or local.

2\. I’d like to get a UV light bulb. Should I get a UVA or UVB or UVA/UVB light bulb? What are recommendations for time of day to use it and for how long ie, any restrictions on the time of use? Websites selling UV lights say not to look at it, I suppose based on the old theory that all UV light is bad for you, But can you actually get too much? can my dogs get too much?

3\. Glasses question on tints. I work indoors with LED screens, computers, florescent lights, and wifi. I have RAoptics reddish orange blue blockers which i’m using at night, which are fantastic, and UVEX blue blockers over my regular untinted prescription glasses when doing computer work during the day. I still have a few hours on most days that I’m looking at a screen without any blockers since I need color perception for my job, so I’m still getting blue light toxicity. For the rest of the time at work I sometimes need a little color perception, sometimes I need no color perception.

1.  During the day, what tint is best when exposed to blue light from computers and indoor lights to protect my eyes? yellow/amber lens blue blockers? my uvex googles? or something else? My uvex goggles provide good coverage but keep falling off my face
2.  For night time driving with those horrible white LED street lights, I need a recommendation for what tint of glasses to try? The uvex glasses and reddish orange lens nighttime blue blockers block out too much light for safe driving at night.

thank you

### Jack Kruse — 2018-04-03T05:18:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hannahs-optimum-journal.21133/post-238572>

1\. you don't need Vitamin C unless you are a carbowhore outside of the solar cycle
2\. I think UVA bulbs are fine for 9-4PM
3\. Id wear the Raoptics to work. I do.
4\. I use bluetech from 9AM through 4Pm after that I use 550 BPI tints that Raoptics uses.
5\. I use my 550 BPI tints.

### caroline — 2018-04-04T08:36:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hannahs-optimum-journal.21133/post-238651>

welcome Hannah......

If you get up before sunrise, try a cold shower or face dunks to wake up. Then still get morning sun as soon as its out, and at that point, Mother Nature's kitchen opens. It closes by sunset.

Yes but if you are leptin resistant, don't you need to eat within 30minutes after waking up? Sometimes you need to drive to watch sunset, means you have to wake up earlier. Normally I would wake up before sunrise and eat and then go to watch sunrise.

### kris90 — 2018-04-19T16:54:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/if-i-wake-up-before-sunrise-when-should-i-eat.21135/post-239551>

> [Antonis said:](/goto/post?id=239548)
>
> Yes but if you are leptin resistant, don't you need to eat within 30minutes after waking up? Sometimes you need to drive to watch sunset, means you have to wake up earlier. Normally I would wake up before sunrise and eat and then go to watch sunrise.

No, you should watch sunrise first before eating. The reason is tied to the transition between pineal gland melatonin release, and pituitary gland diurnal hormone release (i.e. cortisol). Melatonin makes you insulin resistant. That's a good thing for keeping you a fat burner while you sleep, but a bad thing for eating food (hence why eating outside of light cycles is a bad idea and why there's huge spikes in diabetes and metabolic syndrome today).

You need to see AM light before you add food to your body, because you want to turn off melatonin release in the pineal gland. Remember, the eye clock must always run FASTER than the peripheral (gut) clocks. That means the light frequencies that hit your eye has to be a stronger stimulus than the light frequencies that hit your gut. When you eat in darkness, you just sped up your gut clock relative to your eye clock (no different than eating carbs in the dead of winter). That creates chaos in your body.

The eye needs to tell the gut its morning, and then food electrons can be delivered accordingly. This is why your breakfast should fall between initial sunrise and when UV shows up. Once UV shows up, you are designed to feed off electrons from the earth to capture those light frequencies, and use the 42% IR light in the sun to drive the ATPase to make all the ATP you require without food electrons.

> You need to see AM light before you add food to your body, because you want to turn off melatonin release in the pineal gland. Remember, the eye clock must always run FASTER than the peripheral (gut) clocks. That means the light frequencies that hit your eye has to be a stronger stimulus than the light frequencies that hit your gut. When you eat in darkness, you just sped up your gut clock relative to your eye clock (no different than eating carbs in the dead of winter). That creates chaos in your body.
>
> The eye needs to tell the gut its morning, and then food electrons can be delivered accordingly. This is why your breakfast should fall between initial sunrise and when UV shows up. Once UV shows up, you are designed to feed off electrons from the earth to capture those light frequencies, and use the 42% IR light in the sun to drive the ATPase to make all the ATP you require without food electrons.

And by the way, you don't happen to have a blog or website?

# Thread: Shannon's Optimal Journal:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shannons-optimal-journal.21138/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shannon Palmer — 2018-04-04T04:43:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shannons-optimal-journal.21138/post-238641>

My chiropractor/functional medicine doctor introduced me to Jack Kruse a few months ago, finally after reading his book, blogs, and reading forum threads as well as podcasts and listening to the Vermont 2017 talk, I figured I would post in the forum. All responses welcome and appreciated!!

Some history: I'm 20 years old, living in Pittsburgh, Pa, born of a cesarian section. (As were each of my other 3 siblings.) Never had any vaccinations. (thankfully)My childhood medical history is not too expansive, beginning at age 11 I had some kind of vitiligo appear on my thigh, as well as eczema that appears every winter since I was a child. When I was maybe 6 I began to have bad allergies to any kind of mold(obviously) as well as cats. Not too sure if that matters, but I will include it anyway. Only my mother and I were affected by allergies, (her health is genuinely not optimal in general to be kind..)

I have always been a somewhat picky eater, and never had much of an appetite as a kid, always being coerced into eating, and was generally pretty thin in my childhood. Especially from ages 11-14, I was somewhat anxietal, and wouldn't eat much. I developed pretty late, didn't get my menstrual cycle till age 14. In high school, I was still somewhat thin, despite eating a SAD and generally did not pay much mind to my overall health aside from sports. At age 16 I was diagnosed with anorexia nervosa, got down to about a BMI of 15.5. Ended up in a treatment facility, restored my weight up to about BMI of 18.5ish. (2015) In January of 2014, I was put on a myriad of antidepressants and anti anxiety meds, as were all of the patients, but stayed on zoloft and am currently weaning off of it. (Started out at 200mg and currently on 12.5mg, and coming down.)

However, ever since then my health and labs have not been great, unsurprisingly. After being at a healthy weight, I still didn't take great care of myself. Freshman year of college I was practically vegetarian, forced to eat chicken/fish only when I returned home. (I know it's awful) Also living on campus in a city(Such high quantum yield and sooooo many EMFs and horrible artificial light, that I was surrounded by CONSTANTLY.) On top of all of that I was definitely living on carbs and desperately tried to compensate with tons of cardio. (so dumb) All throughout that year I was horribly depressed, and anxietal, definitely due to environment, as well as diet and consuming city water with tons of deuterium.

Finally in September of 2017 I began seeing a functional med. doctor, who I'm still working with now, and he began me on a ketogenic diet. Completely stopped cardio and began weight lifting. Saw improvements (to me) in body composition, started out at 110/112lbs (I'm 5'6) and currently 120 with roughly 13% body fat.
Started zero carb in October, (maybe a bit too early?) Stopped doing totally zero carb in late January because I had pretty poor digestion, constipated frequently, bloated and such. Added in some types of vegetables very little still 20g of carbs or less. In March I started upping my carbs a bit more, to about 50-65g, and am there right now too. Plenty of seafood now as well as sea vegetables since March, as well as honey, some nuts, full fat coconut milk too. I'm very strict with the diet, I would never and have never cheated on it btw, with the exception of probably too much coffee. (definitely not trying to sound self righteous by any means.) Also since my doctor introduced me to Jack, I have been wearing Uvex blue blockers when at work under fluorescent light, and when using my laptop. (Just ordered raoptics glasses so I can wear them to school) My phone screen is red, and I do CT frequently. (Always could be better. CT during all of my showers, and cold bathes about 3 times a week, and being in the sun as much as possible.) I try to get am sunlight as much as possible but definitely difficult with all of the rain that Pa has been getting. (no excuse though) I don't sleep with my phone in my room either and it is turned off/airplane mode at night.

As far as labs go, my thyroid has been low for 5 years, my BUN/Creatine levels are high for the last 4 years, I have taken a 23&me test, showing that I have a methylation problem as well. Which is certainly not the worst of it. In september my total cholesterol was 290 and LDL was over 200. When retested in January my total cholesterol was 1100. It was not just a lab mistake. I have retested my labs another 2 times after that both in March, and the total has not changed. March 10th: 1075 total, LDL 917 I believe, and on March 21st total was 1113, LDL 987. My Alkaline phosphatase has gone up as well since september from 74 to 122. I got a full abdomen ultrasound because elevated ALP points to liver, and nothing showed up all was clear. I am doing a salivary hormone test once it comes in the mail, and as I've , mentioned previously my functional doctor and I have been working a lot on this, he puts so much time into me. I figured that anything may help though, and if anybody has any clue or idea or suggestion then feel free to give it! Thank you to anybody who reads this I know it was disgustingly long winded, and I appreciate all of you!! Thanks in advance, and feel free to as any questions.

### Shannon Palmer — 2018-05-14T03:16:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shannons-optimal-journal.21138/post-241147>

Not sure where to put this, but figured this is my safest bet: after months and months of meticulously counting calories despite being on the leptin rx diet, i finally stopped counting macros. Not sure what finally clicked, but realized that I would be leptin resistant indefinitely if I didn't just quit completely. Currently, I follow the diet without counting, have been very regular with my CT, phone on airplane mode as much as possible, and generally trying to stay off of it when I can. I get sun every day with as much skin exposed as possible, and on days that it rains I'll still go outside and do some grounding. Blue blockers at night etc.. I guess my question is, has anyone else felt like they were eating way too much when not counting? I feel as though I am eating so much more and I am worried about gaining weight. Mind you it has only been about 2 weeks since I stopped counting macros, and my weight is consistently the same. I eat 3 times a day, but just a lot. Does hunger ever settle down? It makes me want to start counting again, and I am wondering if this has happened to anybody else? I understand in leptin sensitive people that calories don't technically matter because of UCP3, but I am likely still leptin resistant. Thanks in advance for any feedback! All is appreciated.

### Mariangela — 2018-05-14T15:26:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shannons-optimal-journal.21138/post-241160>

“I guess my question is, has anyone else felt like they were eating way too much when not counting? I feel as though I am eating so much more and I am worried about gaining weight. Mind you it has only been about 2 weeks since I stopped counting”

Shanon - Yes! But you will get passed that. I spent most of my life counting, counting, and counting those depressing calories. I always gained weight so easy. It was wierd notcounting at first but i was ready to do it. I felt like i was eating waaay too much too. And then i thought , what a relief. For the first time in my adult life i can sit down and just eat! Noguilt, no beating myself up . Took a while to get used to it. Freedom- its a scary thing when you own it. Then i realized all my life I chose what weight to be based on comments and society and ate for THAT weight. My dr even put me on a 500 calorie diet and i still gained. Talk about feeling like a loser! Now I let my body choose. Yes i have gained weight but its a lot of muscle. And after all the nutrient devastation and sickness i know my body will have to adapt to health now. And i will have to mentally adapt too. But thats a terrific thing! I just let it be intuitive . I have no illness. Some skinny photoshopped drug addict model in a magazine ad is not going to tell me how to be a healthy woman. Ya know?
