Welcome!
I found that with children it is easier to put a screen protector on the computer and use IRIS and not use after sunset if possible.
Letting the kids dip their faces in a bowl with cold water with ice is a good start and a game they will really like.
Playing with water in the garden or fill A large water vessel or any activity that causes them to take the clothes off to be in the water and sun
Believe me, I took it as a project It's a challenge and not easy today with screens . i tried to think about how to get the children out of the house in any way possible, a vegetable garden ropes on the trees and wooden houses Any activity outside the house is a blessing

### MKelly — 2018-05-07T18:56:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mother-father-3-children-our-journey-toward-optimal.21281/post-240719>

Thank you Shiran and Drezy, I really appreciate the encouragement.
We just spent 9 days in Morocco, lots of sun and some days 3 hrs in the pool. I can already notice a difference in my daughter after months of rain at the 48th latitude...
We will start some outdoor family projects, we live outside the city so have some outdoor space to work with.
Thank you!

### MKelly — 2019-12-19T19:55:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mother-father-3-children-our-journey-toward-optimal.21281/post-283808>

I have not been updating my mitochondrial journey on here since I began. I thought my journey was not significant, that I wasn’t doing enough, that it wouldn’t add much to the community.

I now realize that is not true.

Looking back, I took my first deep dive into the mito world when I scheduled a skype consult with Dr Kruse in late 2016 (not sure he does those anymore, glad I got in there)

It was early morning in New Orleans and night time in France, where we were living. He insisted my husband join, which was extremely wise. He gave us a talking to that we will never forget, and got my husband on board in a way that I never could have. (It’s interesting how we often have the least amount of influence on the people closest to us… but that’s another post)

Dt Kruse’s first question to me was to ask what time it was. About 8pm. His next question was “where are your glasses?” YES. I got on a computer call with Dr Mitochondria at night with the LED overheads on, the screen on and no eye protection! I have learned since then…!!

The last few years have been slow but steady progress toward a healthier life. We are not black swan poster children. We have made many small changes that have added up over time. When a new world opens up I tend to dive in, become obsessive and live with a level of intensity that is not sustainable long term. As the intensity wears off, a core of new behaviors remain and those become part of daily life, without having to think about it too much.

I start by consuming information, then making changes at home but the real test of whether it will translate into

When I downloaded the Dminder app in October in France and it said “148 days until your next Vitamin D exposure”, I suddenly got it. “Honey, we are flying to Mexico for Christmas! There will be people from the internet on the beach. We will know them by their kiniki bathing suits.” And we did. We needed the Vitamin D but we also needed to meet some of this community face to face. It’s much easier to make big changes when you have met others working to do the same.

We should not underestimate the courage it takes to make such a trip, whether it’s in real life or virtual. For all of you who show up; on this forum, on the calls, on the beach, good for you!

Major change is a mind game, doing it alone usually ends in defeat.

At the end of this month I’m going to Florida to the VIP event. Another act of courage. It’s scary to look. It’s tempting to just accept my primary care doctor’s opinion, which is that I’m fine. I’m not sick, but I am not on the road to wellness and I can feel it. I’ll bring my labs and find out how many more changes we need to make, and how quickly.

I have friends around me, dying of cancer and lung disease. Women in the prime of their lives. It breaks my heart. And it motivates me to take radical responsibility.

### caroline — 2019-12-20T06:16:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mother-father-3-children-our-journey-toward-optimal.21281/post-283819>

Thanks for the great update.....it really is all about baby steps!

### Marko Pollo — 2019-12-20T13:54:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mother-father-3-children-our-journey-toward-optimal.21281/post-283821>

And it motivates me to take radical responsibility.\[/QUOTE\]

# Thread: Emerging from Darkness
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 501
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jenelle — 2018-05-04T17:11:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-240495>

It has been on my mind for a little while to start a new journal here ~ as **fantastic** things are happening for me, and I find it so helpful to have a place to collect my thoughts.

I had a journal here before, which I stopped posting on ~2 years ago. I've recently gone back and re-read the entire thing. I debated starting it back up again ~ but no. That just doesn't feel right. There have been too many changes, and I am not that same person.

I may, however, bring up a few things that were sort of left hanging ~ and I may quote or tag someone who dropped an idea in that thread years ago. I am thankful for all of you. Sincerely. You know who you are.

I also want to become a paying member soon. I want to get some "skin in the game" ~ and I'd like to have access to more resources and maybe the facebook group, because I feel there are probably some great things going on there. _However,_ **I** am not on facebook -- that is a can of worms I put the lid on many years ago, and that lid is there to stay. I wonder if I could use an old dummy account my son created a long time ago (which is how I see things on facebook now, when I need to) or if I could create some other alias with a picture that is not me, just so I could have access to the group. Anybody know if that would fly?

Well, I have more to say ~ but I'll save it for later. I'm off to the store for more **salmon**! Hope you all have a great weekend.

P.S. My deep thought for the day:

_...all roads lead back to Jack..._

But do you get access to the facebook group with that one?

### Jenelle — 2018-05-05T15:31:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-240560>

That feeling when you have a lot of great posts running through your head, but you're too busy feeling good & _getting shit done_ ... yeah. That's me this morning.

So I just wanted to pop in and say, [@Scompy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/17718/) -- I discovered your EMF podcast last night. Listened to both parts of your interview with Jack, and it was great. Pretty cool to be able to hear you guys actually talking, instead of just reading you in "internet print".

My youngest (11) was listening in for a lot of it. I said, "Hey, hear that guy talking right now? He's the one who sent us the kale chip." And he was like, "REALLY?!"

Celebrity. You have now achieved celebrity status in our household.  (Of course, Jack was already there.)

I enjoyed it a lot. Thanks, guys!

### Jenelle — 2018-05-05T21:45:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-240577>

Push-mowing the yard for the first time this year. We might just set that burn pile on fire and relax by it tonight.  The weather is perfect for it.

Bought myself this battery-powered mower a couple of years ago, which I love. Pro: I \*never\* have to get a neighbor man to come and start the damn thing for me. Con: the batteries die so fast! I get in a good groove (you know, of course, soaking up the sun) ~ and then -- crap -- gotta take a break. {disappointment}

Need to buy a third battery for it, since one of them has completely died on me this year.

Anyway, so thankful to see all of this GREEN!

### Jenelle — 2018-05-06T15:06:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-240620>

Lit the burn pile last night for a nice, relaxing fire. The neighbor lady behind us started freaking out and called 911 because she thought our house was on fire. THEN she yelled at us through the bushes to ask if we were okay. We were \*all\* out here yelling back at her, telling her, "it's fine -- we've got it -- NO it's not the house!" She didn't listen.

First, the sheriff showed up. He tried to call off the fire departments that were responding, but nope -- here they all came anyway -- full gear, ready to go on a Saturday night, thinking they were responding to a real call for a house fire. It was so ridiculous. We have lived in this house for 15 years, and we have done this every year.

The great news is that it brought some neighbors over for conversation. Lol.

The other great thing is that it didn't even really stress me out. I was incredibly calm, talking to all of these guys who showed up. Yesterday was LOTS of sun + water + push-mowing + salmon + grounding + time with friends. Which also means much less time in my house, and much less time on phone/computer.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-12T22:26:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-241088>

Just finished watching/listening to Jack's 2016 Vermont talk. I appreciate all of the mentions here. It was fantastic.

So many things resonated with me. But the #1 thing I want to comment on is where he said that the meniscus tear in his knee was the best thing that's ever happened to him.

We have been through some shit in the past couple of years. I know, I know ~ everybody goes through some shit. We all have a story.

But when life hands you something you cannot ignore ~ when it is something so enormous that it threatens to change everything you've ever known and done, everything you had pictured for your future... it is in that lowest moment, when you feel everything is at stake, where you can find yourself with a new clarity about life & what is really important to you. It can set you off in a new direction, to a better place ~ one you never could have imagined before.

I'd say Jack's meniscus tear is something we all should be thankful for. I know I am.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-14T21:02:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-241181>

Listened to Vermont 2017 yesterday & today. I really liked the first one, but I think I liked this one even more. Having serious visions of traveling to Vermont.

[@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) I hadn't watched this yet, so had no idea that you specifically recommended the Kiniki brand. Happy with my purchase & not nearly as see-through once they are on. I have to wonder if the people doing reviews on Amazon even tried them on. Duh.

I shared the 2017 talk yesterday with two family members who are always struggling to lose weight. They were excited & said they'd listen. I can only hope they do. And make the changes.

In fact, we had an awesome moment out in the sun at my aunt's house when all of this came up. The discussion started with grounding. My baby niece (5 months old) was having her first feel of the grass on her bare feet. And in her hands. The whole thing had a bit of a magical feel. It was very happy. Instead of the family being shut indoors in the a/c... for a little while anyway, they gravitated out toward the sun. I got to plant some seeds ~ and it didn't feel forced or awkward. Love it.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-15T14:22:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-241220>

Wondering if anyone has seen a discussion about the Panasonic alarm clocks that are supposed to simulate sunrise. For those who have to get up before the sun.

Is there a specific harm in using these? Beyond having something electronic in your bedroom. Do you think it makes things worse instead of better? I can't decide.

I did search the forums before asking. I know it's not optimal. I just wonder if it is actually harmful to your circadian rhythm in some way. Would love it if anyone wants to discuss.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-15T22:36:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-241283>

Ok, first of all, what the heck is wrong with this (cheap, frozen) salmon? It looks like it has been beat up. I've never seen anything like it. I threw it away.

And second ~ I just wanted to celebrate that I swallowed my first ever BIG, fresh, raw **oyster**! All I've ever had before was canned, and I had to put them in a salad or cook them with eggs. That was a couple of years ago.

Don't know if anyone remembers me trying to hide them in a sugar-free chocolate protein powder shake... lmao. I DO NOT recommend. That was so dumb.

I have never seen an oyster this big in my life. I rinsed, salted, cut in two, and shot it down.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-17T22:56:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-241458>

I listened carefully when the question was posed to Jack in Vermont about what bulbs to use in your home. Got it loud & clear: NONE. And that makes perfect sense, and lines up with what I've felt for a long time.

Then he told the black light story re: daughter's birthday party. How all the kids were sleeping better, etc. And he said they will use black light & incandescent at home when absolutely necessary.

That's where I got the inspiration to try the black light bulbs in the windowless kitchen.

Listening to an interview with Andrew Wunsch today, he said another thing which makes total sense to me: He said {paraphrasing}: experiment with different types of light... and if you are repelled by it, avoid it. If you are drawn to it, it is probably good. (They were talking about different red and amber lights in the home).

So ~ the experiment in the kitchen has been great. We don't spend a lot of time in there these days, but when we do have to be in there, it feels good. In fact, when we open the refrigerator or turn on the light on the stovetop, it is repelling. A very noticeable difference. But I can stay in the black light and unload the dishwasher & I am comfortable. Happy, even. I don't think I have ever felt very happy in my kitchen. I like it.

I think we are going to be the weirdest family in town (ha, probably already are) and replace all of the kitchen bulbs with black lights. The rest of the kitchen rarely needs to be that lit up -- but when it does need to be, it will be all black light.

**Unless** someone tells me they once knew a family who did this & everyone exploded. Or something.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-18T15:35:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-241505>

I have been spending a lot more time (outside)  checking the posts here, since I have 24 days of break between semesters of nursing school.

That's something I've wanted to write a big post about, but it's still brewing. Have you ever had a story that is so big, you don't even know where to start?

Yes. I'm sure we all do.

Anyway ~ I just want to say I am inspired by the posts here every day. Thank you all for being here.

I'm off today to visit my 91-year-old grandmother. Her body is still in great shape, but her mind is slipping. A lot. I don't think I saw her last semester at all. And she only lives an hour away.

I visited a week ago, and while she was so happy to have a visitor (and thankfully she let me in her house, lol) ~ she wasn't entirely sure who I was, until we really got to talking. Even then... I did not see any sign of recognition when I started talking about nursing school. This is the same woman who, less than a year ago, was 100% "there" mentally. We had great conversations, and, at the time, I felt a great sense of support from her. She has been that way for me, my entire life. And I have always been sure to let her know exactly how I feel about her, because, I knew ~ someday, this day would come.

I hope you all have a great day. The sun is not shining here, but I am out in it anyway. And the days of sun in the past can carry us through the stormy days in the future.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-18T18:29:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-241519>

Thanks, guys.

My whole plan for today fell through... I was also going to take some things to my niece and have a visit ~ but she's decided to pack up & head out of town. Grandma is out to lunch with my dad today and getting groceries etc. So all of my good intentions just went right down the crapper. Lol.

I guess I will make the trip next week. Now I'm off to Lowe's. I have another crazy experiment in mind.

My grandmother is probably the most active, outdoors, old person I have ever personally known. At 90 she was still out running her chainsaw & weed wacker. She is a symbol of strength in my life, and always has been. Last week when I was there, she'd been out moving concrete blocks in the garage. I offered numerous times to help (you know, I have a love of lifting heavy things, too... lol) -- she refused.

With my mother & my aunt completely in charge of her finances and her care, I don't have a leg to stand on regarding what she is eating and drinking. Most of my family is very mainstream. They wouldn't understand it, wouldn't believe it, wouldn't spend the money, wouldn't follow through. And sadly, I am not in a position to do so. IF I had room for her here -- I would take her in a heartbeat. Even all of her cats. But we are busting at the seams as it is. And I don't think they would let me do it.

I do appreciate the suggestions, however. And the discussion. These are all good things to know. Someday it will be me making the decisions, and then it will be a different picture.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-19T13:34:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-241552>

Thanks, [@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) !

I ended up making the trip to see family after all -- was in the ER with my dad, who is a whole different story. 70 years old, very overweight, blue light toxic, having sudden shortness of breath and dizziness. They admitted him. Lots of tests yesterday & more cardiac tests today.

He listens to me... Sort of. He insisted that I order his dinner last night & I was like, are you sure?! Lol. He got the salmon, steamed green beans, steamed carrots, water. A far cry from his norm.

I came home just long enough to sleep & see the sun rise. Headed back to the hospital ASAP.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-21T19:42:38+0300

+1 of the many good things coming from my return to this site.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-23T16:48:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-241887>

Loving this Kiniki suit.

Sun is rising here now about 6:15 am. I'm always up before it ~ so, no problem getting that in my eyeballs.

The sun comes up over my neighbor's tree fully by about 9:30 am. So I've been laying out on the patio in my suit around that time. Started with 10 minutes each side, and today I'm up to 35 minutes each side.

The rest of the day I've been outside as much as possible ~ in the shade when I feel I need it, in the sun when I feel I need it. Our bodies tell us. Just like all the other animals.

I'm not living in my Kiniki, but it's a nice tool to have in my toolbox. Thanks to all who helped me decide to purchase. Inger suggested this a couple of years ago ~ then life happened, and I lost my way for awhile. It feels good to be back on track.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-242048>

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=242029)
>
> [@Scompy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/17718/) I'd love your input before I pull the trigger on this. I've decided it's much more important than that t-shirt in my cart I'd like to have.

> [https://www.amazon.com/Meterk-Elect...2812&sr=8-2-spons&keywords=emf+detector&psc=1](https://www.amazon.com/Meterk-Electromagnetic-Radiation-Detector-Dosimeter/dp/B0754VVW4W/ref=sr_1_2_sspa?ie=UTF8&qid=1527242812&sr=8-2-spons&keywords=emf+detector&psc=1)

Yeah, it's good to have a lower frequency reader to know if there are wires or such underneath. I don't have this one, but it looks to measure both magnetic and electrical fields to a point. I just bought the TF2 (Trifield) and it's really accurate for the magnetic fields. Some meters can giver higher false-positives in case it invokes any fears. Also, if you know where the actual powerlines run, that's most of the battle. I think you can call a "Dial Before you Dig" and have the power company come out and markup where there are lines in the ground. The sewers pipes will also get marked, etc. I have a place off the side of my current home that has a wire under the ground and it can give off a 2 to 3 milliGauss field on the grass above the ground... In a worse case out by my mailbox, there's a buried transformer and wires along the road underground, so there are spots that are up to 30 milliGauss. The wires also run along a road averaging around 10 milliGauss, and I find it interesting that neighbors always walk on the opposite side of the road at 0.5 milliGauss as if they instinctively know that 10 feet away on the other side have buried wires below.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-27T14:14:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-242113>

I am experiencing ~ possibly for the first time in my entire 39 years ~ what it feels like to have an actual human circadian rhythm.

Getting up with the sun \*and getting it in my eyeballs\* ... spending as much time outside as possible throughout the day and \*not wearing my contacts\* ... taking off my glasses even for short periods of time to \*let my body know what time of day it is and what should appropriately be going on\* ... and serious (SERIOUS) reduction / avoidance of artificial light in all forms ...

\= when the sun goes down, I'm done. Like, just DONE.

I look forward to going to bed and I know that whatever needs to be done will be there in the morning and that I will be fresh and revived and ready to tackle it.

\---

I spent some time yesterday reading back through my old conversations with [@Scompy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/17718/) from 2 years ago. It was very enlightening. He was giving me such great information (!) and such valuable time ... and I was still so stressed & sleep-deprived that I just wasn't able to assimilate it like I can today. It stands out to me like a giant neon sign just flashing in my face: "You weren't getting enough sleep! Your brain was a disaster!" ...But at the time, it was just my norm. I couldn't see it for what it really was.

I see it now.

I feel almost fiercely protective of my sleep these days ~ and I am willing to do just about anything to maintain it. We cannot put all of the other pieces together if this one basic part of the foundation has huge, gaping holes in it.

It just takes about x2 nights for me around 6 hours a pop and I'll be off for the next 5+ days. I learned this back taking Organic Chemistry at Oregon State by having classes at 6:30am MWF, then labs at 10am Tues/Thurs (waking up 3 hours later). That schedule promoted me to get up every morning at different times of the day at 3 hours apart and noticed then "something wasn't right" if I didn't wake up at the same time every morning.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-28T13:31:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-242192>

[@Scompy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/17718/) I completely hear you & relate. I knew nursing school was going to take everything I had in me. This past semester was the heaviest class load I've ever had ~ or will ever have again. Plus it was in winter, which is not my greatest time of year. I had to take microbiology + advanced anatomy & physiology on top of my nursing classes and clinicals. I got through with all A's and B's ... but there were times where I literally thought, "this is killing me". Of course, we all know, it probably was.

Weekdays I've gotten up out of necessity at 5:45 am (latest) since the kids started school {for anyone who doesn't know -- my kids all went to public school for the first time 2 years ago}. But that doesn't mean I was going to bed at a proper time to support that. I was not.

On the weekends I'd always sleep in and try to "catch up". We all know also that this is NOT how it works.

\---

Also wanted to tell you I got my new EMF meter yesterday ~ just in time for a trip to the big city! I was completely geeking out in the car, and the alarm was going off in certain spots. My husband was driving & my youngest (11) was in the back seat. It was really cool because we had great conversations about it. I said, "if this meter can pick it up, you know your body can ~ every cell is affected by this". And they didn't roll their eyes at me. Lol.

What you said about people walking on a certain side of the street -- oh my -- it was so obvious! Out on a country road, we were consistently getting readings, and then it suddenly went back down to zero. I was like, "the power lines switched to the other side of the road -- just like Scott talked about!"

Also had a great moment when my husband asked me to test his phone, because he had the Bluetooth turned on (he thought we were going to listen to music from his phone... but little did he know that the entire trip was going to be EMF nerdery instead, haha). The alarm DID go nuts up against his phone at times. He says it is "pulsing" or looking for a signal in certain spots, so there is a harder pull. Is that a pretty good understanding? Anyway, got his wheels turning about it. I told him that is why I never have the damn thing up against my head. If I do have to have a conversation, I put it on speaker and preferably put it down somewhere that is not even touching my body. And I never have the Bluetooth on, because I have no reason to do so.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-28T13:44:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-242194>

Also I just walked all around the fenced-in dog area where I spend most of my time these days. Very happy to see that the only place where there is any reading at all is within about 1-2 feet of the electric meter on the outside of the house. This is consistent with what I found when I borrowed your trifield.

None of us hang out next to that thing. But some cardinals built a nest on top of it this year and had babies! I was going to post a picture a couple of weeks ago and say, "why don't they know better?" ...but then I thought, birds hang out on power lines, so...

You are definitely upgrading to Ninja status: Nurse NinJa (J for Jenelle). What I mean by this is that you'll be able to spot many nnEMF problem-spots just knowing there's an electrified wire, how electrical engineers have put transformers and wires, cell phone small cell emitters, proximity to walls, frequency of use of nnEMF generating devices and the time-of-day for blasting circadian bio, etc. Keep in mind that your knowledge here can be painful or invoke fear in others. I have to be mindful of my own nnEMF ninja status around my son and always spin things in a positive way. My wife entirely wants nothing to do with my nnEMF mitigation techniques; that may come as a shock, but I respect her desire and never volunteer any information unless asked, so this can force me to invoke more ninja behaviors and preface how I say things with a beneficial sentiment to not alarm her. Someday when the whole of society catches on, I think more people will warm-up to the idea that nnEMFs are simply another toxin, just like preventing forest fires. Then, minimizing exposures will be looked at as a reasonable for a longer term strategy in environments. In the meantime, many will label those that have a deeper knowledge as nerds, half-nuts or full-out nuts in how we're actually helping their worlds without them knowing what this may mean 10 or 20 year down the road. It's an issue I'm finding of "ultra-delayed-gratification" assuming the world catches up with us.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-28T21:12:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-242214>

[@Scompy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/17718/) ~ Agreed ~ and yes, I understand ... I can talk pretty freely with the family members in my own household, but when it comes to extended family, and certainly when it comes to patients, fellow students, etc ... I am always mindful of what I say about health or nutrition, and how it may be perceived. Often I go about my life and say nothing at all. Everyone has their own journey. I will share a piece of information if it is directly asked, of course ... if it comes up naturally and it just feels right to share what I've learned with that person at that time. I might just plant a seed.

After so many years of living outside of the mainstream ~ I feel like I have developed a pretty good intuition about when to speak vs. when to remain silent. In the healthcare setting ~ I am there to do a specific job, and I have no delusions about changing the system or changing the world. Would I like to make a difference in people's lives? Yes, of course ~ and I get to do that in a variety of small ways. I do hope that I can find my niche in nursing where what I am doing does not go completely against everything I believe in. But I don't dwell on that. For now I'm just trying to get through school.

\---

While cleaning the other day, I found this Brainy Smurf from a McDonald's happy meal ~ it says 2011 on the bottom. This made me think of Meryk's t-shirt design, and smile.
_"No, Brainy -- no! That's **not** a Brainy thing to do!"_

### Jack Kruse — 2018-05-29T03:15:33+0300

### Jenelle — 2018-05-29T03:49:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-242256>

I do not think I am immune to the effects. I knew it was going to be the hardest thing I've ever done, on many levels.

However.

Going back to college for a second degree -- in a different field -- after 16 years out of the workforce -- has been one of the most profound experiences of my existence. It awakened a part of me that had been sleeping for a long time.

Recently I read where Jack talked about embracing discomfort. I love that. It's like weight lifting: the muscle must be challenged -- and broken -- in order to grow.

I've learned in these years that I am more resilient than I could have imagined. I have also learned that I am not, in fact, Super Woman. I do have limits. And I'm cool with that.

I don't know where I'm going to end up ~ I just know that I have an overwhelming sense that this is what I need to be doing. That the pieces will fall into place.

So often people try to picture the outcome and plan out their future... and so often it ends up looking nothing like what they imagined. That leads to disappointment and unhappiness.

These days I take each day as each day comes. I am thankful. For each new sunrise, every new piece of information, and each person who touches my life in a positive way. I've been taught that it is not about the destination ~ it's about the journey.

### Jenelle — 2018-05-29T14:51:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-242283>

I've been out walking the dog every morning around 7:00 am. Guess who I see? A couple of smart older ladies.

No, really ... that's it.

Squirrels.

The other day, we saw a SNAPPING TURTLE! He was big. Out sunning himself on someone's driveway. That was a first.

But the people ~ they're all still locked up in their houses ... or rushing off to work in their cars. Even the dogs aren't out. Everyone is missing this magical part of the day. I used to, also. But no more.

### Scompy — 2018-06-04T00:50:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-242640>

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=242272)
>
>

I've seen patterns for a good 15 years of my life. There are many older people in their 70s, 80s and 90s outside at dawn. Many are walking dogs, but many are walking themselves, instead of using walkers or canes. And many of them are not wearing sunglasses or covered up.

This last January just after dawn, I saw a woman running toward me with longer blond hair. From about a half-mile away, she looked maybe in her 20s due to her running speed. I was in the boonies on a dirt road. She was in shorts and a t-shirt. As she approached me, I realized it was a woman in her 70s, maybe even older. But she said hi as she passed and flew by me like she was 25. I wanted to ask her, "Do you think the jogging/running is what keeps you healthy?" I'd suspect she's figured out that running in the mornings is the best thing for her health and she may attribute the running as 90% of the formula and the other 10% to breathing in good air like most of society, not considering the benefits of the light itself.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-07T14:33:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-242922>

My oldest son turns 17 years old today.

[@caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) asked me his thoughts about the Vermont talk. I truly believe the entire experience was life-changing for him.

He did not stay with me for all of the talk by Dr. Boros. He knows nothing of deuterium yet ~ so it just didn't grab his attention. I encouraged him to go out and sit by the lake and bask in the sunshine. He went down the walking trail, too, and got some great pics during this time.

But he was "all there" for Dr. Kruse.

There was a slide that came up where he turned and looked at me with wide eyes and mouth open. "WOW", he says. It hit him. I am hoping that Dr. Kruse will post it here eventually and begin to use it on a regular basis. I may butcher it... but it said something like, "Nature is INTOLERANT of any light that she did not create."

It was good for him to be surrounded by these other young men, walking around barefoot and outside with their shirts off ~ calm, cool, and confident in who they are and what they are doing. It was a world he had not yet seen... and how can you be a part of something if you can't even imagine it? I did introduce him, and I even intentionally left him with them at the Ra Optics table while I went to the bathroom. Lol. But (unlike mom) he's quiet at first, and takes a little while to get to know people. I had visions at that point of traveling to Mexico, and how good it would be for all of us.

He talked on the way home about the life changes he wanted to make. "I want to get up earlier", he says. And he has been. He has been outside more. He's even been taking off his shirt.

The blue blockers & grounding he has always been pretty good at ~ from when I was active here years ago.

But now he is eating more fish. How could you not, when you hear the importance of DHA over and over again ~ and not just from mom ~ but from a neurosurgeon? ...One who is clearly brilliant. He told me he loves when I have cold cooked salmon in the fridge, and he thinks we should have it in there all the time. DONE. ...Done, kid. Hallelujah!

We were there for such a short time ~ we did not get to speak with Dr. Kruse. I did look for an opportunity. We hung around his table for awhile and I got to know some other people there. But he was busy talking deeply with a family who had a lot of questions and clearly needed that time with him. Later, he was busy on his computer ~ and I thought he must be preparing for this epic talk. I was not about to interrupt him.

If we had gotten a good opportunity, I just wanted to introduce myself and my son, and say thank you. Thank you for being here, thank you for your time and your message. Know that each day, your voice is carrying to places you've never been... and having a lasting impact on this new generation who is growing up in a world very different from our own.

He IS a lightning bolt, as he says. I appreciate his style ~ and I know my son does, as well. "Quit shitting around, people. Don't waste my time or yours. Make the changes or get out of the way." If the message were delivered with any less urgency ~ I believe it would not effect true change in people.

\---

We have "moments" all the time. There are too many to share them all. But I'll close with this one from early yesterday morning, while we were hanging out in the sunrise together (possibly for the first time in 17 years):

He works a couple nights a week at Steak N Shake. He was telling me about this guy who always comes to work high on pot. He says, "He didn't know what travel ball was. I had to explain to him what it meant & what we do." I said, "Well, he clearly didn't grow up out in the sun playing travel ball, or he wouldn't be addicted to pot." And he says, "Yeah. He talks about playing video games \*all the time\*."

And, as Dr. Kruse would say:

BOOM.

### caroline — 2018-06-08T11:15:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-242993>

Thank you so much Jenelle and son for all your insights ........What a great journey you are on!

You write very, very well ...so clear and concise and with such feeling and empathy and kindness and love.

You are changing lives ...starting with you and your son. I am so proud of you for making the effort to get you and your son to Vermont .....well done Mom!

There is so much light and life and love in your pictures.......

### Phosphene — 2018-06-08T21:12:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243027>

Jenelle—I’ll agree that your journal is super inspiring. Keep it up! (Yes I’ll be starting one soon. Have a golden opportunity next week so that’s the plan.)

Speaking of gold, I’ve been on the verge of plunging into paid membership (beyond Patreon) but it just seems there is so much other material I still haven’t absorbed. Hard to imagine adding yet more. I keep reading that the webinars help synthesize everything, so I think it’s close to time...

But in the meantime look what I also have on my lap! (First edition from a local library—wonder if the later versions were updated?) I’m partway through—just read about the Hall Effect. Fascinating.

Be prepared—it’s going to piss you off that all this research was done so long ago...

### caroline — 2018-06-09T05:25:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243040>

You guys are inspiring. It is difficult to try to do all the reading and keep on top of all the new information too.

With a gold membership you will keep on top of all Jack's current wisdom and then have access to the live Q&A. \[these are absolute GOLD!\] when everyone can ask anything and Jack distills down the information in terms us non geeks can understand!!!! \[ususally!\]

For all us non geeks .........it is all about the three legged stool. Personally - I just build on that.

I do fall behind a lot sometimes .....because I am also busy trying to live an optimal life.

All of this information is tough sledding for us non geeks - but I do what I can.

If I have a choice between spending the day at the ocean with my partner ....or reading blogs - guess what I pick??!!

on the flip side - we meet incredible people at the ocean. People who instinctively know that this is where they should be. We have great discussions with young and old about all the advantages of being at the ocean/surf and in the sun.

One elderly gentleman walks down the hill to the ocean with a cane \[ he has a disabled pass and could drive down\]. We did talk to him about taking his sunglasses off in the early morning - and now we see him everyday with them off as he walks in the surf.

All the interactions we have are golden ........inspired by all Jack writes!

Spending time with surfers is inspiring ......they are living the dream!

### Jenelle — 2018-06-09T13:51:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243057>

[@Phosphene](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19648/) and [@caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) ~ thank you both for your great posts. I'll be back with more thoughts.

We got a last-minute opportunity to travel AGAIN this weekend ~ just a couple of hours to a baseball tournament for my son. I'm sitting out in front of the hotel, waiting to see the sun.

That's the National Museum of the US Air Force in Dayton, OH.

There is a \*really cool\* old, historic-looking house behind our hotel. Looks like a haunted house you'd see in the movies. I wanted to know more, and I asked the girl at the desk about it last night. She said it was part of the Underground Railroad, and had a network of tunnels underneath that they were forced to close off when the government decided to build the base here.

Listening to her tell the story of the home, we were transported back to a time that we have only heard about in books or movies. Today, cars speed past every day, and probably don't give that house a single thought. I was drawn to it & fascinated by its beauty ... even in a state of disrepair. It's like it was saying, "listen to me ... I have a story".

So many treasures to be found in this world. I love getting out of my box & exploring.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-11T22:10:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243201>

> [Phosphene said:](/goto/post?id=243027)
>

>
> Be prepared—it’s going to piss you off that all this research was done so long ago...
>
> [View attachment 7851](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/7851/)

[@Phosphene](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19648/) ~ thanks again for your post. I am glad to hear it.

I just recently committed to Gold membership, right before our trip to Vermont. I have only listened to two webinars so far, but I can tell you that I loved both of them.

If there is one thing I have been shown about my learning style in the past couple of years, it is this: Things become solid for me when I learn them from a variety of angles/avenues... and also when I am exposed to the information repeatedly.

I get very excited when I am talking about the connections I have made here. **Passionate.** So much of it is deep ~ but so much of it is simple.

If you sit out in nature and close your eyes and just BE ... rain or shine, windy or still ... there is no arguing with the fact that we were created to be connected to the earth. And there is no denying that we have lost that connection in so many tragic ways.

I believe strongly in what Jack is doing. I am appreciative of his voice in this world full of _bullshit and lies_ ... more than I can adequately put into words. So I also choose to speak with my dollars.

I wasn't led back to this site because I have a lot of time on my hands. I was not led here by any other people this time, either ~ and I think that is an important distinction, in my case. I was led back to this site by an inner voice ... something deep within me. And it feels like just exactly where I am supposed to be.

So ~ I will continue to absorb what I can each day ~ and I will look forward to making new connections. I've come to realize that this is a constant journey, and there will be no point when I can say, _"Hey -- I've arrived!"_ ...and I'm okay with that. I have always had a deep curiosity and love of learning, and I love a good challenge. One of the best ways to stay challenged is to hang out with people who know things you don't.

I haven't had a chance to read the book yet! But I'm sure I'll be back with more thoughts about that {apparently pissed off thoughts, lol} when I do.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-14T00:30:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243359>

> [Jenifer Caplan said:](/goto/post?id=243059)
>
> So sonderful that took Your son! I have been trying to get my kids outside and to be more active. It’s an uphill battle.

...And the more they resist... the more you know they really need it.

It takes a lot of effort on the part of the parents, for sure. In the past week ~ we've visited a state park and purchased an annual pass so that we can visit ALL of them this year, if we so choose. I also just purchased a pool membership today, which my youngest has been asking for ~ almost every time I shut down the wifi and suggest that he is not spending enough time outside & that's why he is low on energy and feeling/acting "bored".

Now. We parents must actually follow through with these ideas.

One great thing I can report which may give you hope: I think the more time they spend outside, the more they do crave it and gravitate toward it. Sometimes the middle of the day is just ridiculously hot ~ and I can imagine it's worse in Florida. But that magical time close to sunrise in the morning and sunset in the evening is perfect for outside activities on those days ~ and ALL days.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-14T01:06:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243361>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=243225)
>
> Jenelle - you write beautifully - and simply and to the point. I love your thoughts.
>
> This definitely is a constant journey of learning and living and giving ........I am blown away every single day.
>
> I am so fortunate to have found a partner that also walks the walk and talks the talk ......... but a solo journey is okay too - gives us lots of scope for crazy biohacks etc.!

[@caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) ~ I appreciate you.

And yes, you most definitely are fortunate to have your partner on the same page ~ where you can have stimulating discussions and support one another on your journey together.

I am not solo. ... Yet, I am solo.

There has been an interesting and empowering shift in my thinking lately.

His choices are his own. And my choices are mine. I cannot do all the work and drag him along ~ **nor** will I be held back from my goals and my purpose.

Jack was talking about low-dopamine spouses (I believe) ~ when he made a comment like, _"okay, I'm going to Mexico without you"_. And I looked at my son and laughed and said, _"okay, I'm going to Vermont without you"_. Because that's exactly what I did. And I didn't have a moment of regret or guilt about it. It was perfect ~ just the way it was.

### caroline — 2018-06-15T06:03:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243436>

yep - you need to be good enuf for yourself .....you just cannot drag someone along kicking and screaming. It just does not work and just drags you down.

You need to be as optimal as possible for YOU .....but we all have a responsibility to our children ...and, at least make sure they have the necessary information.

There is a journal in the journal section by a young 16yo girl whose parents dragged her along to Mexico! I will try to find it and get back to you.

She told us that her parents used to pay her to eat oysters! She also says how she was annoyed by her parents talking about Jack all the time!

After meeting Jack she found out the importance of all this - from the man himself .....not from stupid parents!

### Jenelle — 2018-06-15T20:15:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243467>

> [Jenifer Caplan said:](/goto/post?id=243442)
>
> It’s baby’s Eros fur my kids they are combative and u have a husband who will not help I am trying new methods of coaxing. I just need to turn it off and take away the phones my husband gave them phones but they don’t need them in the summer. At least my son will be outside all day so it’s just the night time rules. My daughter is the worst she hates sports and going outside I have to bribe her. Good luck with your son would love to hear progress

We have 3 sons ~ 11, 13, 17.

My husband will listen & back me up. He sees the problem areas most of the time. I just have to point it out sometimes ... shine the right light on it.

The real problem with him is his job ~ always has been ~ apparently always will be. (We'll see.)

When you can't have a normal circadian rhythm, it screws things up in all areas of life. He's just not on our schedule at all.

My oldest who went to Vermont has been very focused on getting up to see sunrise with me these days. In fact, one day I went in his room and said "it's 6:15" and he was like, "6:15?!" because immediately upon waking, he already knew that sunrise was supposed to be at 6:11.  He is in tune with his body enough that he knows all of this makes sense & his body agrees with it. So it just took our trip to get the ball rolling with him. I've seen great changes ... less attachment to the phone ... more time out in the sun & grounding throughout the day. And blue blockers go on & he's done on the phone when it gets dark ~ no prodding required.

The other two will follow suit, if not completely by choice. There have been good changes with them, as well. All screens are off at 8:00 (that's my rule for the moment anyway). They have to go to bed before I do & I'm pretty religious about getting to bed when it's dark, so they are getting to sleep earlier by default. Artificial lighting in the house is used sparingly. Blue blockers are ordered to be put on when necessary.

I have very little tolerance of the PlayStation, and they know it. My youngest bought that with his own money at Christmas time, and my dumb ass allowed it. We had sold ALL of the consoles & games when I was on this site two years ago. Apparently in my nursing school winter haze, I forgot how it can take over their lives. So now, I am reclaiming that time for them ~ like it or not. Don't argue with me or I will throw the damn thing in the trash can. And they know I SO will. Lol.

My middle asked me yesterday about getting the snap chat app. I told him "you are asking me for more social media at a time when I am already thinking you have too much". He didn't like my answer (which was NO without having to say NO). But you know what? He didn't give me a lot of grief about it. He knows. Deep down ... he knows.

### caroline — 2018-06-16T01:36:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243481>

you go girl! You are doing the right things for your sons - do not waiver.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-16T13:38:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243511>

Another thing that's helpful here is that we have not turned on the air conditioning in the house this year -- not once. We've had several days in the 90's, and we do get some days that are pretty "thick" and humid. But I've stuck to windows and fans.

Sometimes (OFTEN in the evening) ~ it feels nicer outside than in our house. So this encourages everyone to go out more, vs. the opposite: where it would be nice & cool in the house and that would make outside feel so much worse. This is a stark contrast to summers past. I can feel the difference in my own body and mind, so I know it is affecting everyone else similarly.

My kids haven't slept with windows open until these past couple of months. We were too worried about noise, or pollen, I suppose. {rolling eyes}. Their windows are open all the time now, and now they've all chosen to keep blinds cracked so the morning sun can come in. I didn't even have to suggest that one.

### Billybats — 2018-06-16T13:53:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243512>

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=243201)
>
> [@Phosphene](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19648/) ~ thanks again for your post. I am glad to hear it.
>
> I just recently committed to Gold membership, right before our trip to Vermont. I have only listened to two webinars so far, but I can tell you that I loved both of them.
>
> If there is one thing I have been shown about my learning style in the past couple of years, it is this: Things become solid for me when I learn them from a variety of angles/avenues... and also when I am exposed to the information repeatedly.
>
> I get very excited when I am talking about the connections I have made here. **Passionate.** So much of it is deep ~ but so much of it is simple.
>
> If you sit out in nature and close your eyes and just BE ... rain or shine, windy or still ... there is no arguing with the fact that we were created to be connected to the earth. And there is no denying that we have lost that connection in so many tragic ways.
>
> I believe strongly in what Jack is doing. I am appreciative of his voice in this world full of _bullshit and lies_ ... more than I can adequately put into words. So I also choose to speak with my dollars.
>
> I wasn't led back to this site because I have a lot of time on my hands. I was not led here by any other people this time, either ~ and I think that is an important distinction, in my case. I was led back to this site by an inner voice ... something deep within me. And it feels like just exactly where I am supposed to be.
>
> So ~ I will continue to absorb what I can each day ~ and I will look forward to making new connections. I've come to realize that this is a constant journey, and there will be no point when I can say, _"Hey -- I've arrived!"_ ...and I'm okay with that. I have always had a deep curiosity and love of learning, and I love a good challenge. One of the best ways to stay challenged is to hang out with people who know things you don't.
>
> I haven't had a chance to read the book yet! But I'm sure I'll be back with more thoughts about that {apparently pissed off thoughts, lol} when I do.

Wow Jenelle,

I wish I could write like you do so beautifully. You took the words out of mouth and heart of what you wrote in this post. Passionate and that inner voice, yes me too.. Next year's Vermont event we all should try and meet, now we know the layout we can say lets all meet by the lake etc..

### Jack Kruse — 2018-06-16T17:12:43+0300

Excellence is the result of caring more than others think is wise, Risking more than others think is safe, dreaming more than others think is practical, and expecting more than others think is possible. Everything that happens to you matters to me. If it wasn't true why am I desperately trying to awaken you here every day? Some of the most unique things worth having in your life come wrapped in a crown of thorns. That is the kind of prick I am.

Thanks [@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) ~ I know that you care. I also know what it feels like to care about something so deeply that you have to take a step away from those who do not want to hear it or take any action. And to direct your energy elsewhere.

Something I have wanted to mention here and I just haven't taken the time: I spoke with your wife Sandy after your talk in Vermont, and I told her that when you got choked up talking about her & how much she has changed ~ man, it got me choked up, too. I wasn't expecting any tears in Vermont... but they came, nonetheless. Because it is DEEP, and it is real, and it is relatable. Knowing that you are moving ahead on your journey & wanting your loved ones to come along, too ~ but knowing also that you will not, that you \*cannot\*, go backwards or stop moving to stay where they are ... I feel it. I feel it every day.

So I cried a few tears of joy for you & Sandy that day. It is so inspiring to see how far you've come ~ and that you've come this far together. Thank you.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-17T13:49:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243580>

Had a birthday party to go to yesterday, and then visited with my parents for a bit. My family is about an hour north of me.

I wanted to take my EMF meter and measure all around my parents' property. But I forgot to grab it, in my rush to get out the door on time.  100% guarantee I will take it with me another day.

Kids and I stopped at the stop sign at the end of their road on the way home. I got out just long enough to take this picture. This is the giant row of power lines that go through the field behind my parents' house. They stretch as far as the eye can see in both directions.

Why did I not stand there and take more pictures? Because one of these huge suckers is RIGHT next to the stop sign. Out in the still quiet of the country, you can hear it -- so loud -- pulsing, whatever you call it ... just burning/zapping with electricity. It was so disturbing to me. I quickly snapped the picture and got the heck outta there.

I spent the first 18 years of my life in that house. The only thought I had about these power lines was that I knew I had to stay away from the towers, never touch them or climb on them. In fact, I never EVER thought about the effect that they may have had on my health (and that of my parents) until recently.

I have thought about being raised around fields of corn & soybeans, and the chemicals that were sprayed on them every year.

But look at these giant, man-made structures that ~ as a child, were just a normal part of my (pretty dysfunctional) world.

Ran into the new health food store to grab a few items ~ came back home feeling out of sorts.

While I was commenting about the fluorescent lights making people hungry ~ and how conveniently that works out for store owners ~ Mr. Vermont Trip says, **"Well, who wears tank tops to the bleeping grocery store?!"** (because he and I both had)

I was like, oh my gosh -- you are SO right! Jack told us to cover up! ...And he's all like, _yeah, I know..._

So he was listening and absorbing even more than I realized.

Jenelle—You and I seem on the same wavelength in more ways than one. (Finally really understand that expression...)

My son and I were kayaking on a local creek last week. Gorgeous sunny day and lovely trip—until we rounded a bend near the end and I saw we needed to float right under giant high tension wires like you pictured. There was nowhere to go but onward so I sped up, but couldn’t resist anxiously staring at the ominous pylons and crackling, hissing, popping wires not that far overhead.

I am no longer religious in the traditional sense but was raised that way (kicking and screaming) and my spine chilled as I noticed the army of Devils glaring down at me from the pylons.

I wanted to take a pic but purposefully left my phone on dry land. I later looked for an image and was surprised at the different pylon configurations out there. Found this one, but could have just referred to yours.

(((SHUDDER)))

### Jenelle — 2018-06-19T20:28:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243807>

I had once cut out coffee completely. Period.

I don't remember for how long ~ but I know it was at least several months.

We went on a vacation to \*Florida\* in MARCH 2014 ... and there was a Starbucks in the hotel.

I decided to drink coffee again ~ and I decided it didn't make me feel bad! I always chalked this up to being on vacation, and my stress being low.

I've been drinking coffee in some form or another ~ in some amount ~ probably every day since.

Today I've been thinking about where coffee beans grow. (Hint: not in Indiana)

EQUATORIAL BELT.

Now, I wasn't in the equatorial belt on this Florida trip. But much closer to it than at home. And also out in the sun more than I had been at home, and of course getting stronger UV.

But I carried the habit back home with me, and now here I am again ~ 4 years later ~ ready to conquer it for good.

I mean, really... the only people who "should" be drinking coffee are those who live where the beans grow naturally -- and, from a mitochondriac perspective -- maybe not even them?

Anyway ~ tomorrow is the day. I've decided.

I'd love to hear your positive experiences with quitting coffee. Just don't tell me any health benefits of coffee, please. My addicted brain would probably latch right onto that! Lol.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-20T00:43:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243826>

The very first thing Dr. Kruse ever said to me was in February 2016:

"Procaffeination = a toxic light environment = loss of electron flow in ECT. Read the end of the Ubi 4 blog. Right there."

Also:

"coffee = a person who is light stressed and has no proper melatonin recycling so they use caffeine to increase ECT speeds to do what missing melatonin can do. Time 9 breadcrumb."

And:

"creatures of habit = low dopamine levels"

\---

I wasn't in a good place then. I didn't really GET IT like I do now.

I see on my old journal that I tried to go \*completely\* caffeine-free (no decaf, tea, or chocolate, either) ~ about 6 weeks after the above posts ~ and I lasted 9 days. If I had to guess... I'd say probably 9 utterly miserable days. Lol.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-20T14:52:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243866>

I've been talking for weeks about expanding the dog's fenced area of our yard & adding a couple of privacy panels ~ now that I am spending almost all of my time in that backyard area, and some of that time in my Kiniki.

This will allow us more room to roam ~ more access to direct sunlight & the shade of the one tree, when needed.

All of the kids' baseball was cancelled last night due to rain, and I finally grabbed hold of a couple hours of my husband's time to help me with this. So I must share my ONE panel.

It's a work in progress, but I'm excited about it. It means good things.

### Inger — 2018-06-20T17:44:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243881>

well I stopped last two times in fall as my main work ended and I had way more time home, and then on vacation to Spain this winter at my sisters home, we stopped together. Each time it lasted about a month or so. It never was hard for me to stop, like my body is just fine with it and I do not get headaches or stuff. I more have these psychological withdrawals, you know?
In the first days and maybe week. If I am at work these days, it is hard, because of the stress and nnEMF. I there use it to push me I guess….which is of course not good.
I have only been having one cup in the morning and then 1 or 2 espressos at work.

I have to work on why I use it to comfort me... or in stressful situations. It just is no good. Now I stopped Monday, and had have no coffee since then except… yeah.. one half cup at work yesterday. With cream. I just needed some fat calories.. I always get that feeling when I clean the home of the Vet, my second work place. I just crave fat after a while!
My plan is to take something fatty with me to eat then. And have lots of water, and something salty. That should help.
Same at my main work place as a waitress. I also love to take some nice favorite herb tees with me to drink when I need to comfort myself  It helps in the beginning, to just drink a nice herbal tea instead!

After a few weeks sans coffee, if I drink it, I do not like the taste at all anymore, it tastes bad to me. Weird! But if I just drink it anyway, I soon start to like it again..lol

I feel my sleep is even deeper without coffee. I like how it makes me feel. I just know deep inside, it is better for me not to have the coffee habit!
Now, lets do it

### Jenelle — 2018-06-21T19:04:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243979>

[@Inger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1057/) ~ thanks for sharing your thoughts. I hear you. It seems this coffee story is a deep one, for all who can relate.

I'm doing it.  I can't say I feel particularly happy about it at this point ~ but I will persevere. Whether that is for another 30 minutes or 30 years remains to be seen. Lol.

I do get headaches. And I hate how the withdrawal makes me feel. Not like myself.

So far (1.5 days in) ~ I would call this experiment: **humbling**.

I think addictions can give us a sense of weakness while we are chained to them ~ but the full impact of that weakness does not hit until we are truly struggling against those chains.

Today, I've been back and forth between moments of _"I've got this"_ and moments of _"I'm going to make some coffee right now"_.

It is very rainy here today. No sign of the sun at all.

Yesterday went okay. Although I did have what some would probably consider a cheat.

I haven't had any chocolate for a very long time. I was studying out in the sun at school with a friend, when she decided to go in to the bookstore and get a snack. I discovered this little bar, which says it has the same caffeine as one cup of coffee.

On the back, it says:

_"Consume one piece of AWAKE and you'll be ready for operating a calculator or possibly slicing a small ham. Two pieces, and you're ready for giving your cat a pill or explaining Tinder to your mom. Three pieces and you're 3/4 AWAKE -- ready to win a tap dance battle, re-tile a spacious bathroom, or fell a mighty oak. Eating four pieces of AWAKE will have you ready to swim the length of Lake Michigan, memorize the complete works of Shakespeare, or vibrate at a frequency that allows you to pass through solid objects."
_
Well ~ I thought ~ we'll see about that.

...It **was** pretty great.

However ~ I **did** finally purchase a Kiniki suit ~ which **you** suggested to me a couple of years ago. Thank you!

### Jenelle — 2018-06-21T20:05:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243984>

Last night, I had one of only **two** clinical experiences that will have me awake past sunset this summer. I was in the NICU from 7-11 pm.

I don't remember if I've talked about it here ~ but I have already _firmly_ decided that I won't be working night shift when I graduate.

So, I don't know what that means about my future job opportunities ~ but it's not something I am willing to budge on. I know I don't need to go into any explanation with anyone here about that.

That may mean I don't find something right away. It may mean I only find something PRN days. It may mean that I just keep working toward my Bachelor's or my Master's and see where that road takes me. We shall see. One day at a time.

I've also had an obvious aversion to working in a hospital setting after reading all of Jack's recent posts, and being back on this forum, and really thinking about the EMF soup I'd be subjecting myself to. Not just that -- but working on upper floors of a hospital -- further disconnected from the Earth -- where they never go outside. Nurses talk on and on every day across the internet about not even being able to pee, not getting a lunch, etc etc ... for 12+ hour shifts. How this got to be "the norm" is beyond me ~ but sadly, it seems to be the reality for many.

I've considered many other avenues, and I've been thinking outside the hospital box. There are so many things you can do as an RN. I don't know how realistic some of my ideas are -- but I do believe that there is an opportunity out there for me that I just don't know about yet. I can feel it.

Some people feel safe & comfy having their future all mapped out in their mind. But when you've had the experience of a giant bear coming out of the woods, taking a huge dump on your map, tearing it to shreds, and then throwing it in the river ... you stand there for a minute like, _"wtf just happened?"_

**...And then...** you have to figure out how to get places without the map. If you're really resilient ~ you don't spend too much time lamenting about how your map is gone, because you are just glad that the bear didn't decide to EAT your ass in the process. Not only that, but you may decide that following that stupid map was actually pretty boring. All of the other people were on those trails. Creating your own trail is much more exciting.

Anyway  ~ my time in the NICU was fine ~ but it just further cemented what I have already been feeling. I drank a bunch of water as soon as I got out of there, came home, and went straight to bed.

The classmate I was there with told me she was going to Planet Fitness to work out.

### drezy — 2018-06-21T21:10:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-243986>

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=243984)
>
> I've also had an obvious aversion to working in a hospital setting after reading all of Jack's recent posts, and being back on this forum, and really thinking about the EMF soup I'd be subjecting myself to.

Yeah, I've been wondering about your line of work and decisions in this regard.

> but I do believe that there is an opportunity out there for me that I just don't know about yet. I can feel it.

I wish you good luck with that discovery.

> Some people feel safe & comfy having their future all mapped out in their mind. But when you've had the experience of a giant bear coming out of the woods, taking a huge dump on your map, tearing it to shreds, and then throwing it in the river ... you stand there for a minute like, _"wtf just happened?"_

From experience, if it happens enough you actually have a "Yep,It figures!" response. Then you start suggesting how the bear can even more epically destroy your map. Because if it's getting destroyed anyway it might as well be entertaining.

Think instead:
"Excuse me Mr Bear. I get it. I know what you're going to do. That's maybe part of the problem, see? You might want to differentiate yourself from all the other bears. You know, create a brand of map destruction. Brand man! I hear it's pretty much what momma bears are into this season. Just FYI if you want some ursine action. You're the bear, not me, but what I would do is right after dumping on the map do that dog butt dragging thing completely across it because 1: A bear doing it is epic and 2: I'm left with the dilemma of whether I need to make the painstaking decision of abandoning those life plans forever even though they are technically in reach."

> The classmate I was there with told me she was going to Planet Fitness to work out.

There are people that I'm close to but not close enough to. I drop little tidbits, but I can't avoid coming to the conclusion that most people need to crash before ears open.

I've been very lucky to change my little 3 member family (including me) as well as my buddy who is the legal guardian of my son (should my wife and I get hit by a bus). That dude is a blue eyed far North haplotype for sure. Once I convinced him to try the cold he loved the results and felt younger (mostly joints) immediately. I was able to get him to lay off the sunscreen for biking and jogging and move sessions to expose himself to AM light. I feel like Ihit a 777 on a slot machine and I am very grateful for what Doc teaches and how it's helped people I love.

Remain vigilant Jenelle and at least get the bear to butt drag across your next life plans whenever they get demolished. How's that for a visual?

### caroline — 2018-06-22T04:09:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244016>

I have always had great intuition but was to afraid to follow it - in case it is a dumb ass thing to do ...to woo woo etc.

Who follows their intuition for big life decisions anyway?? well.....I do!

That is not to say we don't do lots and lots of due diligence ....but when our intuition speaks - we listen! and btw - it seems without a moment's hesitation!

Later - we will go "what just happened there?????? yikes - did we really just do that? commit to that? well ...yes we did and it was a fantastic outcome - for the moment anyway.

I think life is a moving target- be prepared for the ride of your life........

I have done so many things in the last 5 years that I wouldn't have thought possible for me .........I keep pushing the envelope and there is lots of sunshine and hope and love and inspiration for so much more!

sitting beside me right now is a wonderful, loving \[and very sexy may I just say\] man that I met on eharmony.....I sure never saw that coming!

### Jenelle — 2018-06-22T15:58:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244045>

THIS is part of why I no longer want the coffee to be at _addiction_ status.

At home, I use only organic coffee made with RO water. When I'm out and about ~ I tend to compromise. I reach the point where (almost) any coffee will do.

That's not even because I "need" it so much ~ I am not sleep-deprived, my blood sugar is not all jacked up, I've got a good rhythm going on, and I am not drifting off during the day.

It's just because it is (was) such a strong habit.

\---

[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/fluoride.10149/page-3#post-197892](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/fluoride.10149/page-3#post-197892)

"Excellent rule of thumb: If you are craving coffee, your lighting environment isn't optimal. Pro-caffeination = an environment that cause elevated ubiquitin marking because of a lack of UV/IR light and too much blue light day and/or night. So let's talk about a bio-hack I did in 2014. So if you are using "bulletproof coffee" or you frequent Starbucks in any municipality in the USA you are getting preloaded with tons of fluoride. When I was in Pasadena at the Bullet proof conference people kept asking me why I would not try any of the coffee they provided free of charge. I told them the water source was the problem. I was told the water was believed to fluoride free because it was shipped in.......which I did not believe so I refused. I went further. I took a sample home and sent it to a lab and found it did have fluoride in it as I expected. Why should you be careful with any coffee product or any hot beverage you consume on the road that uses water? Because the higher the temperature of the water, the more fluoride we absorb into our cells. I've known about this because I was dentist and learned this in 1986 when I was in dental school. Fluoride is a dielectric blocker in cell water and is associated with calcium efflux in the pineal gland because it discharges voltages that can be stored in water’s hydrogen bonding network. A dielectric material is an insulator not a semiconductor and does not conduct DC electricity. Water in EZ form acts like a semiconductor. A nerve cell (or indeed any cell) is surrounded by a plasma membrane, made of phospholipid. The cell can be seen as two electrically-conducting regions filled with EZ water, namely the cytoplasm and the extracellular fluid; both regions are electrolyte solutions which are separated by a thin layer of insulator. This is the plasma membrane of the cell. The cell membrane therefore acts as a capacitor and so does EZ water! That said, when a dielectric material like water, is put together or adjacent to a capacitor, depending on the dielectric constant of the material (bulk water is high at 78 EZ = 160), when a DC potential is placed across a capacitor (cell membrane), the charged components of the dielectric will move to either side of the capacitor and hold a voltage equal to that of the potential placed across it. This voltage can be charged or discharged. Dielectric blockers decrease voltage stored in waer decreasing energy available to the cell.

KEY COFFEE/ DENTAL/WATER ALERT: A dielectric blocker, like fluoride, discharges the DC current that is normally stored in cell water and the extracellular water around cells. In this way, fluoride, lessens the battery capacity of bulk or EZ water. Water ceases to be an ideal repository for light or any other electromagnetic waves. So this becomes a double whammy situation for ruining your electrical system in a cell. The moral of the story: trust no one, test water, and make sure your water is pure by checking municipality records on line. If you drink coffee without knowing if the water is good you should drink ice coffee and not hot coffee to lower the amount of fluoride you intake. There was one problem with this in Pasadena California in 2014: drinking "bulletproof coffee cold" tastes nasty because the butter solidifies. This bio-hack comes from my upcoming book. Consider it my "kevlar vest" bio-hack for your bulletproof life. [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3474549/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3474549/)"
