### Jenelle — 2018-06-22T17:26:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244056>

Yes. Intuition. Thank you, ladies.

I started a big post to [@drezy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19879/) yesterday about how, when I was young, I wanted to be a pediatric nurse. That's it. Anyone asked me, that's what I said.

I didn't have any nurses influencing me. The only nurse in my family is my aunt ~ who did not live near us at the time, and whom my mother was not close with. There has always been some ... competition there, some resentment. So I did not grow up close to this aunt, or any other nurses.

Another thing I remember is watching the Peace Corps commercials as a child and telling my parents I wanted to do that. And I remember my mother laughing at me.

I reached a point in high school where I was struggling in math & science classes, and I vividly remember the day when my mother told me that nursing required A LOT of math and science, and that _maybe I should consider doing something else_.

I believe my intuition became completely buried at that point.

My mother, as a CPA herself, encouraged me in all sort of accounting jobs ~ which, admittedly, I am naturally good at. However, I felt like a rat in a cage at each and every one of them ~ something I understand today WAY more than I did then. I thought it was just being tied to a desk. I wanted to be free. I wanted to be on the move. I now know it was not just that ~ but also sitting there, staring at a computer screen all day, under fluorescent lighting.

I also completed a degree in Business Administration, which I have done absolutely nothing with. Because my parents encouraged me to have "something to fall back on".

I have been home with my children and pretty much free to do as I please since November 2000 when I quit my last office job.

In October 2016, when my husband's back got to the point where he could no longer stand up straight, when I was seeing him in constant daily pain, and we were living in fear daily of him being fired from his job ~ my intuition took over in a big way. It brought me clarity and and an ability to make decisions I would not have considered before. It was a blessing in disguise.

Not only did I get into the nursing program ~ I did it with the highest score my program chair has ever seen on an application.

Not only am I doing well in the nursing program ~ people look to me for answers. To all sorts of questions. They know I stay on top of things. I've been able to be a source of motivation, and strength, and LIGHT to those who are struggling.

And the entire experience has been a catalyst for growth and change in me that occurs daily. I enjoy interacting with patients from all walks of life, and pushing myself OUT of my comfort zone to embrace the unknown. I spent way too many years in a state of monotony and staleness. I refuse to go back to that. It would be like trying to jam an elephant into a mason jar at this point.

I'm out of the jar, folks.  Some may worry that I have just traded up for a much larger jar ~ but if so, I will feel it. And I will break the glass accordingly. I now know that I am more than strong enough to do so.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-23T18:21:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244165>

DAY 4 NO COFFEE check-in: for my future self, and for others.

I have not been utterly miserable.  In fact, I had to stop and really think about how many days it has been before I started this post.

I have used some acetaminophen (in the first couple of days) and some dark chocolate to ease the transition ~ neither of which I was consuming before ~ nor do I want to continue. _I don't eat sugar._ If I'm going to keep the dark chocolate, it needs to be 100% cacao nibs ... or something like that.

Had a bridal shower for my niece last night. I come from a family of big coffee drinkers, and of course it was available. It didn't phase me. I stuck with water, and that was great.

I think today is probably the first day I got up and didn't miss it. All of the other days, yes it did feel like something was missing. Like an arm, or maybe a leg. Lol. HABIT.

One concern I had with the coffee drinking is that I knew I was reaching for it at times when I probably really needed water ~ but I did it anyway. I am drinking much more water now, because it's the only option.

Another concern I had was that it was possibly having a constant, negative effect on my digestion. I don't remember for sure what was going on with my digestion prior to resuming coffee on the Florida trip in March 2014 ... but I can \*definitely\* say that my digestion has not been great over the past 4 years. I've done ALL sorts of different things with food ~ but the one constant has been coffee. I would eat (and not eat) just about anything ~ if I could just keep my coffee.

At this point, I can tentatively say that my concerns were correct ~ and that listening to Jack's posts + my intuition on this one is going to pay off big time.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-24T17:36:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244243>

Thanks [@Joyfun](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/21372/) for your encouragement.

Funny ~ I have seen many (MANY) posts on the nursing forum (which I no longer visit) ... women asking, "Am I too old?" to start nursing school. Some a decade younger than me! And I can honestly say it's a question that never entered my mind. Not ever.

I believe you really are as young as you feel. I feel pretty good. Even on my _worst_ day ~ I think I am healthier and happier than many of the girls in the program who are close to half my age.

My mom went back to college to get her CPA when I was 11... so she must have been 42. I hated it as a child ~ but I certainly learned to appreciate it as an adult. After my mom had earned her degree, my dad was pretty much forced into early retirement after being off work for a torn Achilles tendon. Had she not done what she did, when she did it ~ I don't know where they would be today.

I come from a stock of strong women. There is no doubt about that. And I am thankful for it each and every day.

### Mhoej — 2018-06-24T22:47:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244255>

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=244045)
>
> THIS is part of why I no longer want the coffee to be at _addiction_ status.
>
> At home, I use only organic coffee made with RO water. When I'm out and about ~ I tend to compromise. I reach the point where (almost) any coffee will do.
>
> That's not even because I "need" it so much ~ I am not sleep-deprived, my blood sugar is not all jacked up, I've got a good rhythm going on, and I am not drifting off during the day.
>
> It's just because it is (was) such a strong habit.

What is your main obstacle in giving up coffee? Severe caffeine withdrawals? I drank coffee every single day from teenager to early 30's. Had tried to quit several times before but always caved in because of fever/vomit/splitting headaches for days on end from the resulting withdrawals. It's like a hangover whilst you have fever and migraine. I read Jack's take on caffeine, found then and there I needed to quit it and a tip online that I followed was supplementing with Tyrosine and Phenylalanine 2x daily for 5-7 days. Almost no withdrawals. Haven't had any caffeine since then, haven't looked back at all and been more than a year. Whilst no fan of supplements I was happy with the result of kicking the habit that easy and fast. Coffee smells kinda gross now, used to love the smell of a fresh brew.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-24T23:40:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244256>

> [Mhoej said:](/goto/post?id=244255)
>
> What is your main obstacle in giving up coffee? Severe caffeine withdrawals? I drank coffee every single day from teenager to early 30's. Had tried to quit several times before but always caved in because of fever/vomit/splitting headaches for days on end from the resulting withdrawals. It's like a hangover whilst you have fever and migraine. I read Jack's take on caffeine, found then and there I needed to quit it and a tip online that I followed was supplementing with Tyrosine and Phenylalanine 2x daily for 5-7 days. Almost no withdrawals. Haven't had any caffeine since then, haven't looked back at all and been more than a year. Whilst no fan of supplements I was happy with the result of kicking the habit that easy and fast. Coffee smells kinda gross now, used to love the smell of a fresh brew.

Hello ~ thanks for your reply.

Every time I have quit (or gone to decaf) in the past, this is how I describe it to people: "I feel so much better, more relaxed, for a couple of weeks ~ and then I can't stand the mental fog & not getting anything done."

BUT. I think this time will be different. I've never had my life in this sort of order before. I could go back now and clearly point out all of the reasons why I felt that way.

I'm figuring out how all of the pieces fit & I reached a point where I was ready to tackle it. Five days now & I'm doing well. Drinking & enjoying lots of cold water.

Tyrosine and I are not friends. I have the book The Mood Cure by Julia Ross from years ago ~ and at one point, probably owned and experimented with ALL of the amino acids she recommends for various things. None of that ever went well. I now know the true reasons for that. My environment SUCKED & there is no pill for that. I \*know\* ~ you were just suggesting it as temporary help & I do appreciate it.  And thanks for sharing your quitting story ~ I am always glad to hear that people have kicked it long-term & are still feeling great without it. I think that's a good indication that you are doing the right things.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-27T16:25:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244428>

So ~ I made it 6 days this time without coffee.

Replacing it with dark chocolate is not going to work for my gut right now. And I don't want the sugar anyway. Yet that is the direction my brain took me without coffee.

This is telling. I thought I'd made so much progress ~ I could just ditch it. Ha!

But I had a big lightbulb moment about this yesterday. I realized while listening to the Q&A: this is not some sort of personal failure ~ _this means my environment still sucks for me_.

Even spending almost all of my time outdoors ... I am still in a neighborhood. When we do turn on the wi-fi, and I look at the list of options to choose from ~ there are anywhere from 10-20 (?) on there at any given moment.

And then there is my location in general. We have a LONG-ass winter, and I hate every second of it. We have rain. Lord, we have rain for DAYS. I see the little lightning bolts lined up when I check the weather app, and just looking at it makes me feel depressed. I get excited about the sun like a kid at Christmas. Seriously.

I've been drawn to this thread a lot:
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...sort-of-protection-from-non-native-emf.11650/](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/theory-caffeine-and-or-nicotine-offer-s-some-sort-of-protection-from-non-native-emf.11650/)

And there are some gems in there:

I need to read Energy & Epigenetics 4.

"They both increase the current of electrons on ECT so they increase the magnetism of you mitochondria."

I need to read Tensegrity 5.

I need to eat even MORE seafood.

"there are no shortcuts.....just beliefs they exist"

### Jenelle — 2018-06-28T02:22:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244470>

Well, this is long overdue. Can't believe I didn't think of it sooner.

I will have this with me while I listen to every podcast, webinar, Q&A, and while I am on the forum, and reading books, and reading the blogs. So... basically, I should just tuck it into my Kiniki when I roll out of bed each morning.

Jenelle, 6 days are great!   What I do right now is, at work I do just one tiny espresso… one. No more. At home it is not hard for me to be without coffee.

And as I biohack and work on me and figure how to improve things, I am sure I will not need the 1 espresso at work either

I think too much pressure is also not good… baby steps   always improving, always learning  we know the tools

Meditation is great! Just to be silent and empty. For me it is so important. But I am sure you use that tool too, it kind of goes hand in hand with connecting to nature and all things living

### Phosphene — 2018-06-29T13:50:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244551>

My first thought was that it could be a bolete. These are cool shrooms but hard to identify properly. If you had picked this one the stem might have turned blue where it was bruised or cut. (Yes some mushrooms “bleed” blue and now I’m curious to learn why.)

I’ve spent some time with the local mushroom club—a mitochrondiac hobby if there ever was one... I plan to muse more about this in my journal.

[http://www.mushroomexpert.com/boletus\_pseudosensibilis.html](http://www.mushroomexpert.com/boletus_pseudosensibilis.html)

Hi [@Jenelle](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/18778/) and [@Phosphene](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19648/),
I might be singing to the choir, but just in case someone does not know, you can raise the level of vitamin D2 (not D3) in mushrooms by placing them gill side up in the UV rays of the sun.
After I entered the above, I googled this topic and came up with mixed messages about this. Actually, I do not feel I was getting good information on anything I was reading.

### Jenelle — 2018-06-29T20:20:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244576>

> [Phosphene said:](/goto/post?id=244066)
>
> I know all about Big Thoughts...
>
> My initial (and admittedly idealistic) one here is that The Farm and eventually other places like it will certainly need nurses??
>
> In the meantime there are smaller specialized clinics that require nurses too. A good doctor friend of mine got a bit further out of the rat race by working at one for varicose veins. i Imagine those jobs are at a premium though and you’ll need some experience first. You’ll figure it out.

I wondered about this before you posted it... "will they employ RNs at The Farm?"

And, outside of that ~ I admittedly have no clue what other opportunities (like it) may be out there.

I've also wondered if there would be an advantage in some settings to hiring a new grad, one who likes to think outside the conventional box ~ and who is perhaps more malleable, instead of already set in their ways. I would have a lot to learn ... but I would not have a lot to UN-learn.

I spoke with an MD who posted on a nursing forum several months ago. It sounded like a dream job to me ~ while many nurses were bashing this poster about the amount of money she was able to offer. If the location had been south instead of further north {groan} ... it was something I think I would have felt good about and would have strongly considered.

Thanks [@drezy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19879/) ~ we bought the cheapest panels & we're doing it in our usual, "probably not most correct" way. Lol. But as long as it stays standing, I'm happy.

Your comment about liking to seem useful was thought-provoking to me. I know you are right & it is something I have thought about before... but now I'm thinking about it some more.

I have \*had\* to be very independent due to the nature of my husband's job. Besides the fact that it's just how I'm built.

I think we all want to seem useful. I think it is probably harder for the guys ~ maybe all of my guys ~ especially with school out for summer. With your comment in mind, I will be on the lookout daily for opportunities to fulfill that need for them. Thanks again.

Finally finished & tied into the dog's existing fence. This is the view from the patio where I spend my Kiniki time each day. There's a sunroom on the other side of the patio, so between the two ~ this gives me a much greater \*feeling\* of privacy, anyway.

That dirt spot is where the dog's fence line used to be. He is completely thrown off by this new arrangement ~ but he'll get used to it.

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=244722)
>
> That dirt spot is where the dog's fence line used to be. He is completely thrown off by this new arrangement ~ but he'll get used to it.

That looks great.

It's telling when animals get confused by outdoor humans. I have a roadrunner that complains to me every day I'm outside, a rabbit that hops within 5 feet of me every 6:15 - 6:30 AM light session almost like clockwork, and a hummingbird that stops to look at me for a few seconds regularly. I swear they are all thrown by seeing a two legged animal out there with them.

### Jenelle — 2018-07-04T20:28:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-244971>

Okay, folks ~ I know there are at least a few people posting here who have recently become paying members, like me. So you've got this new treasure trove of things to listen to. I just want to say:

LISTEN TO NOVEMBER 2017 WEBINAR.

I just finished, after seeing Jack say it was #2 in importance after the April 2016 (which I have already listened to).

Each of us are spoken to by different things in different ways at different times ... but I feel like this one has to speak to everyone ~ in some way, regardless of where you are on your journey.

This feels like a HUGE piece of the puzzle to me for why some people feel so much better on a carnivore / "zero carb" way of eating ... and why they feel so good, they can continue it for years and years ~ leaving plant foods behind for good. They don't care whether it is ketogenic (although it often is) ~ that's not the focus.

Deuterium depletion.

I believe that's what it comes down to, after listening to this webinar.

### Jenelle — 2018-07-08T18:05:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-245332>

The other day, husband says: "You look like you belong on a beach somewhere."

Me: "MAYBE I DO."

He's also called me a "tan beast". I'll take it ~ especially since these muscles being revealed were created long ago, and are not currently in active training.

I think I once actually believed that if I missed a lifting session, they would magically disappear overnight.

### Jenelle — 2018-07-08T22:10:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-245366>

I also just want to say that I have not put a contact lens onto my eyeball for at least 7 weeks now. Not once. And I don't intend to.

Well-meaning people tried to tell me to ditch contacts AND glasses 2 years ago ... and I was just like ... what? I'm blind. Lol.

I didn't understand and I didn't have the mental clarity to put all of the pieces together at the time.

I was also \*frequently\* in an overstimulated state (which was just "my normal") ... and I reached a point every day where I could not stand the feeling of having glasses on my face.

I don't feel that way anymore. It honestly just hasn't been an issue. And my eyes feel SO much better.

I do believe vanity was a key player back then, as well. I made the effort to wear my glasses at home more, sure ~ and went without them in the AM sun ~ but wear them out in public?! Ha, I dunno ....... I doubt it.

^ I'm over that. I don't wear contacts, and I don't wear makeup. We had a big family wedding yesterday, and I didn't even think about using either of those things. It hit me on my sunrise walk this morning, how much I have changed ~ inside & out.

Feeling grateful.

### Jenelle — 2018-07-12T13:29:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-245715>

Did 7 days of tracking phone & computer time, just to see what averages for a typical week look like right now.

Phone time is checking email & this forum, pretty much. I didn't track texting time, but that is infrequent and sporadic ... not like I sit down and text for 5 minutes straight. I also didn't count when I am just listening to podcasts or webinars and not touching my phone.

Computer time is always with a purpose and never mindless roaming. School work I have to do, taking care of the checkbook and spreadsheets, ordering things from Amazon, specific emails or messages that need to be sent.

Phone (iPhone 7): 1 hr 36 minutes per day

Computer (apple laptop ~ nowhere near my lap): 1 hour 42 minutes per day

Total: 3 hours 18 minutes per day

(Always outside ... it's a personal rule)

It adds up! That's about 20% of my awake time.

Good experiment & need to do it again sometime. It did make me more mindful of my choices.

### Phosphene — 2018-07-12T14:15:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-245718>

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=245366)
>
> I also just want to say that I have not put a contact lens onto my eyeball for at least 7 weeks now. Not once. And I don't intend to.

I could have written almost every word of this post. Thanks for doing it for me!

I admit to using my contacts a few times a month over the past year, but as I virtually lived in them since age 14 this is a vast improvement. A few times for vanity, but mostly for swimming or an advanced yoga class where actually seeing the instructor is a must. I’m pretty blind (-6 in both eyes, mysteriously improved from -8 through no effort of my own before finding this site) but so grateful I didn’t get lasik surgery done. Plus one for intuition on that!

### Jenelle — 2018-07-12T14:38:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-245724>

> [Phosphene said:](/goto/post?id=245718)
>
> I could have written almost every word of this post. Thanks for doing it for me!
>
> I admit to using my contacts a few times a month over the past year, but as I virtually lived in them since age 14 this is a vast improvement. A few times for vanity, but mostly for swimming or an advanced yoga class where actually seeing the instructor is a must. I’m pretty blind (-6 in both eyes, mysteriously improved from -8 through no effort of my own before finding this site) but so grateful I didn’t get lasik surgery done. Plus one for intuition on that!

Yep. Got my first contacts at age 11 and they were the hard, gas permeable type. My eyes were so irritated all the time in middle school ... a friend's father seriously quizzed her about whether I smoked pot.

I never did the surgery A) because we never had the chunk of money for it and B) because -- EYEBALLS! -- lol ... no thanks. I'd have had to have a major dose of sedative to hold still for it. And it just never felt like the right thing to do. Was tempting at times. Never enough to seriously look into it.

My prescription is around -7 in both eyes. One is a little worse than the other. I've been wearing my older glasses that I think are a weaker prescription, but I haven't called to verify that. I know I put my newer ones on the other day for a minute and it made me feel a little strange. They went right back in their case.

If the location had been south instead of further north {groan} ... it was something I think I would have felt good about and would have strongly considered.

I've still been slowly working my way through Becker's book, as time allows. I love it.

Last night as I was reading, I remembered this conversation with the MD. So I went to read it again.

It's a psychiatrist who is using transcranial magnetic stimulation for the treatment of major depression. Going into private practice and was looking for an RN to deliver the treatments, due to the (very small) risk for seizures.

[@Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) ~ I would love to hear your opinion about this type of treatment. I will also do some searches.

### Jenelle — 2018-07-15T17:05:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-245923>

Putting together a Power Point for a group presentation for class. My part is adolescent growth and development.

I think I'll leave this part OUT (from our textbook):

_"Because some adolescents spend a great deal of time outdoors participating in athletics, it is a critical time for them to avoid excessive sun exposure so they don’t develop skin cancer (i.e., melanoma) from ultraviolet rays."_

Just one example of the many unfortunate things I've encountered this week.

### Jenelle — 2018-07-15T19:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-245928>

All through my morning walk today, I was thinking about **should** vs. **must** ...

I personally don't feel the pressure of what _others_ think I "should" do, as much as I feel the tug of war each day in my own mind.

There are things I feel I should be doing, but other things I must do first. ...Maybe I will get to the thing I feel I should do, and maybe I won't. But the musts ~ well, they must get done.

So it is the **must** who we end up being. How we spend each day, what we prioritize... what we get done. Action.

If you are living a life of "must" ~ no one really sees your "shoulds". When they become important enough to you, they become a must, and you take action.

If you are living a life of "should" ... well, I think I've already been there, done that. And in hindsight ~ I can say that it's a miserable place to be.

How many people walking among us each day are stuck in the should?

### caroline — 2018-07-16T03:17:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-245960>

love your thoughts Jenelle. I don't ever remember doing what I was expected to do.

Not having much of a family has definite benefits ....I guess I just flew by the seat of my pants - maybe another way of saying my intuition - altho I don't think I ever realised it until now.

I have a very strong need to be able to take care of myself ......and my partner sometimes has a bit of a problem with that because he wants to take care of me ...I keep resisting but it is also a very lovely thing - to be loved and cared for with one's whole heart and being.

### Jenelle — 2018-07-18T22:09:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-246143>

So, my husband says his back, hip ~ none of it hurts when he is barefoot.

This is an excellent development, and literally took about 10 seconds of my time to suggest it one day. He listened. He tried it. He stuck with it. He observed.

This week, he is working with a guy (about our age ~ maybe a little younger) who has had multiple surgeries for all sorts of spine issues. My husband shares his own experience with him, and tells him to get outside barefoot in the yard.

Guy says "what about running around barefoot and naked?" {laughing, thinking he's making a joke}

And my husband is like dead serious, straight-faced: "Yeah, actually, if you can do it naked, that would be even better."

So he tells him about how he just had to privacy fence part of his yard so his wife could be out every day in her "see-through suit".

### Jenelle — 2018-07-31T00:39:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-247036>

Over the past two days, I've spent some time reading the book Strong Medicine, published in 1962 by Blake Donaldson, MD. The entire book is available online:

[http://justmeat.co/docs/strong-medicine-blake-donaldson.pdf](http://justmeat.co/docs/strong-medicine-blake-donaldson.pdf)

It was super interesting to me. Some of the information is incredibly outdated (_of course_) ... and the way he talks about women is hilarious.

BUT he was also spot-on about so many things ... and it made me feel kind of sad to know that this information was already out there, published ~ when my dad was just a teenager. LONG before he'd lost both parents -- and two of his brothers -- to diabetes & heart problems. LONG before he and my mother had married and had children, and LONG before his daughter taught him so many of these things she's learned on her own... _which were already published in **1962**._

\---

\---

### Jenelle — 2018-07-31T17:49:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-247114>

I've worked really hard at not asking questions here ~ googling & doing my own research. But I am going to ask a question because today, because I need the help.

I've seen (somewhere) Jack say that he doesn't like acetaminophen & doesn't use it with his patients. And I read the explanation as to why.

What I wonder is if any of you have seen him discuss other painkillers and what would be considered "least bad" when one is needed. Even just a point in the right direction would be appreciated, if anyone has it.

A neighborhood dog \*took me out\* yesterday ~ didn't bite me, just ran full-force into me and flipped me like a pancake on our sunrise walk. I came down hard on both elbows, and my head bounced off the pavement. No, I don't have a concussion... I think having my hair in a bun saved me. Lol. And no, I did not go to the hospital to be checked out.

I did give in and take two acetaminophen because I had a headache from the stress of it all ~ and crying my eyes out like a baby, once the initial shock wore off.

Today I just feel like ... I've been flipped like a pancake. And I just wanted to throw this out there, in case anyone has this info. It would be good to know, for future reference.

### Jenelle — 2018-08-01T17:54:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-247224>

A couple of thoughts about the facebook link posted elsewhere:

1) It has been on my mind lately that there are 9 years between my older sister and myself ... due to multiple miscarriages in between. My thoughts have been along the lines of "I am the strong one that finally made it" ... Now I am wondering if it's more like "I was the best of a bad bunch".  She was 31 when I was born ~ which was pretty old at the time. We think \*nothing\* of that age now.

2) My husband and I chose to get married & have kids younger than most were doing at the time. My kids were born when I was 22, 25, 27. That has "seemed" young to me when I look around at others our age, with little ones ... & here we are, thinking about how we are possibly going to pay for college. Soon. But, for a variety of reasons ~ we've both \*always\* been glad we did not wait to have kids. Now I have another reason to add to that list.

[https://m.facebook.com/drjackkruse/...96131008&notif\_t=feed\_comment\_reply&ref=notif](https://m.facebook.com/drjackkruse/posts/2246826482048360?comment_id=2247704611960547&reply_comment_id=2249306871800321&notif_id=1533130096131008&notif_t=feed_comment_reply&ref=notif)

### drezy — 2018-08-01T18:10:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-247227>

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=247224)
>
> "I was the best of a bad bunch"

I think that's the safest assumption to start with. I start with that assumption regardless of my mom's young age (21) at my birth. Taking minor course corrections (hopefully) way early on potential diseases that show up in my mitochondrial line is the name of my game.

If you really follow the bouncing ball around here I think 99% of this is taken care of without heavy analysis. All minor issues I've had and related to future diseases found in my line have been taken care of better than ever since I've acted on what I learned from Doc.

### Jenelle — 2018-08-06T16:20:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-247686>

Now... I've seen some pretty cool stuff around our neighborhood and our own property, since spending so much time outside this summer.

But this has to be the coolest ~ most unique thing I've seen so far.

The kids discovered it the other day while hanging out on the swing on our front patio. Had they been in the house glued to screens ... none of us EVER would have noticed this. The bottom of our house is limestone, top is siding ~ and this guy was hanging out underneath the ledge that goes all around our house in the middle.

### Jenelle — 2018-08-12T14:19:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-248301>

Spent the day yesterday helping my sister clean her new house out in the country so they can move into it (the people left the kitchen quite a mess ~ yuck).

My brother-in-law is building new fences for their animals, and my 11- and 13-year-olds helped with that all day. MAN ~ that was good for them.

I didn't have my phone with me most of the time, but I took pictures in my mind. I do that a lot these days. I stop and enjoy the moment ~ take in the sun, the smells, the beauty of everything around me ~ and I capture that moment in my mind.

They have a nice few acres, with lots of trees ~ some of them fruit trees, and a little bit of roll and weedy wildness to the land back where their goats & chickens will be "free" to roam.

They have a new grandchild, and one on the way ... everyone will be living together in this big new house. I walked around & could picture the kids running around someday... the happy moments they will have there.

I grew up in the country, and I am thankful for it. My husband grew up in a tiny town in the middle of Kansas, which very much reminded me of Mayberry the first time I went there.

I had memories of riding my bike down dirt roads to meet up with other country kids, playing in the drainage ditch (lol) for the entire day. I'd set up "bases" in the yard and play baseball by myself. A lot of my childhood was by myself ... or was it? There was always nature. We always had cats & dogs.

My husband had memories of riding his bike all over town to friends' houses. And when the time came to choose this house, where we have now been for 15 years ~ he wanted our kids to grow up in a neighborhood where they could do the same.

But it's not the same. Nothing is the same.

My kids are certainly not growing up in Mayberry, and most of their friends probably wouldn't even understand the reference. We rarely see kids out on their bikes. It's not the norm. My youngest does go around on his bike "to see if anyone is out" and often comes back empty-handed. His best friends in younger years were another homeschooling family who had a bunch of kids and spent a lot of time outdoors ~ but they moved away years ago.

Real human interactions and connection with nature is so quickly becoming a thing of the past. This is not a new revelation. It's just had a spotlight shined on it in my mind for today.

### drezy — 2018-08-12T15:53:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-248306>

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=248301)
>
> Real human interactions and connection with nature is so quickly becoming a thing of the past. This is not a new revelation. It's just had a spotlight shined on it in my mind for today.

That's a very poignant realization when it and it's implications really starts to hit you like that.

I try to break through this sometimes. Try this experiment. Next time you go to the grocery store(where most people my age and younger apparently need headphones in to hear their soundtrack 24/7) find a few elderly people shopping and just ask them very basic questions like "Excuse me, I've never tried this product is it good?"

A simple interaction with humans that pre-date this faux busyness phase is always interesting and appreciated.

For the record my funniest interaction so far was with a very old gal. She was (rightfully) in one of those store scooter carts and staring way up at vidalia onions. I asked her if she wanted me to get one for her and she replied "No, but if you got it for me I'd keep it." (with a special lilt on the "you"). I still chuckle thinking about my meeting with the gal I still think about as empowered woman 1.0.

### Jenelle — 2018-08-12T16:36:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-248309>

I have had wonderful interactions with the older ladies who walk around the same time as us every morning. I've learned most of their names. Connie came by right after the dog took me out ~ and it was her genuine caring and tone of concern that brought me to tears. She gave me a hug, and I just started crying like a baby. Lol.

We know & have good relationships with most of the neighbors immediately around us. But branching out into daily sunrise walks has allowed us to get to know others we never would have met otherwise.

My oldest used to be annoyed when the ladies stopped & talked to us. They commented about him walking in his socks, and I think it made him feel uncomfortable to have that attention drawn to him. I have, however, watched him blossom and come out of his shell more and more ~ as we have had more of these interactions with them, and it has become our norm. He is no longer annoyed by it. I think secretly he may even enjoy it.

My husband and I talk and joke with strangers all the time. At the store... anywhere out in public, really. I'd say I'm by far one of the most outgoing in my class ~ I'm friendly to everyone. I can't tell you how many people have said "they couldn't tell" my kids had been homeschooled... because there is an assumption of being socially isolated & backwards. We've gotten genuine shock -- "I don't know what you've been doing, but keep doing it. They are great kids."

So my thoughts are more along the lines of: Their opportunities for those genuine relationships are fewer ~ because the world is different. It has to be a conscious effort now to get kids out in nature and talking to each other? Ridiculous.

My kids' grandparents are even very wrapped up in technology. We don't visit often ~ but my mom is eventually drawn to the computer or her phone every time, often in the middle of a conversation. My dad has been retired as long as I've had kids, and we rarely see him. He's addicted to Craigslist and who knows what else. Missed opportunities abound. I know my parents have great memories of growing up with their own grandparents... but everything is different now. People have spread out. It takes effort to see each other. And there is so much else to do -- we are so "busy"!

### drezy — 2018-08-12T17:49:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-248313>

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=248309)
>
> He is no longer annoyed by it. I think secretly he may even enjoy it.

That's funny. Buckle up mom.

I watch the deer on my property and have to laugh at the similarities. The little spotted fawn often can't clear some of the neighboring property fences when their mom jumps over them. My property becomes a fawn daycare center. Obviously some little fawn are male. They cower into bushes and hide during the day so quietly.

It's funny seeing that knowing that in 3 years, when the season comes, that same male will be stomping around so loudly (to impress) that I'll be able to hear them through 250' of soil, up my foundation, and into my bed frame in the mornings.

It's a lot like seeing the picture of a little shy boy and then seeing him out on the town with buddies at 20 years old.

I do however have a creeping suspicion that if we look over our shoulder while pointing and laughing at the young there will be older people behind us pointing and laughing...

### Jenelle — 2018-08-30T18:48:48+0300

What. Is it?

My son says he sees these around our house every year. Where have I been?

Thank you! Yes, I think you're right. They are on mulberry trees around here. That's a tiny mulberry tree growing on the edge of our burn pile. And there are several in the top of my neighbor's mulberry tree -- right where I look toward the sun every morning now. I guess I didn't see them until he pointed them out, because I am super blind without my glasses.  I'll share the link with him -- thanks again!

### Jenelle — 2018-09-01T15:39:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-249880>

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=241552)
>
> Thanks, [@JanSz](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/933/) !
>
> I ended up making the trip to see family after all -- was in the ER with my dad, who is a whole different story. 70 years old, very overweight, blue light toxic, having sudden shortness of breath and dizziness. They admitted him. Lots of tests yesterday & more cardiac tests today.
>
> He listens to me... Sort of. He insisted that I order his dinner last night & I was like, are you sure?! Lol. He got the salmon, steamed green beans, steamed carrots, water. A far cry from his norm.
>
> I came home just long enough to sleep & see the sun rise. Headed back to the hospital ASAP.

Happy to report that my dad is down 24 pounds since his last doctor visit (not sure when that was) & he has had no more of these episodes, no more trips to the ER... which were starting to become a regular thing.

I've shared with him a variety of JK stuff, I've really tried to stress not eating after dark, stopping the screens and going to bed. He told me he always loses weight when they go on fishing trips, and I've told him to try to replicate that environment as much as possible when at home. They're out in the sun all day & go to bed when it's dark! There's something TO that, Dad... we've had good conversations and he's listening & looking at things from new angles. Thinking about things he'd never thought about before.

He's also cut carbs dramatically, doing whatever his version of keto may be ~ I don't know the details of his days, and I don't hound him about it. It's been a HUGE change for him and it is clearly paying off. The weight loss was very slow in the beginning & I told him to CALL ME before he got frustrated and fell off the wagon. I have all kinds of tips & tricks that do not involve a box of donuts! ...But he feels so much better, the difference is undeniable ~ and he's stuck with it. Super happy for him.

### PJP71 — 2018-09-01T21:57:20+0300

That great! Wish I could have that affect on my parents. Bit by bit I am trying.

### Jenelle — 2018-09-03T17:24:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-250044>

Made a trip to the dollar store yesterday, specifically looking for a notebook of this size that can fit into my purse and go everywhere with me.

Just because I **can** use "notes" on my phone for everything ~ doesn't mean I **should**.

I also feel that learning and retention are enhanced through the actual process of _writing_ vs. typing.

Look what I found!  There were four of them there ~ just waiting for me on the shelf:

### PJP71 — 2018-09-03T18:24:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-250052>

Yep. Typing,writing, reading aloud, visualizing etc. all create additional pathways to memory.

### Jenelle — 2018-09-04T22:46:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-250161>

A nurse from the eye center (where we have to visit and watch a surgery one day for clinicals) came to class today to talk to us about what to expect.

In her talk, she told us that **everyone** gets cataracts, and that we all have the beginnings of them now. She also told everyone that you only have to get them removed one time in your life.

The girl behind me asks, "If you only have to get them removed one time in your life, why not just go ahead and get it done when you are in your twenties?"

And the lady's response was that some people do. She said some babies are born with cataracts. And now everyone in class thinks this is something they're going to have to do someday...? And that's just the way it is.

And I'm sitting there thinking:

_I can't even...

But...

Ugh._

I did.

No lemon juice, no hot sauce ~ just salt.

They go down much easier this way for me.

The day I realized I was cutting up giant LIVE oysters & shooting them down my throat without chewing was the day I stopped eating oysters.

Yesterday I tried again with the canned ones Jansz posts pictures of here (Crown Prince). I was proud of myself to get several of them down... sort of chewing, but trying to avoid that hard thing in there. Whatever it is. Gagged at some point ~ couldn't eat any more.

So I realized today that I just need to purée them. I ate about half of that bowl ~ so 1/2 can. More than yesterday.

My dog will not touch them.

### Jenelle — 2018-09-08T17:00:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-250526>

So... I'm getting the sense that Ray Peat goes against pretty much everything Dr. Kruse says about the importance of eating fish / seafood in abundance.

[http://raypeat.com/articles/nutrition/oils-in-context.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/nutrition/oils-in-context.shtml)

[http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/fats-degeneration3.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/fats-degeneration3.shtml)

From the second link:

"Eating low-fat seafood (sole, whitefish, turbot, scallops, oysters, lobster, shrimp, squid, etc.) once in a while can provide useful trace minerals, without much risk. However, fish from some parts of the ocean contain industrial contaminants in the fat, and large fish such as tuna, swordfish, Chilean sea bass and halibut contain toxic amounts of mercury in the muscles. Chilean sea bass (Patagonian toothfish) is very high in fat, too.

About ten years ago I met a young man with a degenerative brain disease, and was interested in the fact that he (working on a fishing boat) had been eating almost a pound of salmon per day for several years. There is now enough information regarding the neurotoxic effects of fish oil to justify avoidance of the fatty fish."

So my dog has found sympathy with the Peat-meisters. Great.

### caroline — 2018-09-10T04:37:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-250604>

mackerel head smoothy = MHS

I use small whole frozen fish from a Chinese grocery, thaw slightly and chop in pieces and put in a blender with cold water, fresh lemon juice and fresh herbs ......and then blend the bejesus out of it!

for the grilled oysters .....just use a couple of drops of melted butter and a small grating of parmesan.

### Jenelle — 2018-09-15T16:23:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-251035>

I don't pay much attention to it ~ but I'm still receiving newsletters from Dr. Perlmutter.

Today I see the title: "Sun Exposure and Your Health" ~ so I click on it.

Things are actually looking good, I think (!) when I read:

_"Our ancestors spent a lot of time exposed to UV light, and we are just beginning to unravel more of the story as to why this was, and is, healthful."
_
But things take a turn for the worse in the last sentence:

_"So I would consider avoiding sun exposure from 10 A.M. to 2 P.M., and then limit it to around 15 minutes in total each day."_

FAIL. Unsubscribe.

Unbelievable. What a crock of shit.

[https://www.drperlmutter.com/sun-ex...9b9d39fb741e0e329ada04b54923ac0b79c6679b859e1](https://www.drperlmutter.com/sun-exposure-health/?inf_contact_key=9e41e8e0b0c5684f0589b9d39fb741e0e329ada04b54923ac0b79c6679b859e1)

### Phosphene — 2018-09-26T18:28:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-251725>

Hey Jenelle, how's it going? You must be in the throes of your hands-on nursing stuff, and trying to refrain from punching every other instructor in the schnoz. I feel for you.

My slim maternal grandfather ate tons of saturated fat and lived to 101. Of course he also ate plenty of sweets, so quite the conundrum. I’m sure his last 10 years would have been far more comfortable and less humiliating if he were a mitochondriac.

### Jenelle — 2018-09-27T01:20:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-251773>

Hey, thanks for stopping in!

It's ... going. I'll probably just leave it at that for now. Lol.

I've said for several years now: protein & fat is where it's at. I'll gladly leave the sugar for others. It's done me no favors.

I'm excited to see the word getting out more and more. Then I wonder -- is it, really? Sitting in class yesterday we were told that a diabetic diet needs to be 45-65% carbohydrate and 15-20% protein.

Curse words.

### Mike David — 2018-09-27T23:00:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-251837>

Same dumb stuff they tell my kids in “health class”. I make them tell me what the teachers said then I give them a really good question to ask the teacher. Who knows if they actually ask but I at least try to teach them more based on reality than fantasy.

### Jenelle — 2018-12-18T18:08:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-258363>

[@caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) recently asked for an update ~ and it's been on my mind to do so, anyway.

For anyone out there who might want to know ... I've officially finished nursing school!

JK is always with me. He doesn't know it ~ but he is. There have been times lately when I've wanted to put contacts back in my eyeballs. I haven't done it. All of my pictures from the pinning ceremony have glare on my glasses. _"Do I have eyeballs under there? Are they open?"_ ...Not sure... but at least there aren't any contact lenses in there.

In all of the "still very much busy"-ness of now preparing for the big exam to get my license ~ and also preparing for Christmas... I wanted to make sure and take a few minutes to thank everyone here who has supported me on this journey. Every positive comment has meant more to me than you know.

I especially want to thank [@Sean Waters](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19464/) for telling me I'm going to be "a fucking epic nurse". I told him that remains to be seen... but I sure appreciate it. You never know when your words are going to be just exactly what someone needed to hear at that moment. We all have a choice, to reach out to others and share the good things we're thinking, or to keep it to ourselves. I often choose to compliment total strangers out in public ~ and it makes me feel good to see that it made them feel good. I love that through this forum, we can connect and support each other across miles... across _oceans_. Thank you, Sean. I wish you all the best in the exciting days ahead.

And a special shout out to [@drezy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19879/) ... who is Super Dad and Super Husband ~ yet somehow, still manages to find time to make us laugh, and encourage us to do just a little better than we are currently doing. At one point during this last semester, he was ready to take out a life insurance policy on me ~ because surely, I was about to die. I'm happy to be here today to say: _"I'm still kickin'"_ ... and you are not rid of me just yet, dude. Sorry.

Huge congrats from me too!!! and an extra huge Koala bear hug.

Enjoy a wonderful Christmas with your family and be so proud of yourself that you are about to burst! What a fabulous gift you have given yourself and your family......enjoy every last second!

Have you listened to Jack's Dec 2018 Q&A ...... he mentioned briefly that he would probably be using a nurse to facilitate some things ......just saying.

Thanks for being here ....you are such a bonus!

### Jenelle — 2018-12-20T16:21:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-258537>

No, I haven't listened to that one yet ~ but I hear good things! Maybe that will be a Christmas present to myself.

I will respond more to your post later ~ I just had to tell you that I shared your comment with my husband this morning, and he was immediately looking at railroad jobs in the New Orleans area, and telling me all about them. LOL. It has been discussed... many times.

### Jenelle — 2018-12-31T15:58:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-259262>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=258413)
>
> Maybe another time .....when you have time - you can tell us what you did to offset all the hospital nnEMF crap.
>
> How do you stay well in that environment?
>
> Thanks for being here ....you are such a bonus!

[@caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) ~ I'm sorry it took me so long to really respond to this. Thanks so much for your kind words. I appreciate you.

My clinical days at the hospital in nursing school were pretty spread out. This last semester, for example -- I was only there for **8 days** out of the entire four months.

The other days, we had some simulations and lab days at school ... we had a day where we did health teaching/presentations for an awesome local program, where they are helping people with various challenges learn new skills so they can get out in the workforce ... we had a day at the EMS station, where we got to learn all about who they are and what they do.

The truth is, there is nnEMF crap pretty much everywhere we go. In all of these experiences. And in my neighborhood. In the entire town.

I believe I saw them putting up (what must be?) a new 5G tower the other day, as I was driving past a different neighborhood, not far from mine. I didn't stop to take a pic to post here -- although I thought about it -- because I was too busy processing what I just saw, and streaming a few curse words about it to one of my teenagers.

So ~ back to your original question. What did I do to stay well? Well, I wore long sleeves under my scrub top, always. I drank a lot of water. And I ran like hell when it was time to go home.

### caroline — 2019-01-01T01:59:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-259304>

very nice update Jenelle .....A wonderful and Happy New Year to you and your family and tons of new quantum entanglements moving forward!

### Jenelle — 2019-02-03T19:38:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-262204>

Thank you, [@caroline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/888/) ~ I hope you are doing well in the new year!

The weather is beginning to warm up here ... for a couple of days, anyway, we'll have temps in the 50's. And SUNSHINE today!

Last week my kids were out of school for 3 days, due to **highs** below zero. _Yuck_... yuck does not even begin to describe it. I'll leave it at that, for now.

After a ridiculous amount of time spent on the computer preparing for my NCLEX exam, I passed it on January 14 and had my license number on the state website the next day. So it's official: RN. This was a huge relief of pressure, on one hand ~ and the beginning of a whole new pressure, on the other.

It's been an interesting few weeks, perusing job listings... deciding which direction I really want to go for now.

I applied and interviewed at the biggest, fanciest long-term care facility in town. My interview was 3.5 hours ~ we really clicked ~ it was great! The nurse manager told everyone we met on my tour that I'd be "a great fit".

However.

They are only ever hiring for night shift -- 6:00 PM - 6:30 AM. I offered to fill in daytime gaps: vacations, illnesses -- last-minute holes or busy times in the schedule, even. Nope... they say they have no daytime need. Their nurses tend to stay, and there is a list of people waiting for days. So. I moved on.

Oh, the temptation was there. I won't lie. But I know the reality of that lifestyle too well, after seeing what it has done to my husband ~ not to mention everything I've learned here. _"It's only 3 days a week",_ the lying little bastard in my head wanted to tell me. Ha! You don't recover from that on the other days of the week. It's a mess.

So now I have a job lined up at the nursing home where we did clinicals. They had no problem offering me a PRN (as needed) daytime position, and a great rate of pay. It was pretty cool how it all worked out... _almost_ too good to be true... because I've thought all along that I might like to work there. It was my favorite clinical experience, by far. I have a heart for this population, and they need good nurses. It is also a relatively small facility, and NOT a hospital. It is a completely different work environment, really getting to know the residents and being in their home. It's not so damn fluorescent and bright everywhere you go. And sunshine is just a few steps away.

Is it perfect? No. Nothing I do is going to be perfect. But it is about the best I could possibly hope for, at this stage of the game.

As long as I don't have tuberculosis and I pass my physical, I'll be starting orientation soon.  Hope you all are doing well!

### Jenelle — 2019-02-26T13:03:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-263981>

Thanks for your comments and good wishes! I'm sorry I haven't responded sooner.

The new job is going well. It's just a big adjustment, taking up a lot of my time for now. Since I'm still training, I think I'm probably working more than they will actually need me when I'm on my own. We'll see.

I got my first paycheck... I quit working to be home with the kids in November 2000 -- so I can't really explain how freaking cool that was. Lol. All of this hard work I've done over the past two years, finally paying off. And not just in monetary form. I love my job. It's rewarding far beyond the paycheck. I could write stories all day.

And [@Phosphene](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19648/) you are so not alone in those visions. You can bet I have pictured it, as well.

### Jenelle — 2019-02-27T14:55:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-264043>

In other good news ~ our 4 privacy fence panels we quite unprofessionally installed over the summer held up to 65 MPH winds that came through here a couple of days ago.

Our neighbors on the other side (who have been here for our entire 15+ years) are moving ... soon. I'll be purchasing panels for the other side of my sunning area now, and those suckers are going up as soon as the ground is soft & weather good enough to do it. Hopefully before new neighbors move in.

This will allow me and my Kiniki a lot more roaming room. I haven't decided if I will put panels across the back and do the whole area yet. There's a duplex quite close behind us, but we have a row of huge overgrown bushes that provide "mostly privacy". That may not be good enough for me anymore.

It was the big 4-0. And it was a good day.

My husband and I both had the day off, which is rare. We went to the grocery store together. Lol. Kids had activities in the evening after school, as usual. No gifts and no big plans. But I'm in a good place in life, and I'm so thankful to be here ~ so that's plenty.

### Jenelle — 2019-03-29T06:35:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-266711>

Thanks again for all of the birthday wishes! I'm sorry it's taken me so long to say that ~ and sorry that, yes, I am posting late at night. Just this one time.

I wanted to say 3 words: Dave and Buster's.

That's it.

Good Lord. We're on our way back home from seeing my husband's family for the first time in 3 years. It's been raining ALL day, so we decided to check this place out in Kansas City. Now I'm in the truck with the dog, waiting for the rest of my family to come out of the building.

Please... just come out of the building. Lol.

What strikes me most is that there are so many families in there with small children. It's loud, it's bright, and it's non-stop video games. For us ~ I'd say this is a one-time thing. But how many come here regularly? Geez.

### Mike David — 2019-03-29T15:33:39+0200

### drezy — 2019-03-29T16:12:04+0200

> [Mike David said:](/goto/post?id=266740)

Temporarily. The next morning it comes back 5x stronger. It also becomes impossible to sort out whether it's the sugar shock or the alcohol.

It's a brilliantly devious business plan to set kids loose, get mom and dad drunk, inevitably mom and dad will loose logic by the time kids come back and ask to double down on the money spent. All the while blue lights flash and blink away.

I stand in awe. It's like someone thought "Let's drop this MkUltra and set this up over here!"

### Jenelle — 2019-03-29T17:41:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-266756>

It was St. Louis ... not KC ... not that it matters ... it's all over now, and I can't say I'll be going back.

He says, "Are you Elizabeth?"

Ummm... no. No I am not.

### Phosphene — 2019-04-02T23:28:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-267153>

I’ve somehow managed to avoid this unholy entity called Dave and Busters, but it sounds just like Chuck E. Cheese. Remember that hellishness? Has it been banished to darkness or just reincarnated into D and B?

I recall a few horrid birthday parties I attended, huddled in a corner with (if memory serves) a watery beer and cardboard pizza while the kids bounced off the walls. And the bells and buzzers and lights!!!

Somehow I survived, but never again. I’ve even sworn off Disney World and potential future grandkids’ pleas shall go unheeded and countered with nature.

### Jenelle — 2019-04-03T03:47:05+0300

### Jenelle — 2019-05-26T04:46:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-271307>

Summer is finally getting into full swing ~ nature is awake and alive all around us.

I've got a nest of baby birds on my back patio (on top of the meter again... poor birds!)

Uncovered a toad from his hiding spot while I was ripping old carpet out of the sunroom today.

Found an inchworm on my coffee cup. My husband made the kids laugh when he said the worm was trying to drink the coffee: _"Screw this! I want to be a footworm, not an inchworm!"_  (Maybe you had to be there.... or maybe you can picture my whole family actually outside, barefoot, together, in the grass, in the sunshine, laughing... and it's enough to make you smile, too.)

I am blessed to live somewhere where we have large, old, mature trees throughout our neighborhood... I'm blessed with good neighbors. Squirrels, rabbits, raccoons, and sometimes deer.

But it's times like these -- when I am truly awake, feeling my best, and taking it all in... when it really, really hits me how long and depressing the winter months are for all of us. I told my husband today while we were out on our walk, living here is literally taking years off our lives. And he agrees.

We are finally in a position where I feel like we have a choice. For now, for this moment, we choose to stay here and continue to work toward a new plan, a new location.

We are crammed in this house like a can of sardines -- but I will NOT buy another house here. I just keep playing the song over and over in my head, _"Love grows best in little houses... with few walls to separate... where you eat and sleep so close together, you can't help but communicate... oh, and if we had more room between us, think of all we'd miss... love grows best in houses just like this."
_
Thanks, Doug Stone!

### caroline — 2019-05-26T05:43:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-271312>

lovely thoughts ....smart too!

### drezy — 2019-05-26T17:06:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-271331>

> [Jenelle said:](/goto/post?id=271307)
>
> We are crammed in this house like a can of sardines -- but I will NOT buy another house here. I just keep playing the song over and over in my head, _"Love grows best in little houses... with few walls to separate... where you eat and sleep so close together, you can't help but communicate... oh, and if we had more room between us, think of all we'd miss... love grows best in houses just like this."
> _
> Thanks, Doug Stone!
>

It's great to hear from you.

I had to dig my cowboy boots out of the back of the closet to listen properly to that song.

I agree. I've had old coworker buy McMansions more than double the size of my home. I think little homes are great because

1.  You are nearer to each other. Kids are going to grow up and leave so fast our heads will spin.
2.  If the house is too big and comfy you'll spend too much time in it and having to clean it... Get outside! All money spent on my property now is spent improving my outdoor setup.

### Jenelle — 2019-05-26T17:26:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-271332>

> [drezy said:](/goto/post?id=271331)
>
> It's great to hear from you.
>
> I had to dig my cowboy boots out of the back of the closet to listen properly to that song.
>
> I agree. I've had old coworker buy McMansions more than double the size of my home. I think little homes are great because
>
> 1.  You are nearer to each other. Kids are going to grow up and leave so fast our heads will spin.
> 2.  If the house is too big and comfy you'll spend too much time in it and having to clean it... Get outside! All money spent on my property now is spent improving my outdoor setup.

Yes.

My big project yesterday was cleaning out the sunroom/dog room, ripping out the carpet so it's just a concrete floor now. We leave the outside door propped open all the time so he can go in and out (we just have to let him out the sliding door to the house). So the carpet was wet and nasty. Plus, one corner is leaking.

I started out the day telling my husband we were NOT going to put a single dime in this sunroom -- because eventually, I was picturing tearing it down / letting it fall down (lol) and then just having more patio there.

BUT after getting all of our stupid miscellaneous shit out of it (which is now stacked up in the garage, and I have to sort through) ... making it into a useable space, with just a table and chairs in there -- it came in handy yesterday and this morning during the **rain**, for sure. I can still have all of the windows and the door open, and be outside without being outside.

Which is great, because, in other news -- I started my BSN degree online. It's a self-paced program that you can sort of fly through if you already have prior knowledge of a subject, or slow down and take more time on the ones you don't. I love it so far. Now that I have my picnic table on my patio, and my sunroom all set up for studying -- no reason I have to be stuck inside my little house this summer. Even though, you know, love grows best there. Lol.

### Sean Waters — 2019-05-27T11:30:25+0300

### Jenelle — 2019-05-27T14:25:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-271370>

LOL [@Sean Waters](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19464/) ... thanks for stopping in. I'm sorry if now it is stuck in your head, too!

Thankfully, I love the song. Because I'd say I think of it at least daily. It holds a lot of meaning for me.

We've had our fair share of life throwing surprises at us -- but I'm feeling very grateful for where we are and what we have right now. And that is a beautiful place to be.

Our oldest turns 18 in a couple of weeks. In August, we'll celebrate being married for 20 years.

We are at that stage of the game where, as [@drezy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19879/) said, we can see the kids moving on and making their own lives. And it's okay. We've stayed strong and best friends through it all. It's exciting, in a way ~ picturing that next chapter together. When you're in the middle of raising 3 littles... you can't picture that AT ALL. Lol.

JK and being a part of this forum has helped me stay focused and appreciate the things that really matter in life. Thank you all for being here.

He also knows (because I've made it quite clear due to situations with other members in our family) that I will not be raising my grandkids for them. You decide to grow up, you decide to grow up all the way!

### Jenelle — 2019-07-14T03:46:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-274268>

So I don't gunk up other people's journals with my own stuff... an update.

Worked 2 jobs for about 6 weeks ~ you know, just to see if I could kill myself, I guess. Lol.

Actually ... I realized that the first place I was at really just was NOT a good fit for me. My 12-hour days were consistently turning into 15- or 16-hour days. So I started at another facility, working PRN at both of them. It didn't take long before I decided to let the first one go.

I do miss the people. I liked and respected so many of my coworkers. I loved taking care of the residents. They become like family. But ultimately, it was dragging me down to a place I did not want to be.

My new job is an hour drive each way, but it is worth it. Shifts are 8 hours and _most days_, you actually get to leave on time. The pace is not 100 MPH all day every day ~ and when I leave there, I can actually think about other things besides work.

I didn't realize how **very** stressed I was at the first job until I really got away from it.

\---

So I've been hammering away at my BSN online. Loving this self-paced program. But I had to come and share with you guys -- my first roadblock:

BIOCHEMISTRY.

I just did my pre-assessment, and it's the first one I haven't passed with flying colors. That's okay -- it doesn't count for a score or anything. But it gives you an idea of where to focus your studies.

DNA and RNA ... that's where I go first. My lowest score.

"DNA Inheritance and Genetic Testing"

"Nucleotides and Central Dogma" <-- (I don't even know what that means)

"DNA Mutation and Repair"

I'll be using resources like Khan Academy and whatever else I can find that will help all of this be interesting and "click" with me. [@drezy](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19879/) if you and [@Sean Waters](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19464/) want to dress up and make a fun music video or something ... I'm game.

### Sean Waters — 2019-07-14T14:19:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-274279>

Hahahahha... I've just quit my Nutritional therapy/ Biochemistry diploma, so I probably ain't the best guy to go to....

Unless, they got any modules on Quantum Bi-.......... nevermind

### Jenelle — 2019-08-20T16:09:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-276279>

The central dogma of molecular biology -- in its simplified version, which is all I really need to know for this class: DNA --> RNA --> protein.

Wow. That wasn't so difficult.

So I've moved beyond the intimidated stage, and now I'm fully immersed in this class. I had to wait until my kids went back to school so I could actually have some time to study for this one without being interrupted. I completed a couple of other classes in the meantime.

Yesterday I was sitting outside for hours, while I'm learning about the harmful effects of UV radiation ... how it causes thymine dimers to form, and how our bodies use nucleotide excision repair to fix it.

I started the day yesterday just generally pissed off about signing up for this degree ... and I realized it's because Biochemistry doesn't come "naturally" to me, and I'm going to have to put some serious, actual work into it -- which would _not_ bother me at all if I didn't have so many other things around here staring at me that need to be done. I love learning new things. I don't love doing it under pressure or a deadline.

But as the day went on, and things began to click, I actually got excited about knowing the things I am learning. Perhaps it will help me be able to understand JK lingo a little better. It's definitely taking me to a deeper level of thinking and understanding.

All I have to do is GO OUTSIDE and what do I see here? Peace. Nature. Focus.

### Sean Waters — 2019-08-20T16:14:54+0300

### Jenelle — 2019-08-20T16:36:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-276285>

> [Sean Waters said:](/goto/post?id=276281)
>
> The recommended reading list of books might help you learn quicker..... Nick Lane is pretty epic!

I was trying to tell my sister yesterday about Andrew Marino ... we were on a discussion about cell phones & 5G.

I realized later, as I was thinking about it in the shower ... I told her Dan Marino. Yeah, you know, the NFL quarterback?  I lmao to myself about it, then promptly sent her a correction text, with a picture of Going Somewhere from Amazon. I put it in my cart. I do want to read that one, for sure.

_Somebody_ has to stop signing up for degrees ...... or does she? It's helping. Maybe this is my roundabout way.

### Sean Waters — 2019-08-20T17:18:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-276290>

Marino's book is epic. Teaches you a lot about how to Argue with naysayers on EMF. He lays out so many examples of where he completely fucks people up with his questioning of their views, using precise questions. It teaches you how to think better...

But in terms of learning Quantum Biology, or even Biology, I'd say it isn't good at all. He states in the intro it isn't about the biology. It is an Auto-Biographical account of his journey, and all the people he meets and the events.

I would recommend Becker's book. But, he discusses Magnetism and BioPhysics, and uses little sketches. It can be a bit, I don't know, vague at times. Although his glossary is epic, I don't think it would really assist you on your Biochemistry modules. It would be more suited to seeing how Bone & the CNS generate a current to heal/ regenerate.

That is why I suggested Lane, over anything!.........

Nick Lane is modern, and uses a lot of biology, chemistry, evolutionary biology. You would learn a huge amount about DNA, RNA - the differences between Bacteria/ Viruses/ Eukaryotes - human/ animal/ plant cells. It would give you the history of the fucking world and all the cells and how it is thought they came to be.

It is dense, and you will spend hours on two or three pages, trying to download what he is saying.

But, once you get through it - you are literally like a Royal Marine amongst soldiers in your degree class.

It is tough. But, nothing comes easy. If you really want that degree go for it. If that changes, then I suggest that you don't be afraid to set the whole thing on fire, including all the money you "wasted" on it (which I would disagree is a waste) and move on to something even better.

Money is a tool at the end of the day. People become too emotionally attached..... I use it to buy me Freedom and Shortcuts round shit I don't want to waste my time on. If that means paying £4000 on my final year, of a course that I've quit, and will never get certificate for... so be it..........

I did exactly that yesterday. I literally forgot about it until now........ most people shudder at £4k going to waste. But I don't see it that way at all! I know more money is on it's way. But my Time, I can never get back. So doing that course and not going to Mexico/ pursuing Writing/ other ambitions is **far worse** than paying £4000.

But that is just my perspective, I don't have kids/ dependants either so it is bigger for you to get this right I guess......

### Jenelle — 2019-08-21T17:20:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-276375>

[@Sean Waters](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/19464/) ~ I appreciate your comments.

I added The Vital Question to my cart, so I don't forget about it.

I do have Becker's book. I made it partway through last summer. I'll pick it up again ... someday.

You are absolutely right about money being a tool. It takes a lot of self-discipline to not let what you want \*right now\* get in the way of what you REALLY want long-term.

I DO want to have this degree.

When I chose to become a nurse, I bought my family a backup plan for financial security that we did not have before.

The BSN will open up more doors in the future, if needed. I'd much rather be standing there with the key in my hand ~ ready to go ~ than to be wishing I had it, and kicking my own ass for not doing it.

I think a good question to ask ourselves is, would I be doing this if I weren't getting paid for it? I thought about this yesterday. Yes, I would (and am) learning Biochemistry because I want to -- now. It just took a little bit for me to get my head in the right place to do it. Now that I've gotten into the material, I do like it. And I think when I pass that big exam someday in the near future, it will feel like such a huge accomplishment BECAUSE it took a lot of work. You're right, most things worth having don't come easy.

### Jenelle — 2019-09-04T04:14:22+0300

Biochemistry done!

### Jenelle — 2019-09-04T14:16:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-277115>

I've reduced my days at work kind of drastically ... especially from back when I was trying to work two places. I've been out in the sun more ~ yes, often doing school work on the computer ~ but HOME, at my picnic table, in my yard.

I'm learning my limits at work ... how much is too much, when to walk away and take a break, when to say no. Because there will always be shifts to pick up. There will always be more that needs to be done. I'm a perfectionist, and very dedicated to my residents. So the first place I worked was REALLY a bad fit. I never, ever, ever felt like my work was done ... I left there exhausted, often after being there for 14-16 hours, doing my charting under glaring lights at the end of my shift. RIDICULOUS.

It's a shitty phase I think I needed to go through, but now I am very, very thankful to have it behind me.

My husband has been on jobs that put him home more lately, too -- and the kids' baseball is done, and they are back in school, so we actually get some time ALONE together ... what?!  Most days, realizing how much I am still in love with this man... who has been there and supported me through every single goal I have ever set my mind to.

Our 20-year anniversary was last week. Took our oldest to tour a college for the first time. It was a great day. Lots of exciting things going on. Maybe just everyday things that everyone goes through, but I feel so incredibly blessed right now. Taking life one day at a time. Soaking it all up.

### Sean Waters — 2019-09-07T14:54:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emerging-from-darkness.21283/post-277355>

Sounds like you are really starting to value yourself, and it sounds like you are starting to truly embrace the chaos........ and taking even more pleasure in the order that follows it... I think you should be really blessed to see your children reach college. That is mega for you as parents. I can only imagine.

As much as it sucks to be around those who are toxic ... and it is repellent, and does not make you feel good ... these exposures, in limited quantity, can also help keep you on your chosen path ... and even assist to perfect your trajectory.
